Extended Abstract (English Edition)

     Exploring the most fundamental building block of our universe is humanity’s eternal pur-
suit. While philosophical and scientific exploration spanned centuries, the discovery of the
electron in the late 19th century marked the beginning of the subatomic era, paving the way
for modern particle physics. The 20th century witnessed extraordinary progress in this field.
Key developments included the emergence of quantum mechanics, the formulation of quan-
tum electrodynamics, and the refinement of quantum field theory. These theoretical advances
went hand in hand with experimental breakthroughs, many driven by advances in particle ac-
celerator technology. Together, they led to the creation of the standard model (SM) of particle
physics—a comprehensive framework that describes the microscopic world. The SM classifies
all known fundamental particles into fermions (quarks and leptons) and bosons (gluons, pho-
tons, 𝑊 , 𝑍, and Higgs bosons) and unifies the strong, weak, and electromagnetic interactions.
Rooted in fundamental gauge symmetries and the Higgs mechanism, the SM has become a
cornerstone of modern physics. Over the last fifty years, its predictions have been consistently
confirmed in collider experiments, with the discovery of the Higgs boson at the Large Hadron
Collider (LHC) in 2012 serving as a landmark achievement in particle physics.
     Despite its remarkable success, the SM is not the ultimate framework for understanding
nature. It has significant limitations, including its inability to explain dark matter, unify grav-
ity with the other fundamental forces, or account for neutrino masses, among other issues. To
address these gaps, theorists have proposed numerous extensions to the SM that predict new
particles and phenomena. Searching for evidence of physics beyond the SM at the LHC has
therefore become a top priority. Additionally, precise measurements of SM parameters, espe-
cially those involving the Higgs boson, serve as rigorous tests of the theory. Any deviations
from theoretical predictions in experimental results could provide critical clues to new physics.
These goals define the current experimental programmes at the LHC.
     The LHC is home to two general-purpose experiments, ATLAS and CMS, which have
been recording proton-proton collision data at TeV energy scales for over a decade. Thou-
sands of physics analyses have been conducted to improve the precision of SM measurements
and search for potential signs of new physics. A key challenge for experimental physicists is to
continually enhance data analysis methods to achieve greater precision and sensitivity. Since
                                               VII
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


2012, the rise of modern deep learning has revolutionized statistical and analytical techniques,
enabling unprecedented capabilities in handling complex data. Deep neural networks (DNNs)
have proven particularly effective in analyzing intricate datasets, often surpassing traditional
methods as data complexity increases. In recent years, cutting-edge deep learning applications
in the CMS experiment have significantly improved measurement precision and boosted sen-
sitivity to potential new physics signals. As these methods continue to advance, their potential
to reshape LHC analyses remains vast. Exploring the full capabilities of deep learning in parti-
cle physics holds great promise for transforming experimental approaches and unlocking new
discoveries at the LHC.

     This dissertation explores state-of-the-art deep learning techniques for large-radius (large-
𝑅) jet tagging. In the CMS experiment, quarks or gluons produced in high-energy collisions
undergo hadronization, forming collimated sprays of particles known as jets. Jets are the most
ubiquitous objects in collision events but pose significant challenges for analysis due to their
complex structure. While small-radius jets typically consist of a few dozen reconstructed
particles, large-𝑅 jets—clustered with a larger radius 𝑅—can contain over 100 particles and
exhibit intricate internal substructures. These jets are particularly crucial for reconstructing
highly Lorentz-boosted resonances. Specifically, high-energy resonance particles with hun-
dreds of GeV to TeV-scale energies produced at the LHC often decay into their full set of
final-state particles, which can be entirely contained within a single large-𝑅 jet. Such jets offer
unique advantages for specific analyses; however, distinguishing signal-like jets from substan-
tial background processes requires sophisticated tagging algorithms. Designing observables
to characterize jet properties has long been a central challenge in LHC physics, making this
domain highly suited to the application of modern deep learning techniques. Over the past five
years, the direct application of advanced DNNs to low-level particle features within large-𝑅
jets has delivered significant performance gains, highlighting the transformative potential of
deep learning in this area.

     This dissertation provides a comprehensive study of advanced deep learning methods for
large-𝑅 jet tagging, integrating recent developments in DNN algorithms, a novel calibration
technique, an experimental breakthrough achieved within the CMS collaboration, and a novel
paradigm for future applications. The content is organized into four parts, each addressing a
key aspect of the subjet. The first three parts focus on challenges encountered when deploy-
ing DNN-based taggers in practical physics analyses: (1) algorithm design, which is funda-
mental to developing high-performance taggers; (2) calibration, a critical step for correcting
                                               VIII
                                Extended Abstract (English Edition)


simulation-to-data discrepancies when applying taggers to real LHC data; and (3) experimen-
tal design, which ensures reliable physics results. The fourth part explores future directions,
proposing a paradigm shift in jet tagging through the pre-training of a universal jet model. This
approach utilizes a “large models for large-scale classification” methodology, with subsequent
fine-tuning tailored to specific analysis needs.

    Part I of this dissertation introduces the foundational knowledge of experimental particle
physics and deep learning. On the particle physics side, it provides an overview of the SM,
the LHC, and the CMS detector, along with Monte Carlo event simulation and reconstruction
processes in the CMS experiment. On the deep learning side, it traces the rise of modern deep
learning since 2012, focusing on the vital technological advancements that have driven its rapid
evolution.

    Part II highlights recent advancements in large-𝑅 jet tagging algorithms over the past
three years. It begins with a historical overview of early methodologies, including rule-based
algorithms (e.g. 𝑁-subjettiness for jet substructure analysis), approaches utilizing “shallow”
machine learning techniques such as boosted decision trees (BDTs), and the initial adoption of
deep learning. Early deep learning efforts explore convolutional neural networks and recurrent
neural networks to represent jets in formats such as images or sequences, achieving notable per-
formance improvements. However, these methods were eventually surpassed by newer tech-
niques, leading to the emergence of a critical insight: the design of superior DNNs for jet data
depends critically on finding better low-level jet feature representations and applying advanced
architectures suited to the task. The introduction of the Particle Flow Network and ParticleNet
introduced point-cloud and graph-based representations for jets, marking a leap in algorithm
design. This part focuses on two critical advancements that have further driven progress in this
field: (1) the incorporation of Lorentz symmetry into jet tagging algorithms and (2) the adop-
tion of Transformer-based architectures. For the first advancement, it is widely recognized in
deep learning that respecting the intrinsic symmetries of data can enhance both performance
and robustness. In the context of jet tagging, earlier efforts focused on specific invariances,
such as rotational or boost invariance, but often overlooked the broader and more fundamental
invariances under the full set of Lorentz transformations. This part highlights LorentzNet, a
network explicitly designed to respect Lorentz symmetry, and demonstrates its superior perfor-
mance. A systematic study that follows demonstrates how incorporating Lorentz symmetry as
an inductive bias can substantially improve network accuracy and robustness. This approach
offers general guidelines and insights for integrating symmetry principles into future algorithm
                                                IX
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


designs. For the second advancement, this dissertation explores the growing prominence of
Transformer architectures in modern deep learning and their application to jet tagging. The
Particle Transformer (ParT), a representative network built on Transformer principles, is in-
troduced and analyzed. The discussion emphasizes ParT’s unique design features, particularly
its ability to preserve underlying symmetries through the attention mechanism, and highlights
its dual advantages of enhanced expressiveness and computational efficiency. Transformers
are particularly well-suited for scaling to larger models, a capability that is explored further in
Part V of this dissertation. Based on these developments, the part concludes with a discussion
of future directions in DNN algorithm design.


     Part III introduces new advancements in calibrating advanced DNN-based large-𝑅 jet tag-
gers in the CMS experiment. Measuring the difference in selection efficiency between data and
simulation, in terms of the scale factor (SF), for the employed jet tagging algorithms is a crit-
ical step in physics analyses. This part focuses on calibrating a specific subset of jet tagging
algorithms, namely, the boosted-jet flavour tagging algorithms, which aim to identify whether
a large-𝑅 jet originates from a resonance 𝑋 decaying into a bottom or charm quark-antiquark
pair (𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏/𝑐𝑐). Such tagging algorithms are widely applied in Higgs physics studies and
searches for new physics. Calibrating these taggers is challenging, primarily due to the diffi-
culty in finding suitable proxies for 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏/𝑐𝑐 in data. A previous approach employed jets
originating from gluon splitting, specifically 𝑔 → 𝑏𝑏, which are abundant in quantum chromo-
dynamics (QCD) multijet events, as proxies for 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 after applying appropriate selection
criteria. However, this method faces limitations when applied to high-performance taggers
(e.g. those based on the ParticleNet algorithm). To address this challenge, this part introduces
a novel calibration method, named the sfBDT method. While retaining the use of 𝑔 → 𝑏𝑏/𝑐𝑐
jets as proxies for 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏/𝑐𝑐 signal jets, this method leverages a multivariate analysis approach
based on a boosted decision tree (BDT) to identify a unique phase space closely resembling
𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏/𝑐𝑐 jets. The BDT is trained to distinguish between two categories of jets from QCD
multijet events, defined by a generator-level variable: one category exhibits signal-like charac-
teristics, while the other does not. The BDT training exploits features derived from associated
secondary vertex and track information. The resulting BDT discriminant is then used to select
an optimized 𝑔 → 𝑏𝑏/𝑐𝑐 phase space to act as a proxy. This work introduces two generations of
generator-level variable designs for the sfBDT method, along with two approaches for event
selection using the BDT discriminant. In the calibration procedure, SFs are extracted via a tem-
plate maximum likelihood fit, augmented with additional sources of uncertainties, including
                                                X
                                Extended Abstract (English Edition)


the one arising from variations in the BDT selection thresholds. These additional uncertainty
components contribute to the robustness and reliability of the extracted SFs. The SFs resulting
from the sfBDT method reach good accuracy and robustness and find applications in numerous
CMS analyses. The part concludes by presenting the comprehensive calibration results using
the sfBDT method for seven 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏/𝑐𝑐 tagging discriminants utilized in CMS analyses, across
different working points, jet transverse momentum ranges, and data-taking conditions.


    Part IV of this dissertation presents recent progress in measuring the Yukawa coupling
strengths of the Higgs and charm quarks within the CMS experiment. This work employed the
high-performance 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 large-𝑅 jet tagger based on the ParticleNet algorithm and the sfBDT
calibration method described in Part III. It begins by outlining the physics motivation behind
this measurement of the Higgs-charm coupling. As the Higgs mechanism is at the core of the
SM, measuring the Higgs boson coupling strengths with SM fermions and verifying their pro-
portionality to the fermions’ masses represents a crucial test of the SM’s accuracy. To date,
the couplings of the Higgs boson with third-generation fermions have been experimentally
confirmed; thus, measuring its coupling with second-generation fermions (including muons,
charm and strange quarks) will be the next milestone. This work presents the analysis using the
full CMS data collected during Run 2 to measure the Higgs boson coupling strength to charm
quarks through the 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 decay accompanied by the leptonically decayed 𝑊 or 𝑍 boson (re-
ferred to as the VHcc analysis). The VHcc analysis achieves a substantially higher-precision
measurement compared with previously established measurements. The work presented in this
dissertation focuses on scenarios where the produced Higgs boson has a high Lorentz boost.
In these cases, the analysis employs a large-𝑅 jet to reconstruct all final states produced by the
hadronization of the high-momentum Higgs boson decaying into 𝑐𝑐, with a larger radius 𝑅 of
1.5. The analysis exploits a DNN with the ParticleNet architecture for 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 large-𝑅 jet tag-
ging and mass reconstruction via regression, significantly enhancing the physics sensitivity: in
terms of 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 identification, the experiment achieved about a fivefold suppression of back-
ground events compared to the previous analysis; for mass reconstruction, it obtained a peak
with higher resolution, enhancing the signal strength upper limit extraction by 20–25%. This
analysis establishes the event selection, background estimation, and signal extraction based on
three key variables. First, a BDT is trained with kinematic variables (excluding jet flavour and
mass information) to provide a coarse separation between the 𝑉𝐻 (𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐) signal and the
main background processes. Second, the ParticleNet-based tagger is exploited to efficiently
select jets closely resembling 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 characteristics. Finally, signal extraction is achieved
                                                XI
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


by fitting the reconstructed mass spectrum regressed via the ParticleNet algorithm. For back-
ground estimation, dedicated specific control regions are designed to highlight contributions
from different backgrounds. A simultaneous fit of all signal and control regions is performed
to control the background for each process. The control regions are constructed by reversing
BDT selections and other kinematic selections. Before measuring the 𝑉𝐻 (𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐) process,
the entire analysis strategy is validated using the 𝑉𝑍 (𝑍 → 𝑐𝑐) process. The 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 result
is obtained by performing a simultaneous fit with this analysis channel, dubbed the merged-
jet analysis channel, and the other resolved-jet analysis channel that reconstructs the Higgs
boson with two separate small-𝑅 jets, using orthogonal event phase spaces between the two
channels. The results show that for the 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 process, the observed (expected) upper limit
on the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal strength at a 95% C.L. was 14 (7.6), establishing the observed (ex-
pected) range for the Higgs-charm coupling modifier 𝜅𝑐 within a 95% C.L. as 1.1 < |𝜅𝑐 | < 5.5
(|𝜅𝑐 | < 3.4), representing the best direct measurement of the Higgs boson to 𝑐𝑐 coupling to
date. As a validation of the analysis strategy, the process of 𝑍 → 𝑐𝑐 production accompanied
by a vector boson achieved the first observation of the 𝑍 → 𝑐𝑐 process at a hadron collider, with
a statistical significance of 5.9 𝜎. This result highlights the exceptional advantage of advanced
DNN algorithms in processing large-𝑅 jets, especially in high-performance jet tagging.


    Part V introduces a novel approach to large-𝑅 jet tagging, building upon the experimental
application of the ParticleNet algorithm in the VHcc measurement and integrating advance-
ments in deep learning. The work explores the development of a pre-trained, comprehensive
and large jet model for large-𝑅 jets and employs the “pre-training and fine-tuning” paradigm
to extend its applicability across a wide range of physics analyses. The discussion begins
by revisiting the jet tagging problem from statistical principles, understanding the theoretical
limits of the problem and explaining why DNN algorithms are helpful in approaching this
limit. It further examines various potential directions for the future development of DNN-
based jet models, with an emphasis on the emergence of the fine-tuning paradigm, inspired
by established analysis design principles. The focus then shifts to developments within the
CMS experiment, leveraging the “large model for large-scale classification” strategy. This
involves a combination of classification and regression tasks to train a versatile, pre-trained
large jet model named Global Particle Transformer (GloParT), developed using hundreds of
finely categorized large-𝑅 jet classes. Detailed information is provided on the sample produc-
tion process, jet categorization, model input design, and training methodology. The model’s
performance across a wide range of classification and regression tasks is showcased, demon-
                                               XII
                                Extended Abstract (English Edition)


strating its ability to achieve optimal performance in nearly all evaluated scenarios. On top of
this, the following chapter uses two examples to demonstrate how fine-tuning can be employed
to unlock the potential of GloParT for specific applications within the CMS experiment. These
examples focus on cases of transfer learning, where GloParT’s latent space features serve as
input for subsequent networks, a suitable approach for integration into the current experimental
workflow. The first example explores fine-tuning the model to identify jets from a new category
that is not included in the training, i.e. a leptoquark resonance decaying into a 𝑏𝜏 final state.
The second example investigates the model’s ability to derive other jet models commonly used
in CMS analyses, showcasing its capabilities as a “base jet model” for analysis-level applica-
tions. Specifically, the model is transferred to a top-jet tagging model that does not require
mass decorrelation, utilizing GloParT’s latent features combined with additional jet mass and
momentum inputs. These studies demonstrate that deploying a pre-trained jet model and facili-
tating the “pre-training and fine-tuning” paradigm is expected to have a broad application value
in future CMS analyses. The final chapter of this part presents the reproduction of the GloParT
training and application scenarios on datasets generated via DELPHES’s fast simulation. This
work introduces the development of the JETCLASS-II dataset—a large-𝑅 jet dataset spanning
an extensive range of transverse momentum and mass values and covering comprehensive jet
phase space—and the Sophon model, which is pre-trained on this dataset. This work highlights
the Sophon model’s capability of producing multi-output probabilities and utilizing its latent
space representations for model transfer, revealing its tremendous potential for both model-
specific and model-agnostic searches for new resonances at the LHC. The part concludes with
a discussion on the large potential of GloParT to contribute to Run 3 physics analyses in the
CMS experiment, along with the significant value of the JETCLASS-II dataset and the Sophon
model for training foundation models in future high-energy physics research.


    In summary, this dissertation approaches the topic from the perspective of cutting-edge
deep learning technologies applied to large-𝑅 jet tagging, providing a comprehensive overview
of recent advancements in algorithm development, calibration techniques within the CMS ex-
periment, the physics application, and its potential for future applications. These topics reflect
research projects in which the author was deeply involved or served as a principal contributor
during the doctoral studies. As a final remark, it is a compelling era to witness the transfor-
mative impact of deep learning technologies on scientific research and beyond. Their capacity
to drive innovation in large-scale scientific programmes and to enhance our understanding of
fundamental science demonstrates their profound significance. The author aims to continue
                                               XIII
                             Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


contributing to this transformative journey through future research endeavours.




KEY WORDS: large-𝑅 jets, deep learning, Transformer, calibration, Higgs physics, pre-training
and fine-tuning
                                              XIV
                                             Abstract




Abstract

    Modern deep learning technologies have demonstrated remarkable potential in analyzing
complex LHC data, accelerating foundational scientific discoveries at the LHC. This doctoral
dissertation focuses on modern deep learning techniques for tagging large-𝑅 jets, one of the
most challenging physics objects to analyze at the LHC. It discusses novel methods, applica-
tions, and potentials in jet research. Surrounding this technology, the dissertation systemat-
ically summarizes the forefront progress in various aspects, involving advanced deep neural
network (DNN) algorithms, calibration methods developed in the CMS experiment, break-
throughs in measuring Higgs boson properties, and discusses the new application paradigms
of this technology in future experiments.
    The dissertation is organized into five parts. The first part introduces the fundamentals
of experimental particle physics and deep learning. The second part discusses new develop-
ments in the field of large-𝑅 jet tagging algorithms in the past three years, with a special focus
on the algorithm designs that respect Lorentz symmetry and DNN algorithms based on the
Transformer architecture. The third part introduces a new method for calibrating advanced
DNN-based 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 or 𝑐𝑐 jet tagging algorithms in the CMS experiment, namely the sfBDT
calibration method: this method uses multivariate analysis to select a phase space closer to
𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 or 𝑐𝑐 jets in gluon-splitting 𝑏𝑏 or 𝑐𝑐 jets as a proxy for calibration, tacking the problem
of previous methods failing to calibrate advanced DNN-based taggers. The fourth part presents
significant physics results obtained using cutting-edge DNN algorithms in the CMS experi-
ment in measuring the Higgs-charm Yukawa coupling strengths: this work aims to explore
the phase space of highly Lorentz-boosted Higgs boson decays into a charm quark-antiquark
pair (𝑐𝑐) reconstructed by a large-𝑅 jet, using the advanced ParticleNet algorithm for 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐
jet tagging and mass regression, resulting in the best direct measurement of 𝜅𝑐 and marking
the first observation of the 𝑍 → 𝑐𝑐 process at a hadron collider. Finally, the fifth part in-
troduces a novel application paradigm for large-𝑅 jet tagging by leveraging advancements in
deep learning. It emphasizes the development of pre-trained, comprehensive jet models and
the adoption of the “pre-training and fine-tuning” paradigm to extend their applicability to di-
verse downstream tasks. Key contributions include the Global Particle Transformer (GloParT)
model, developed within the CMS collaboration, and the Sophon model, trained on the newly
                                               XV
                             Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


developed JETCLASS-II dataset. Extensive experiments demonstrate their significant potential
for CMS Run 3 analyses and future research.
    Through a comprehensive discussion of the forefront progress of modern deep learning
technology applied to large-𝑅 jet tagging, the dissertation reveals their transformative impact
on particle physics, particularly in the CMS experiment. It highlights the growing role of deep
learning in contemporary scientific research patterns.




KEY WORDS: large-𝑅 jets, deep learning, Transformer, calibration, Higgs physics, pre-training
and fine-tuning

                                                                                    XXXVI
  Part I

Foundations




     1
                             Chapter 1   Experimental Particle Physics




Chapter 1
Experimental Particle Physics

1.1 The standard model
1.1.1 The path to the SM
    The journey of particle physics to the standard model (SM) can be traced back to the 19th
century with the discovery of the electron by J. J. Thomson[1] , marking the divisibility of the
atom. This was followed by the discovery of the nucleus in 1911[2] and the discovery of the
neutron in 1932[3] . On the theory’s side, quantum mechanics emerged in the 1920s and was
found suitable for describing the atomic and subatomic processes. It soon evolved into quantum
field theory (QFT), a specific form of which is the quantum electrodynamics (QED) established
in the 1940s[4-7] that describes electromagnetic interactions within a quantum framework and
is integrated with special relativity. In the 1950s, the Yang-Mills theory was introduced[8] , in-
corporating QFT with the non-Abelian gauge group, which was crucial to the later formation
and unification of electroweak interactions[9, 10] . At the same time, advances in accelerator
technology boosted the discovery of a large number of subatomic particles, which led to the
quark model[11] and the formulation of the quantum chromodynamics (QCD)[12, 13] . Follow-
ing this, the formulation of the Higgs mechanisms in the 1960s[14-16] explains how particles
acquire mass through spontaneous symmetry breaking in gauge theories. These theoretical
and experimental milestones paved the way for the SM, a comprehensive theory that unifies
all known elementary particles and forces except for gravity.
    A general picture of the SM is provided in Fig. 1.1 to showcase the particle constituents and
their interactions. At its heart is the Higgs boson, which gives masses to the other particles. 𝑊
and 𝑍 bosons are the carriers of the weak interaction, and they acquire mass via spontaneous
symmetry breaking in the Higgs field. The photon, which mediates the electromagnetic force,
remains massless, preserving the U(1) gauge symmetry associated with electromagnetism. The
gluon is also a massless boson that mediates the strong interaction. It holds quarks together
within protons, neutrons, and other hadrons. In addition, fermions are the building blocks of
matter and are divided into quarks and leptons. They all constitute three generations. The first
generation includes up and down quarks, which make up protons and neutrons, and the electron
                                                3
                                      Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


along with its neutrino. The second and third generations are heavier counterparts, including
the charm and strange quarks, the muon and its neutrino, for the second generation; and the
top and bottom quarks, and tau and its neutrino, for the third generation.

                                1st            2nd           3rd




                                                                                             strong nuclear force
                                2.2 M         1.28 G        173.1 G           Mass: eV/c2                               125.09 G


                              u    +2/3
                                              c   +2/3

                                                            t   +2/3            Charge

                    QUARKS

Figure 1.1 A visualization of all elementary particles in the SM and their properties summa-
rized in Ref.[17] .


1.1.2 The SM Lagrangian
    This principle of symmetry is the core of the SM framework. The Lagrangian of the SM
is built to respect the SU(3) × SU(2) × U(1) gauge symmetry. It describes the fermions con-
stituting the world and their interactions mediated by bosons. The SU(3) describes the nature
of quantum chromodynamics, and SU(2) × U(1) reveals the unified electroweak interactions.
Given that discussions of the SM framework have been widely covered in various literature
over several decades, a concise summary is provided as follows.
    The compact form of the SM Lagrangian is written as
           1 𝑎 𝜇𝜈
      ℒ = − 𝐹𝜇𝜈       ̄ 𝜇 𝐷𝜇 − 𝑚)𝜓 + (𝑦𝑖𝑗 𝜓̄ 𝑖 𝜙𝜓𝑗 + ℎ.𝑐.) + |𝐷𝜇 𝜙|2 − 𝑉 (𝜙).
                𝐹𝑎 + 𝜓(𝑖𝛾                                                                                                                                                 (1.1)
           4
Here, the first term corresponds to the kinetic term for gauge field, where 𝐹𝜇𝜈 is the field
strength tensor that covers the electroweak force and the strong force. The second term is the
coupling of fermions, such as leptons and quarks, to the gauge field. The third term character-
izes the Yukawa coupling, and it describes the interaction of the fermion fields with the Higgs
field. The fourth term is the Higgs interaction with the boson field. The fifth term is the Higgs
field potential. Each term maintains its form under the gauge transformations. These interac-
tions are depicted in Fig. 1.2, in the format of Feynman diagrams, a tool to graphically present
                                                                          4
                            Chapter 1   Experimental Particle Physics


the quantum fields and their interaction within a Lagrangian.

1.1.3 Higgs mechanism and the origin of mass
    Allowing particles to acquire mass without breaking the gauge symmetry was a challenge
in history, and the Higgs mechanism provides a viable solution. The Higgs mechanism intro-
duces a self-interacting complex scalar field 𝜙, which spontaneously breaks the SU(2) × U(1)
symmetry. A concise summary is presented below.
    The Higgs field is a complex scalar doublet field,

                                          1     𝜙1 + 𝑖𝜙2
                                   𝜙=                       .                          (1.2)
                                         √2 (𝜙3 + 𝑖𝜙4 )
With the Higgs field, the Higgs potential term in SM Lagrangian is designated in the form of

                                 𝑉 (𝜙) = 𝜇 2 𝜙† 𝜙 + 𝜆(𝜙† 𝜙)2 .                         (1.3)

The minimum potential is not at the zero point when 𝜇 2 < 0 and 𝜆 > 0. Without loss of
generality, the physics vacuum state where the potential reaches the minimum can be written
                                               5
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


as
                                    1      0                                𝜇2
                            ⟨𝜙⟩ =                ,     where 𝑣 = √−            .           (1.4)
                                    √2 (𝑣)                                  𝜆

The choice of a specific vacuum state indices the spontaneous symmetry breaking. The scalar
field can be expanded around the vacuum expectation value as

                                            1         𝜙1 + 𝑖𝜙2
                                    𝜙=                               ,                     (1.5)
                                          √2 (𝑣 + ℎ + 𝑖𝑎)
where ℎ is the physical Higgs bosons. These Goldstone bosons associated with the fields 𝜙1 ,
𝜙2 , and 𝑎0 are absorbed by the electroweak gauge fields, providing the necessary longitudinal
components for the 𝑊 ± and 𝑍 bosons. This is the procedure through which they acquire
masses. The mass equations for the 𝑊 ± and 𝑍 bosons are directly proportional to the VEV,
with
                                      𝑔 2 𝑣2                 (𝑔 2 + 𝑔 ′2 )𝑣2
                              𝑚2𝑊 =          ,       𝑚2𝑍 =                   ,             (1.6)
                                        4                          4
where 𝑔 and 𝑔 ′ are the gauge couplings for the SU(2) and U(1) gauge fields. Here, ℎ denotes the
single scalar field degree of freedom, and it emerges as the Higgs boson with a mass determined
by the Higgs potential parameters, given by 𝑚𝐻 = 𝑣√2𝜆. After the electroweak symmetry
breaking, fermions gain mass through Yukawa interactions with the Higgs field. This fermion
acquire masses (𝑚𝑓 ) as
                                                      1
                                          𝑚𝑓 =            𝑣𝑦𝑓 ,                            (1.7)
                                                     √2
which is proportional to 𝑦𝑓 , the corresponding Yukawa coupling strength. Part IV in this
dissertation focuses on the measurement of the Yukawa coupling between the Higgs boson
and the charm quark, 𝑦𝑐 . Eqs. (1.6) and (1.7) highlight the structure of the SM related to the
Higgs mechanism and its foundation role in the generation of particle masses for the SM weak
bosons and fermions.


1.1.4 Higgs boson phenomenology

       In the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) experiments, a thorough understanding of the Higgs
boson’s phenomenology is essential for finding the most promising final states and implement-
ing optimal event reconstruction methods, which enable precise measurements of its properties.
This section highlights the key aspects of the phenomenology of the Higgs physics at the LHC,
with a focus on Higgs boson production and decay modes. In a proton-proton collision at the
LHC, Higgs bosons are primarily produced through four mechanisms:
                                                      6
                                                                       Chapter 1               Experimental Particle Physics


Gluon fusion (ggF)                                       The most dominant Higgs production mode at the LHC involves the
fusion of two gluons mediated by a virtual quark loop, depicted in Fig. 1.3 (a). This method is
known as the gluon fusion process, often denoted by ggF.

Vector boson fusion (VBF)                                                   The VBF process produces a Higgs boson through the interac-
tion of two vector bosons, which are emitted by quarks, as shown in Fig. 1.3 (b). The process
is characterized by the jets induced from the emitted quarks being widely separated in pseudo-
rapidity 𝜂 and having a large invariant mass.

Higgs boson production associated with a vector boson (𝑉𝐻)                                                                                               The Higgs boson can be
produced in association with a vector boson 𝑊 or 𝑍, denoted by 𝑉𝐻. The Feynman diagram
of this process is shown in Fig. 1.3 (c). It is also referred to as the Higgsstrahlung process.
The emitted vector boson can provide a triggering of the Higgs boson to facilitate the experi-
mental search. This production channel is the main research target in the physics measurement
discussed in Part IV.

Higgs boson production associated with a top quark-antiquark pair (𝑡𝑡𝐻)                                                                                                       The 𝑡𝑡𝐻 pro-
cess involves the Higgs boson production in association with a top quark-antiquark pair, illus-
trated partially in Fig. 1.3 (d). It is notable for its lower cross section owing to the substantial
mass of the resulting particles.

         The above processes are the dominant Higgs production mode at the LHC. The cross
sections of these processes are compared as a function of centre-of-mass energy in Fig. 1.4 on
the left plot. Additional production modes include the 𝐻 production associated with 𝑏𝑏, which
is also shown in Fig. 1.3 (d), or associated with a single top quark as shown in the Fig. 1.3 (e,
f).
                                               Higgs boson production modes                                                                         Higgs boson decay channels

Figure 1.4 The Higgs boson production cross sections in various production modes as a func-
tion of the centre-of-mass energy[20] (left), and the branching functions as a function of the
Higgs boson mass[20] (right).


      Regarding the decay of the Higgs boson, it can decay through multiple channels due to
its interactions with leptons, quarks, and vector bosons, each with distinct branching fractions
shown in Fig. 1.4 on the right plot. In general, the decay of the Higgs boson prefers heavier
final-state particles but is subject to phase-space limitations. The decay modes are briefly
outlined below.

Decays to vector bosons                                            The Feynman diagram for Higgs boson decay into a pair of vector
bosons is shown in Fig. 1.3 (g). The mass constraints of the 𝑊 and 𝑍 bosons forbid Higgs
boson’s onshell decay into vector bosons. However, the decay branching ratios to the onshell–
offshell 𝑊𝑊 ∗ and 𝑍𝑍 ∗ pairs are still considerable. In addition, the decay to a pair of 𝑍
bosons plays an important role at the LHC in the Higgs boson due to a very clean experimental
signature in the four-lepton final state.

Decays to fermions                                       The Higgs boson decays to fermions through a direct Yukawa coupling,
depicted in Fig. 1.3 (h). Given that the Yukawa coupling strength is proportional to the fermion
mass, the Higgs boson’s decay to first-generation fermions, i.e. 𝑏𝑏̄ and 𝜏 + 𝜏 − pairs, are signifi-
cant. These decays are predominant but challenging to isolate in hadron collider environments
due to the difficulty in constructing their final state signatures. Searching for their decay into
second-generation fermions can be more challenging as the branching ratio is small. Neverthe-
less, they can be the next important milestone in uncovering the Higgs boson property. Part IV
of this dissertation discusses the recent measurement of the Higgs boson decaying to 𝑐𝑐 within
the CMS experiment.
                                                                                                               8
                             Chapter 1   Experimental Particle Physics


Loop-induced decay processes The Higgs boson decay into massless particles, including
gluons and photons, is possible through a loop-induced process. The Higgs boson to 𝑔𝑔 decay
takes up a considerable proportion but its direct measurement is difficult. Meanwhile, the
Higgs boson decaying to diphoton through a vector-boson loop or a fermion loop, as shown in
Fig. 1.3 (i, j), provides clear modelling of a peak structure on the diphoton invariant mass and
is the golden channel to probe the Higgs boson at the LHC.


1.2 The Large Hadron Collider
     The LHC is the world’s largest and highest-energy particle accelerator. Located on the
border of Switzerland and France, it spans a total length of 27 km and is designed for accel-
erating heavy ions for collision, primarily focusing on proton-proton collisions. The LHC is de-
signed to reach collision energies of 14 TeV, equivalent to accelerating protons to 99.9999991%
of the speed of light.
     The acceleration of protons in the LHC is achieved through multiple stages, including
initial acceleration in Linac 2, boosting in the Proton Synchrotron Booster (PSB), further ac-
celeration in the Proton Synchrotron (PS) and Super Proton Synchrotron (SPS), and finally
injected into LHC’s vacuum tubes. A schematic view of the LHC with various accelerators at
CERN is shown in Fig. 1.5.




                         Figure 1.5   A schematic view of the LHC[21] .

     To increase the probability of proton collisions, the protons in the accelerator are squeezed
into a very small space before colliding. Therefore, the collisions at the collision points involve
proton bunches. The collision of proton bunches (bunch crossing) is every 25 nanoseconds or a
collision frequency of 40 MHz. Proton collisions occur at four particle detectors on the LHC:
                                                9
                                                                        Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


ATLAS (A Toroidal LHC Apparatus), CMS (Compact Muon Solenoid), ALICE (A Large
Ion Collider Experiment), and LHCb (Large Hadron Collider beauty). ALICE is a heavy-ion
detector primarily focused on studying quark-gluon plasma at extreme energy densities. LHCb
explores the differences between matter and antimatter through 𝑏-quark physics. ATLAS and
CMS are two general-purpose detectors with the same research scope and physical objectives.
        The LHC programme’s physics goals range widely, including the detailed study of SM,
properties of the Higgs boson, and the extensive search of the beyond-the-SM (BSM) signa-
tures. The latter includes searching for dark matter, exploration of supersymmetry, searching
for extra dimensions, etc. It allows physicists to test the predictions of the particle physics
theories. The independent discovery of the Higgs boson in 2012 by the ATLAS and CMS
experiments marks a major milestone in the success of the LHC programmes.
        The LHC programmes have evolved through various operational phases. The Run 2 data-
taking period (2015–2018) was operated at the centre-of-mass energy of 13 TeV and collected
data corresponding to an integrated luminosity of around 140 fb−1 . In the year 2024, the Run
3 period (2022–2025) is ongoing, with the LHC’s increased luminosity and energy. The peak
luminosity can reach around 2 × 1034 cm−2 s−1 , and the energy at a centre-of-mass energy of
13.6 TeV. The High-Luminosity LHC (HL-LHC) project is a substantial upgrade planned to
start in 2029 following the long shutdown 3 (LS3). The HL-LHC aims to significantly increase
the luminosity, potentially by a factor of 10, extending the LHC’s operational life and discovery
potential out to 2041. Figure 1.6 shows the timeline of the LHC and HL-LHC programmes.
                                                                                       LHC                                                                                            HL-LHC

        Run 1                                               Run 2                                                                  Run 3                                                       Run 4 - 5...


                             LS1                            EYETS                                LS2                       13.6 TeV          EYETS                 LS3                        13.6 - 14 TeV
                                              13 TeV
                                                                                         Diodes Consolidation
                      splice consolidation                              cryolimit           LIU Installation                                                      HL-LHC
7 TeV     8 TeV        button collimators                               interaction                                                           inner triplet
                           R2E project                                  regions              Civil Eng. P1-P5        pilot beam               radiation limit   installation

                                                                                                                                                                   2027        2028    2029               2040
                                                                                                                                                                                              5 to 7.5 x nominal Lumi

1.3 The CMS detector
        Located in Cessy, France, the CMS detector is one of two general-purpose colliders whose
physics targets include precision measurement of the SM and the search for new physics.
                                                                                                                10
                            Chapter 1   Experimental Particle Physics


    The CMS detector is 21 m long, 15 m in diameter and weighs about 14000 tons. It consti-
tutes the following subdetectors: the inner tracking system including the silicon pixel detector
and silicon stripe detector, an electromagnetic calorimeter, a hadron calorimeter, and a muon
system. A cutoff view of the CMS detector with its subcomponents is shown in Fig. 1.7. Each
subdetector consists of a small and individual hardware unit covering most of the area around
the collision point. All subdetectors are inside a superconducting solenoid at 3.8 T, except for
the muon system and a minor part of the hadron calorimeter. When charged particles pass
through the trackers, the large magnetic field induces a curved trajectory, which enables a
precise measurement of the particle momenta.




                  Figure 1.7    A cutaway diagram of the CMS detector[22] .




CMS coordinate system The CMS experiment uses a right-handed coordinate system. In
this system, the origin lies in the nominal interaction point. The 𝑧 axis runs counterclock-
wise along the beam direction and the 𝑥–𝑦 plane is the transverse plane. The 𝑥 axis points
to the centre of the LHC ring, and the 𝑦 axis points upward. The 𝜙 and 𝑟 characterize the
azimuthal angle and the radial coordinate on the 𝑥–𝑦 plane. The coordinate system is depicted
in Fig. 1.8. Besides, in the three-dimensional coordinate, the polar angle is denoted 𝜃, and the
pseudorapidity 𝜂 is defined as 𝜂 = − log(tan(𝜃/2)).
                                               11
                               Peking University Doctoral Dissertation




                      Figure 1.8    Illustration of CMS coordinate system.


1.3.1 Inner tracker

     The inner tracking system measures the trajectories of charged particles. The tracker is
divided into several subregions, as illustrated in Fig. 1.9. Closest to the collision point lies the
silicon pixel detectors, each with a pixel size of 100 × 150 𝜇m2 . The pixel detector has a total
surface area of approximately 1 m2 with around 6.6 × 107 pixels. The silicon strip tracker is
placed outside of the pixel detector, composed of 80-to-180 𝜇m-wide strips. It is subdivided
into the inner barrel part (TIB), the inner disks (TID), the outer barrel (TOB) and outer end-
caps (TEC), with the configuration outlined in Fig. 1.9. The silicon strip tracker spans 200m2
with about 9.6 × 106 strips.
     The inner tracker covers a spatial extent of |𝜂| < 2.4. This setup enables the tracking
of particles through electrical signals generated by their interaction with the detector, with
material thickness varying from about 0.4𝑋0 at 𝜂 = 0, to around 1𝑋0 at |𝜂| = 1.6, and back to
0.6𝑋0 at |𝜂| = 2.5, where 𝑋0 denotes the radiation length.
     The pixel detector encountered an upgrade in the LHC’s year-end technical stop in 2016–
2017, during the Run 2 data-taking period. The new detector is named the Phase I pixel de-
tector, and it adds a new pixel layer in the barrel and endcap section, increasing the coverage
of pseudorapidity from |𝜂| = 2.5 to |𝜂| = 3.


1.3.2 Electromagnetic calorimeter

     The electromagnetic calorimeter (ECAL) is built with lead tungstate (PbWO4 ) crystals,
which accurately measure the energy of electrons and photons. It utilizes a dense yet opti-
cally transparent material to stop high-energy electrons and photons. The material is primarily
metallic, heavier than stainless steel but highly transparent. It emits scintillation light pro-
portional to the energy deposited by electrons and photons, making it an accurate, fast, and
                                                 12
                             Chapter 1   Experimental Particle Physics




Figure 1.9 An 𝑅-𝑧 cross section of a half of the CMS detector, showing the layout of the
inner tracker system[23] .
                                           .

compact detector. This energy is then collected via photodiodes, which estimate the energy of
an incident particle.
     The ECAL comprises a barrel and two endcaps positioned between the tracker and the
hadron calorimeter. The barrel covers |𝜂| < 1.479 and the two endcap disks 1.479 < |𝜂| < 3.0.
In ECAL barrel section contains 61200 crystals, and the traverse size of the crystal measures
22 mm × 22 mm at the front face, corresponding to 0.0174 × 0.0174 on the (𝜂, 𝜙) space, with
a length of 230 mm. The ECAL endcaps are made up of nearly 15000 crystals. The relative
energy resolution 𝜎/𝐸 shows
                              𝜎   2.8%   12%
                                =      ⊕       ⊕ 0.3%.                                       (1.8)
                              𝐸 √𝐸/ GeV 𝐸/ GeV

To achieve higher spatial resolution, ECAL includes a preshower detector with superior granu-
larity located in front of the endcaps, made of lead block and silicon sensor strips. It allows for
distinguishing between single high-energy photons and two closely spaced low-energy photons
and improves the separation power between photons and neutral pions.


1.3.3 Hadron calorimeter

     The hadron calorimeter (HCAL) is a hermetic sampling calorimeter built around the ECAL
and inside the solenoid, used to measure the energy of outgoing hadrons. It is built by alter-
nating brass absorber and plastic scintillator layers, the later emit light when a particle passes
through, collected by optic fibers into photodetectors for amplification. The light across is
summed over multiple layers and used to estimate the particle’s energy. The HCAL has a bar-
                                                13
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


rel covering |𝜂| < 1.3 and two endcaps covering 1.3 < |𝜂| < 3.0. In addition, the HCAL Outer
(HO) is installed outside the solenoid and serves as an additional absorber covering |𝜂| < 1.3.
The HCAL segmentation in the (𝜂, 𝜙) is space is 0.087 × 0.087 for |𝜂| < 1.6 and 0.17 × 0.17
for higher pseudorapidities, which is much more coarse compared to the ECAL. The energy
resolution 𝜎/𝐸 for the combined ECAL and HCAL measured in a pion test beam is
                                      𝜎   110%
                                        =      ⊕ 9%.                                        (1.9)
                                      𝐸 𝐸/ GeV

1.3.4 Muon chambers

    The CMS experiment has the best muon identification system at the LHC. Muons can
penetrate the calorimeters, so they are detected at the very edges of the CMS using a dedicated
muon system. The system consists of a barrel and two endcaps. Its detection planes are inter-
leaved with the steel yoke that returns the solenoid’s magnetic flux. Three types of gaseous par-
ticle detectors are used in the muon system, outlined in Fig. 1.10. The drift tube (DT) chambers
are used for precise trajectory measurements in the central barrel region (|𝜂| < 1.2) of the de-
tector. In contrast, the cathode strip chambers (CSC) are used in the endcaps (0.9 < |𝜂| < 2.4),
areas with uneven magnetic fields and high radiation levels. The DT and CSC subsystems can
not only provide measurements on muons. Still, they can also used to trigger the 𝑝T of muons
with excellent efficiency and high background rejection, independent of the rest of the detec-
tor. The resistive plate chambers (RPC) are installed in both the barrel and endcaps covering
|𝜂| < 1.6, providing rapid response for the CMS trigger system.


1.3.5 Trigger and data acquisition

    The rate of collisions (40 MHz) at the LHC will result in a data output of 15 PB per
year, which is far beyond the capability of mass storage systems. The CMS trigger and data-
acquisition system scrutinizes each event to determine which ones to store for analysis.
    The CMS trigger system is split into two levels: the Level-1 trigger (L1T) and the high-
level trigger (HLT). The L1T is built with custom hardware processors to collaborate with
the calorimeters and muon detectors to filter event rates down to less than 100 kHz within a
4 𝜇s latency. It selects events based on predefined energy thresholds or momentum for objects
like electrons, photons, muons, and jets, using logic encoded in application-specific integrated
circuits (ASICs) and programmable memory lookup tables (LUT) or field-programmable gate
arrays (FPGA) for implementations. The HLT further reduces the L1-accepted rate of 100 kHz
to 100 Hz for the final output, processing full events using large computing farms for tasks
                                                14
                             Chapter 1   Experimental Particle Physics




Figure 1.10 An 𝑅-𝑧 cross section of a quadrant of the CMS detector, showing the layout of
the muon detector[24] .


exploiting full detector information and making the software-level decisions on event storage.
It uses multiple dedicated streams or trigger paths for event selection at the analysis level.



1.4 Monte Carlo simulation in CMS
    It is essential to compare collected data with SM predictions in collider experiments. The
CMS experiment employs extensive Monte Carlo (MC) simulations to model phenomena ex-
pected in the detector. MC simulations are particularly valuable when analytical solutions are
infeasible due to the system’s complexity. They numerically estimate observables by sam-
pling pseudorandom numbers according to the phase space and using the acceptance-rejection
method to approximate integrals.
    In the CMS experiment’s typical workflow, MC simulations include multiple stages. First,
the simulation of a collision event produced by a proton-proton collision is delivered by event
generators, as detailed in Section 1.4.1. The simulation of the detector effect and the mixing
of pileup is introduced in Section 1.4.2.


1.4.1 Event generators in CMS

    Event generators are MC simulation tools used for exclusive collision event generation.
This process involves both the hard-scattering process and the parton showering.
                                                15
                               Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


    The inclusive cross section of a hard-scattering process can be analytically calculable by
QFT at the fixed order in 𝛼𝑠 . However, for exclusive event simulations in collider experiments,
MC simulations are employed, with physics models and the rules of QFT at their core. The
frequently used event generators at this stage within the CMS experiment mainly include MAD-
GRAPH5_aMC@NLO[25] (MG) and POWHEG[26, 27] . MG is a versatile event generator capable
of simulating hard-scattering processes at the leading order (LO) and next-to-leading order
(NLO) accuracy. It has flexible support for various physics models. POWHEG, on the other
hand, focuses on generating events with NLO accuracy in QCD while preserving the correct
behaviour of parton showers.



    The parton showering mainly includes fragmentation, a process to add new emissions
of quarks and gluons when the energy scale goes lower, and the hadronization process that
constructs colourless hadrons from the partons. They handle the non-perturbative QCD effect
at the low energy scale and are archived by dedicated phenomenological models. The PYTHIA
generator[28] is most frequently used within the CMS experiment, and HERWIG[29] is often used
alternatively as a validation. The event generators for parton showering can also simulate the
complex case for initial- or final-state radiation of the hard-scattered quarks or gluons, and
the particles that come from the break-up of the colliding protons. These processes are often
accompanied by the hard-scattering process and are called underlying events. In addition,
the PYTHIA and HERWIG generators include built-in matrix element methods that can compute
simpler processes at LO accuracy and subsequently evolve them through parton showering. In
the CMS experiment, the QCD multijet process, which has the highest statistical demand in
simulations, is primarily generated by PYTHIA, with HERWIG serving as an alternative option.



    In the CMS experiment, the physics events generated using the aforementioned methods,
represented by their respective event counts, are summarized in Fig. 1.11.From a technical per-
spective, the wide variety and combinations of event generators are integrated into the CMS
software (CMSSW) framework through various modules. These modules leverage various forms
of multithread computing utilities, which represent a key computational advantage at the core
of CMSSW[30] . The implementation of multithreaded event generation support was first intro-
duced during the Run-2 ultra-legacy data reprocessing campaign, where the author contributed
to its development.
                                                 16
                             Chapter 1   Experimental Particle Physics




Figure 1.11 A pie chart illustrating the distribution of usage across various event generators
and their combinations, represented by the number of events generated.


1.4.2 Detector simulation

    The hadronized particles induced by the hard-scattering and accompanying underlying
events are propagated to the location of multiple CMS subdetectors. This detector effect is
delivered by an MC simulation toolkit, GEANT4, which carries the detector layout of all CMS
subdetectors and simulates the interaction of these particles with the detector material.
    The detector simulation in CMS integrates the effect of pileup. Pileup describes the ad-
ditional proton-proton collisions when two beams of protons are made to collide. It can be
induced from the same bunch crossing (in-time pileup) or in the adjacent bunch crossing (out-
of-time pileup) that can overlap with the current readout chain. To simulate pileup, the inelastic
events are generated first with PYTHIA, proceeded to GEANT4 for detector simulation, and then
superimposed on the simulated hard-scattering event.



1.5 Event reconstruction in CMS
    Event reconstruction in the CMS experiment is essential for transforming raw detector
data into meaningful physics objects, such as electrons, muons, photons, and jets, enabling
effective analysis of particle collisions. At the heart of CMS event reconstruction lies the
particle-flow (PF) algorithm[31] , which combines information from all subdetectors to iden-
tify and reconstruct individual particles within each event. This leads to the creation of PF
candidates, providing a global description of the event (detailed in Section 1.5.1). The recon-
struction processes for electrons, muons, secondary vertices (SVs), jets, and missing transverse
                                                17
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


momentum are briefly described in Sections 1.5.2–1.5.6.
     To support the development of jet tagging tools in both Part IV and Part V, generator-level
jets (GEN-jets) are produced directly from stable hadronized particles. These GEN-jets serve
as truth-level references for jets and are detailed in Section 1.5.7.

1.5.1 Particle-flow candidate
     PF candidates are unified objects representing a reconstructed particle. They play a cen-
tral role throughout this dissertation, serving as the fundamental building blocks for jet recon-
struction in the CMS experiment and providing the primary input features for modern deep
learning-based jet processing.
     The PF candidates are constructed using the PF algorithm, which integrates information
from all subdetectors. For illustrative purposes, Fig. 1.12 (adapted from Ref.[31] ) depicts a
toy jet composed of charged and neutral hadrons, as well as photons. The charged hadrons
(𝜋 + and 𝜋 − in the figure) are identified through geometrical associations between the tracks
and calorimeter clusters (as well as the absence of a signal in the muon detectors). Photons
and neutral hadrons are linked to ECAL and HCAL clusters without associated tracks. In
addition, electrons are identified by the presence of a track and an ECAL cluster, while muons
are associated with tracks in both the inner tracker and the muon detectors (not depicted in the
figure).
     The process begins with the reconstruction of PF elements, including track reconstruction
in the inner tracker (detailed in Section 1.5.1.1), with specialized treatments for electron and
muon tracks, and calorimeter clusters in the electromagnetic and hadronic calorimeters (Sec-
tion 1.5.1.2). The PF elements from different subdetectors are linked together in a predefined
order to produce the PF candidates, as described in Section 1.5.1.3.

1.5.1.1    Track reconstruction

     Charged-particle track reconstruction is crucial for understanding particle trajectories,
their origins, and properties like momentum and direction. The method utilizes a Kalman
Filtering (KF) based combinatorial track finder that operates in three main stages: initial seed
generation from a few compatible hits, trajectory building to collect hits across the tracker
layers, and final fitting to determine the particle properties. Reconstructed tracks must pass
several criteria, including a minimum of eight hits for a track to be considered, originating
from a specific area around the beam axis, and having a certain minimum 𝑝T . Specifically,
the combinatorial track finder uses an iterative procedure. It was developed to enhance track-
                                                18
                                 Chapter 1       Experimental Particle Physics

                                 CMS

                                  H1

                              -250        -200     -150          -100      -50    0
                                                                                 x (cm)

Figure 1.12 An event display of a toy jet initiated by two charged particles 𝜋 + and 𝜋 − , one
neutral hadron 𝐾𝐿0 , and two photons from the 𝜋 0 decay, shown in the (𝑥 𝑦) coordinate[31] . The
symbols T1,2 , E1,2,3,4 and H1,2 represent tracks and energy deposit in ECAL and HCAL measured
by the CMS subdetectors corresponding to these particles.



ing efficiency without significantly increasing the misreconstruction rate. It employs multiple
rounds of tracking with gradually relaxed criteria. The later iterations aim to resolve challenges
in finding very displaced tracks and in distinguishing tracks within the dense high-𝑝T jets.

    Electron tracking benefits from both the ECAL-based approach, relying on energetic ECAL
clusters, and a tracker-based method, which uses iterative tracking for higher efficiency. The
tracker-based approach improves electron detection, particularly in nonradiated electrons and
radiated ones producing lower 𝑝T tracks. In this approach, the probability of radiation within
the tracker material is used to distinguish electrons from hadrons. Selected tracks are refitted
with a Gaussian-sum filter (GSF) and required to pass a BDT selection.

    Muon tracking uses the muon spectrometer and the inner tracker for high-efficiency iden-
tification over a wide momentum range. Different muon types, i.e. standalone, global, and
tracker muons, are identified based on different matchings between the inner track and muon
spectrometer track segment. The high efficiency of muon tracking is attributed to the precise
measurement capabilities of the inner tracker and the specific use of the muon spectrometer to
improve momentum resolution.
                                                          19
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


1.5.1.2   Calorimeter clusters reconstruction

     Energy deposits in calorimeters form the clusters, which is crucial to analyses as they mea-
sure the energy and direction of stable neutral particles such as photons and neutral hadrons.
The clustering algorithm distinguishes these neutral particles from charged particle energy de-
posits. It helps better reconstruct electrons and any bremsstrahlung photons that accompany
them and assists in measuring charged hadrons’ energy.
     In PF element reconstruction, a dedicated clustering algorithm is developed and performed
separately in each subdetector, including the ECAL barrel and endcaps, the HCAL barrel and
endcaps, and the two preshower layers. The clustering algorithm identifies cluster seeds in
cells with a minimum energy threshold and other requirements. Topological clusters then grow
from these seeds by including neighbouring cells. The clusters are reconstructed from these
topological clusters with an expectation-maximization algorithm based on a Gaussian-mixture
model. This model assumes energy deposits in cells follow Gaussian energy distribution cen-
tred around the seeds. In an iterative procedure, it adjusts the Gaussian distribution parameters
to fit the observed energy deposits.
     Calibrating the reconstructed calorimeter clusters is crucial to acquire accurate recon-
struction of photons and neutral hadrons. ECAL calibration includes the absolute calibration
of ECAL response to electrons and photons, cell-to-cell calibration, and refinement with the
collision data. Energy deposit in HCAL is adjusted to account for the nonlinear response to
hadrons, separating the case where hadrons deposit energy only in the HCAL and in both ECAL
and HCAL.


1.5.1.3   Particle-flow element linkage

     PF elements are linked through a set of specific conditions. Tracks from the central tracker
are extrapolated to the calorimeters and linked to clusters based on proximity to the cluster area,
which is adjusted by considering uncertainties due to gaps between cells, or effects on multiple
scattering. For electron bremsstrahlung photons, tangents to the GSF tracks are linked to ECAL
clusters if they lie within the cluster boundaries. Conversion finder algorithms link tracks
resulting from photon conversions. Calorimeter uster-to-cluster links are established between
HCAL and ECAL clusters and between ECAL and preshower clusters, preferring the links
with the smallest distances. Charged tracks can be linked through common secondary vertices,
indicating nuclear interactions. The tracks are also linked to the muon detector information for
reconstructing muons.
                                                20
                             Chapter 1   Experimental Particle Physics


     PF blocks result from these linkages, including PF elements related directly or indirectly.
The reconstruction sequence begins with muon identification, followed by electron reconstruc-
tion, which includes recovering bremsstrahlung photons. Isolated energetic photons are iden-
tified next. Then, tracks with high 𝑝T uncertainty are masked, and the remaining elements
undergo cross-identification for charged and neutral hadrons and photons. The reconstructed
PF blocks form the PF candidates as a global event description. As a final step, these candi-
dates are postprocessed to alleviate misreconstruction by correcting the large artificial missing
transverse momentum.

1.5.2 Muons
     The muon candidates are selected on top of the global and tracker muons with additional
criteria. To determine the isolated global muons, muons with a large energy deposit in their
vicinity are vetoed to reject more hadrons misidentified as muons. The selection of muons
inside a jet, which is crucial for identifying heavy flavours of jets, also requires more stringent
criteria. For muon momentum, the inner track’s 𝑝T is used if it is below 200 GeV; above this
threshold, the choice is based on the lowest 𝜒 2 probability among various track fits.

1.5.3 Electrons
     An electron candidate originates from a GSF track if its associated ECAL cluster is not
connected to three or more tracks. ECAL clusters in the PF block, linked to the supercluster or
a GSF track tangent, are assigned to the candidate. Tracks linked to these ECAL clusters are
considered part of the candidate if their momentum and the ECAL cluster’s energy support the
electron hypothesis. ECAL clusters and tracks from identified photon conversions connected
to the GSF track tangents are also included. The summed energy of these ECAL clusters is
adjusted for any missed energy during association. The electron candidate further undergoes
requirements on isolation and its energy deposit. The electron’s final energy is obtained by
combining the corrected ECAL energy with the momentum of the GSF track, and the electron’s
direction is chosen to be that of the GSF track. The electron is further selected with a BDT
using features associated with the GSF track and ECAL cluster.

1.5.4 Secondary vertices
     The SVs can indicate the commonality of displaced tracks. The presence of SVs provides
a good handle to identify a jet initiated from heavy-flavour hadrons. SVs are reconstructed
by the inclusive vertex finding (IVF) algorithm. The algorithm uses all reconstructed tracks
                                                21
                                  Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


in an event with loose criteria as input. Starting with identifying a collection of seed tracks,
it proceeds with the track clustering step, SV fitting and cleaning with the adaptive vertex
fitter[32] , track arbitration, and the SV refitting and cleaning procedure.


1.5.5 Jets

       Jets are clustered from PF candidates using the anti-𝑘T algorithm[33] with a distance pa-
rameter of 𝑅. This algorithm runs in an iterative clustering procedure to either merge two input
constituents into one or merge one constituent with the beam, which means that the constituent
is declared as a jet. In each step, the anti-𝑘T distance is computed for each pair of particles (𝑖,
𝑗),
                                                                    Δ𝑅2𝑖𝑗
                                      𝑑𝑖𝑗 =   min(𝑝−2
                                                   T,𝑖 ,   𝑝−2
                                                            T,𝑗 )           ,                (1.10)
                                                                     𝑅2
and between a particle and the beam,

                                                𝑑𝑖𝐵 = 𝑝−2
                                                       T,𝑖 ,                                 (1.11)

and proceed to the merging step. The procedure stops until there is no particle left. When
applying the anti-𝑘T algorithm, the small-𝑅 jet collection is obtained from parameter 𝑅 = 0.4
and is referred to as AK4 jets. In the CMS experiment, the large-𝑅 jet collection typically
results from a clustering parameter 𝑅 = 0.8 (AK8 jets) but can be customized by using an
even larger radius 𝑅 = 1.5 (AK15 jets) for certain analyses, e.g. in the Higgs-charm coupling
measurement discussed in Part IV.
       The particles originating from pileup are mitigated through the charged hadron subtrac-
      [34]
tion         algorithm for AK4 jets and the pileup per-particle identification (PUPPI)[35, 36] algo-
rithm for AK8 or AK15 jets. The CHS algorithm first identifies charged PF candidates origi-
nating from pileup and removes them before the jet clustering. After AK4 jets are clustered, an
event-by-event correction based on the jet area is applied to the jet four-momentum to remove
the remaining energy from pileup, mostly from the neutral particles. In the PUPPI algorithm,
charged particles identified to be originating from pileup are discarded, and a weight between
zero and one is assigned to each neutral particle as the probability of the particle originat-
ing from the primary vertex. The four-momenta is rescaled per particle to form the new PF
candidate collection, which is used as input to cluster AK8 or AK15 jets.
       The soft drop algorithm[37-39] is applied to each large-𝑅 jet to acquire the soft-drop mass
in a procedure to remove the soft radiation patterns within the jet progressively. This algorithm
first decluster a jet into its constituents and recluster them using the Cambridge/Aachen clus-
                                                     22
                              Chapter 1     Experimental Particle Physics


tering algorithm. This algorithm is similar to the anti-𝑘T one, except that the power index in
Eqs. (1.10, 1.11) on 𝑝T is changed from -2 to 0. This results in a clustering tree that represents
the merging history of the constituents. An iterative procedure runs on the reversed merging
history to split one constituent into two; for each splitting, the criteria
                                  min(𝑝T1 , 𝑝T2 )                              𝛽
                                                               Δ𝑅
                                                       > 𝑧cut                              (1.12)
                                      𝑝T1 + 𝑝T2               ( 𝑅0 )

is examined, with 𝛽 = 0 and 𝑧cut = 0.1, and 𝑅 = 0.8 or 1.5 for AK8 and AK15 jets. If
the criteria are failed, it indicates that one branch is comparable soft and is discarded, and the
procedure continues with the remaining branch. The procedure stops until the requirement
is satisfied; the remaining two branches are considered two subjets of the given net, and the
soft-drop mass is designated as the invariant mass of two subjets.

     The missing transverse momentum (𝑝⃗miss
