1.5.7 Generator-level objects
     The GEN-jets are clustered from all stable final particles generated by PYTHIA, with the
anti-𝑘T algorithm and 𝑅 = 0.8 or 1.5. Two cases are considered, one in which the neutrinos are
excluded from the particle list (denoted as “no-𝜈”), and another without the exclusion (denoted
as “with-𝜈”). The former is the default configuration, and the clustered GEN-jets provide a
reference for the reconstruction accuracy. For both cases, a soft drop algorithm with the same
settings as described above is adopted to mimic our handling of the reconstructed-level jets.
     The “no-𝜈” and “with-𝜈” GEN-jet collections after soft-drop are matched to the large-
𝑅 jet collection using the standard CMS convention. To perform the matching, each jet is
matched with the GEN-jet with the smallest Δ𝑅 with the jet, also requiring Δ𝑅 < 𝑅0 . This
                                                      23
                             Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


matching process is carried out for each jet, following a sequence sorted by decreasing jet 𝑝T .
Some jets may have no matched GEN-jet after this procedure. The “no-𝜈” and “with-𝜈” GEN-
jet soft-drop mass for each jet is determined as the mass of the matched GEN-jet for the two
collections.




                                               24
                                    Chapter 2   Deep Learning




Chapter 2

Deep Learning

2.1 The path to modern deep learning
    Deep learning is a specific type of machine learning that utilizes the ability of neural net-
works (see Fig. 2.1), usually with multiple layers, to extract progressively higher-level features
from data. The journey of building artificial neural networks was initiated in the 1940s when
the first effort of making brain-like neurons was made[40] . It provides a mathematical rep-
resentation of brain cells and initiates interest in creating networks that could simulate brain
functions. The first perceptron was devised later in the 1950s, which marks an early learning
algorithm that adjusts its learned parameters with data[41] . The first hierarchical, multilayer
perceptrons (MLP) were devised in 1965 and are generally considered the work pioneering the
first deep learning architectures. Despite this early success, the community soon encountered
criticism of the effectiveness of neural networks and witnessed the first “artificial intelligence
(AI) winter”. There were no further leaps until the 1980s, when the backpropagation algorithm
was developed[42] , allowing for more complex networks to learn. Another breakthrough during
this period was the introduction of the renowned convolutional neural networks designed for
image recognition tasks utilizing the backpropagation technique[43] , The long short-term mem-
ory (LSTM) network, designed in the late 1990s, is an efficient structure to handle sequence
data[44] . In 2006, the introduction of deep belief networks[45] demonstrated the potential of
deep architectures, advancing the arrival of the modern era of deep learning.




             Figure 2.1   A view of a typical neural network. Source: Mathworks.

                                                25
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


    In 2012, the introduction of AlexNet[46] marked a significant milestone in deep learning,
notably outperforming existing algorithms on the ImageNet challenge[47] . This breakthrough
led to a surge in deep learning research, where DNNs began to surpass traditional methods
consistently. Advances in hardware technology, such as graphic processing units (GPUs),
have also enhanced networks, which have grown larger and deeper. This allows substantial
methodological advancements across various domains, with a brief overview presented in the
next section. Today, we are on the verge of an era dramatically transformed by modern deep
learning, driven by its unprecedented capabilities to analyze, understand, and interact with data
in ways that mimic human intelligence.



2.2 Advances in deep learning
    The rapid development of deep learning over the past decade has reshaped the academic
landscape and its applications to broader fields. This section provides a selective and concise
summary directly relevant to the topic of this dissertation. It highlights advancements in train-
ing techniques and methodologies, algorithm developments, computational progress, and the
rapid expansion of application domains.
    The advancements in training methodologies have been the early driving force behind the
surge in deep learning applications. Historically, there have been many practical challenges to
train DNNs efficiently and to achieve high performance with DNNs. The challenges involve
the overfitting issue, vanishing or exploding gradient problems, the imbalance in datasets, etc.
Several proposed solutions have become standard within the community for present DNN de-
velopments. The innovative methodology in DNN training also includes transfer learning,
which leverages pre-trained models to achieve high performance in data-constrained environ-
ments (the one we will discuss in Part V), the model compression techniques that aim to reduce
model size without sacrificing accuracy, etc. They lead the advancement in the recent deep
learning development in large datasets and large model eras.
    Training algorithms involve engineering methods that develop network architectures and
training strategies that enhance learning efficiency and capabilities. The field is witnessing
a progressive development in the algorithm of DNNs. Several key milestones have been es-
tablished within the deep learning community. Early landmarks in the DNN design include
the development of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for image recognition[43, 48] and re-
current neural networks (RNNs), such as LSTM networks[44] , for natural language processing
(NLP). Recent achievements involve the generative adversarial neural networks (GANs)[49] ,
                                                26
                                     Chapter 2   Deep Learning


which start a new era of building generative models, and Transformers, which revolutionized
NLP by enabling models like BERT[50] and GPT[51] to understand and generate human-like
text. Section 2.2.1 will introduce typical DNN algorithms in the modern era that emerged
from the deep learning community. They also have a significant impact in their applications
to experimental particle physics.
     The computational advances have enabled the model to train faster and deploy more effi-
ciently, allowing the model to become larger, more complex, and thus more performant. The
evolution of hardware such as GPUs and field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs) has played
a vital role in this transformation. GPUs, with their parallel processing abilities, have proven
to be very effective for parallelizable tasks, especially for the training of DNNs.
     The deep learning applications were centred in the domain of computer vision (CV) and
NLP in their early development, but it has now found applications across a wide range of do-
mains involving all aspects of human lives and work. Deep learning applications for science
are especially intriguing as they can potentially transform how we do scientific research or de-
liver scientific discovery. One example is the invention of AlphaFold[52, 53] , which sets new
standards in protein structure prediction and accelerates biological research. In Part IV (Sec-
tions 7–9), a detailed discussion of its role in improving the understanding of the Higgs boson
properties, which is a central focus of this dissertation, will be presented.
     The following subsections will discuss detailed concepts in algorithm developments, which
are crucial to the rest of the dissertation.


2.2.1 Modern deep learning algorithms

     The MLP is the prototype of the neural network design, which is good at handling fixed-
dimensional data. The real-world data often manifest structural patterns and inherent hierar-
chies. Various algorithms are designed to accommodate different types of data.
     CNNs are designed to process data with grid-like structures, such as images. They employ
layers of convolutional filters to capture spatial hierarchies in the data. RNNs are designed to
process sequential data such as text or speech, and they have the unique ability to maintain a
memory of previous inputs through loops in their architecture. Figure 2.2 shows the prototype
of the two network classes. They drive the early advancements in CV and NLP, respectively.
     Following the widespread adoption of CNNs and RNNs, more expressive network archi-
tectures tailored for specific tasks were introduced, notably graph neural networks (GNNs) and
Transformers. These architectures have also substantially advanced the techniques in particle
                                                 27
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation




Figure 2.2    The prototype of convolutional neural networks and recurrent neural networks.
                                              .


physics. A detailed review of the structures and principles in GNNs and Transformers will be
provided below.



Graph neural networks       GNNs (initiated in, e.g. Ref.[54] ) extend deep learning frameworks
to graph-structured data. The algorithm efficiently proceeds with the local node features, the
edge features between pairwise nodes, and the global graph features by dedicated network
designs, mainly through aggregating information from a node’s neighbours (known as message
passing). This allows GNNs to be especially suitable for node classification, link prediction,
and graph classification. To be specific, consider the graph 𝐺 = (𝑉 , 𝐸) built from a list of
vertices and edges. In its 𝑙-th layer, denote the node feature as 𝑣𝑙𝑖 for the node 𝑖, and the edge
feature 𝑒𝑙𝑖𝑗 connecting the nodes 𝑖 and 𝑗, the message passing from all neighbouring nodes to a
given node can be generally described as an edge-to-node and a node-to-edge message passing,
written as

                                 𝑒𝑙𝑖𝑗̃ = 𝜌𝑙𝑉 →𝐸 (𝑣𝑙𝑖 , 𝑣𝑙𝑗 , 𝑒𝑙𝑖𝑗 ),
                                          ∑                                                 (2.1)
                                  𝑣𝑙𝑖̃ =        𝜌𝑙𝐸→𝑉 (𝑣𝑙𝑖 , 𝑣𝑙𝑗 , 𝑒𝑙𝑖𝑗̃ ),

Transformers The Transformer network[56] is a milestone in DNN algorithm design and it
started by setting a new standard in NLP by purely exploiting self-attention mechanisms. This
algorithm enables parallel processing and data and reduces training times. It brings the birth
of the large language models such as BERT[50] and GPT[51] that begin to transform human
lives in many aspects. Transformers have also rapidly manifested themselves as a universal
algorithm, suitable to be applied to many other domains such as CV (ViT[57] with its vari-
ants, e.g. Refs.[58, 59] ), graph learning (e.g. Ref.[60] ), point cloud recognition (e.g. Ref.[61] ),
etc. Transformer models have also shown their power in solving science problems. Examples
include AlphaFold 2[53] for accelerating protein structure prediction, and Pangu-Weather[62]
for improving global weather forecasting, etc.
     For generality, the multi-head attention operates on tokens represented by three vectors as

2.3 Deep learning dataset

     Data serves as the essential fuel for training performant models in the deep learning era.
The ImageNet dataset[47] is widely acknowledged as a key driver behind the rapid advancement
of modern deep learning algorithms. The quest for large, labelled datasets containing million-
level samples or beyond is crucial in almost every domain to advance deep learning progress.
Datasets produced outside of the experimental groups are widely used to develop jet-tagging
DNNs. This section introduces the datasets specifically developed for the jet tagging domain,
which will be revisited in both Part II and V.

     Unlike the CV and NLP domains, where unlabelled datasets are most common and abun-
dant, public datasets in the high-energy physics domain mostly come from simulated events
that are naturally labelled. These datasets are simulated with MC event generators for hard-
scattering process and DELPHES[63] for fast simulation of detector effects and reconstruction of
common analysis-level objects. DELPHES is incredibly useful for delivering phenomenological
studies in LHC physics and deep learning. In DELPHES, stable particles produced by event gen-
erators are propagated through the detectors configured with the DELPHES card and interacted
with the tracker, ECAL and HCAL modules sequentially to build charged-particle tracks and
calorimeter towers with simplified algorithms, based on predefined detector geometry, granu-
larity and resolution, etc. The processed tracks and towers are merged into the E-flow object
collection, which is considered the reconstructed particles using all the detector information, in
analogy with the PF candidates in the CMS experiment. Jets are clustered from E-flow objects
with the jet clustering algorithm, and they constitute the samples in the jet tagging dataset.

     The following paragraphs overview three jet tagging datasets.




Top tagging dataset The top tagging dataset (abbreviated as the Top dataset) comprises
1.2/0.4/0.4 M jets for training, validation, and testing. It includes two sets of large-𝑅 jets, one
initiated from the top quark and the other from the QCD multijet events initiated by quarks
and gluons. Events in this dataset are generated by PYTHIA8[64] and passed to DELPHES[63] for
fast simulation of the detector effect. Jets are clustered from the E-flow objects using the anti-
𝑘T algorithm with 𝑅 = 0.8. The dataset includes kinematics information of jet constituent,
with the 4-momenta saved in Cartesian coordinates (𝐸, 𝑝𝑥 , 𝑝𝑦 , 𝑝𝑧 ) for up to 200 highest-𝑝T
constituents.
                                                 31
                                Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


Quark–gluon tagging dataset The quark–gluon tagging dataset (abbreviated as the QG
dataset) includes 1.6/0.2/0.2 M jets for training, validation, and test. It includes two sets of
small-𝑅 jets, the quark jets and gluon jets, initiated from 𝑍(→ 𝜈𝜈) + 𝑢/𝑑/𝑠 and 𝑍(→ 𝜈𝜈) + 𝑔
generated by PYTHIA v8[64] , respectively. Jets are clustered from the E-flow objects with anti-
𝑘T and 𝑅 = 0.4.
     The dataset includes kinematics information of jet constituent and particle identification
(PID) information according to the reconstructed E-flow object. The PID varies from 8 types,
including electrons, muons, photons, charged hadrons (including charged pions, kions and
protons), and neutral hadrons (including neutral pions and neutrons). The PID is presented by
an integer following the Particle Data Group[17] numbering scheme. Particles are assigned a
positive PID, antiparticles a negative one. For reference, the absolute value of PID for 𝑒, 𝜇, 𝛾,
𝜋 ± , 𝜋 0 , 𝐾 ± , 𝑝, 𝑛 are 11, 13, 22, 211, 130, 321, 2212, 2112.
     Based on the earlier usage of the PID information for the QG dataset, two scenarios are
defined according to the convention in Ref.[65] . The first scenario is abbreviated as QGexp ,
where the information is restricted into only five classes, including electrons, muons, photons,
charged hadrons, and neutral hadrons, according to the detector limitation in real LHC ex-
periments further to distinguish the hadron types with charged and neutral ones. The second
scenario is named QGfull , where full identification of hadron types presented in this dataset is
used. Table 2.1 indicates the difference between the two scenarios.



JetClass dataset The JETCLASS dataset is large, comprising 100/20/20 M for training, vali-
dation, and testing. It is composed of ten classes of large-𝑅 jets, including five decay modes
of the Higgs boson, two decay modes of the top quark, two initiated by 𝑍 and 𝑊 bosons,
and one from the QCD event initiated by quarks and gluons. Events in this dataset are first
generated by MG[25] for resonance production and their decay, then they proceed to PYTHIA
v8[64] for parton showering and DELPHES[63] for detector effect simulation. Jets are clustered
similarly from the E-flow objects with 𝑅 = 0.8. The constituent-level features are used as the
input to the jet network, including the kinematics information, particle identification flags, and
trajectory displacement features, which are close to the feature list for the tagging development
within the CMS experiment.
     Due to the nature of multiclass classification, the recommended metrics are in the form
of binary background rejection, which evaluates the ability to separate two certain classes,
𝑆 (signal) and 𝐵 (background), where 𝐵 is typically the QCD class. Evaluating this metric
                                                  32
                                                         Chapter 2        Deep Learning


involves first defining binary 𝑆 vs. 𝐵 discriminant as
                                                                               score(𝐴)
                                             disc.(𝐴 vs. 𝐵) =                                ,                                          (2.6)
                                                                         score(𝐴) + score(𝐵)
then calculating the background rejection rate (1/𝜖𝐵 ) under given signal efficiency 𝜖𝑆 at 𝑋%,
similar to metrics defined in the Top and QG datasets.

        As a summary of the included features, Table 2.1 showcases the particle features in the
Top, QG (QGexp and QGfull ), and JETCLASS datasets. It includes several customizations to
standardise the inputs for the ease of training, e.g. the use of logarithm on momemtum and
energy, and the hyperbolic tangent on impact parameters. This will be the main configuration
adopted in the studies in Part II unless specifically mentioned.

JETCLASS dataset, adapted from Ref.[65] . Customizations are done including the expansion of
kinematic features and the use of logarithms and hyperbolic tangents for input standardization.

                     log 𝑝T        logarithm of the particle’s 𝑝T                                               ✓     ✓       ✓         ✓

                                                                          33
         Part II

Algorithms for Jet Tagging




            35
                              Chapter 3   An Overview on Algorithms




Chapter 3

An Overview on Algorithms
    Jet tagging aims to identify a jet’s origin using the available properties associated with
a jet. Developing algorithms for large-𝑅 jet tagging is more challenging due to the larger
complexity of the jet constituent particles and the diversity in the structure. This chapter will
focus on algorithms developed for the large-𝑅 jet tagging. First, a review of its development
history will be presented, spanning from the earlier rule-based criteria to the flourishing of
algorithms when machine learning approaches are widely considered.
    Especially, in the modern deep learning era, new opportunities arise when one can dive
deep into the low-level particle features and use an advanced neural network to explore the
comprehensive relations among the input features and different particles. This also brings
new challenges in making the algorithms more performative, where the knowledge is rooted
in the intersection of deep learning advances and particle physics. This chapter will review
the development path of deep learning algorithms. In Chapter 4, the recent advances on this
forefront will be highlighted.



3.1 Rule-based algorithms
    Rule-based algorithms have been developed to reflect jet properties from various aspects.
This section focuses on three aspects of them.


3.1.1 Jet grooming

    Jet grooming techniques[37-39, 66-68] , initiated a decade ago, particularly aid in mass recon-
struction when the large-𝑅 jet is initiated from a resonance. These techniques aim to remove
soft emissions within a jet that come from the pileup or underlying events. Including the soft
particles in the mass calculation may result in a smeared mass distribution.
    Four methods are highlighted and have been explored in the CMS and ATLAS experiment,
namely, jet trimming, jet pruning, jet filtering, and the soft drop algorithm. To probe the soft
patterns within a jet, these algorithms utilize the technique to recluster all constituents within
                                                37
                                Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


a jet to form subjets to identify the soft ones, or decluster the jet into constituent particles and
find the steps with soft radiation pattern. The procedure is briefly outlined in Fig. 3.1.




Figure 3.1 A schematic image of the four jet grooming algorithms: jet trimming, jet pruning,
jet filtering, and the soft drop algorithm. Image source: CMS Open Data workshops


     The soft drop algorithm among the four is most frequently used in the CMS experiment,
and its details can be found in Section 1.5.5. It is also called the modified mass drop tag-
ger (mMDT)[37] . The variable can be incorporated with a multivariate analysis or used for
likelihood fit to extract the signal jets directly.


3.1.2 Jet substructure

     Probing the jet substructure is a good handle to separate signal jets from backgrounds,
especially for the large-𝑅 jets where the substructure can reveal more complexity. One widely
used approach is to define 𝑁-subjettiness[69] to characterize the jet substructure. This variable
                                                  38
                                                                         Chapter 3              An Overview on Algorithms


provides a measure of the 𝑁 subjets that can be found inside the jet, defined as
                                                                   𝑀
                                                                1 ∑
                                                         𝜏𝑁   =      𝑝T,𝑖 min{Δ𝑅𝑖,sj1 , Δ𝑅𝑖,sj2 , ⋯ , Δ𝑅𝑖,sj𝑁 },                                                                                                           (3.1)
                                                                𝑑0
                                                                             𝑖=1

where 𝑀 is the number of constituents in the jet, Δ𝑅 = √(Δ𝜂)2 + (Δ𝜙)2 represents the angular
separation between each constituent and the subjets. The subjets within the jet are determined
from an exclusive 𝑘T algorithm on the original jet constituent before jet grooming is applied. If
the jet resembles an 𝑁-subjet structure, the constituents will be centred to the 𝑁 axis, resulting
in a smaller 𝜏𝑁 value. On top of this, the 𝑁-subjettiness ratio 𝜏𝑁𝑀 = 𝜏𝑁 /𝜏𝑀 is usually defined
to optimize for separating two classes of jets.
         The 𝑁-subjettiness has been widely used in the CMS experiment to identify the 2-prong
or multipronged signatures originating from a resonance. For example, 𝜏21 is designed to tag
2-prong hadronically decayed 𝑊 /𝑍/𝐻 jets against the QCD multijet background, and 𝜏32 can
be designed to identify 3-prong hadronically decayed top jets. Figure 3.2 shows the 𝜏21 and 𝜏32
normalized distributions for the 𝑊 /𝑍/𝐻 jets and top jets, respectively, comparing them with
the distribution of the QCD background jets to demonstrate the discriminating power.

jets on the 𝑁 -subjettiness ratio 𝜏21 and 𝜏32 , and generalized energy correlation function ratio
𝑁2 [70] .


         Another set of techniques reveals the energy distribution within a jet without the need to
reconstruct subjet axes. The construction is initiated from the 𝐶-correlators[71] ,
                                                                             𝑀               𝑀
                                                            𝑓                ∑               ∑                                                    𝜇               𝜇
                                                           𝐶𝑁𝑁           =             ⋯                        𝐸𝑖1 ⋯ 𝐸𝑖𝑁 𝑓𝑁 (𝑝𝑖̂ , ⋯ , 𝑝𝑖̂ ),                                                                             (3.2)
                                                                                                                                                     1             𝑁
                                                                             𝑖1 =1           𝑖𝑁 =1

                                                                                                                                                                  𝜇                       𝜇
where 𝑓𝑁 is a function on the pure direction of the particles, 𝑝𝑖̂ = 𝑝𝑖 /𝐸𝑖 . This function
correlates 𝑁 particles among 𝑀 ones using a customized function 𝑓𝑁 to characterize their
                                                                                                                         39
                               Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


spatial distributions, and it has good properties such as infrared and collinear safety. This
induces the energy correlation functions (ECFs)[72, 73] by choosing
                                                ∏          𝛽
                                 𝑓𝑁 (Δ𝑅𝑖𝑗 ) =           Δ𝑅𝑖𝑗 ,                               (3.3)
                                                 1≤𝑖<𝑗≤𝑀

where Δ𝑅𝑖𝑗 is again used to describe the distance on the 2D plane spanned by the pseudo-
rapidity and azimuthal angle (𝜂-𝜙 plane) to adapt the hadron collider’s configuration. ECF
variables facilitate tagging the 𝑁-prong structure within the jet. ECFs can be generalized to
choosing only the smallest 𝑚 values of Δ𝑅𝑖𝑗 to make the product in Eq. (3.3), instead of using
all 𝑀(𝑀 − 1)/2 values. For example, a widely used 2-prong jet tagger in CMS 𝑁2 , i.e. a ratio
constructed from generalized ECFs using 𝑚 = 2 and 1, is written as
                 ∑            𝑝T,𝑎 𝑝T,𝑏 𝑝T,𝑐
                   1≤𝑎<𝑏<𝑐≤𝑀                 min{Δ𝑅𝑎𝑏 Δ𝑅𝑎𝑐 , Δ𝑅𝑎𝑏 Δ𝑅𝑏𝑐 , Δ𝑅𝑎𝑐 Δ𝑅𝑏𝑐 }

                                                                                     .       (3.4)
                                    ( 1≤𝑎<𝑏≤𝑀 𝑝2            Δ𝑅𝑎𝑏 )
                                                        T,𝐽

The distributions of 𝑁2 on 𝑊 /𝑍/𝐻 jets and QCD jets are shown in Fig. 3.2 on the right
plot. The series of generalized ECFs and their ratios constructed like 𝑁2 can be exploited in
a multivariate approach to yield better discrimination. Besides, another extension of ECFs is
to define the energy flow polynomials (EFPs)[74] , where the used Δ𝑅𝑖𝑗 for constructing the
product in Eq. (3.3) is selected from the edge of a given graph 𝐺. The ECFs then correspond
to the special case defined on the fully connected graph. Advances in defining rule-based
algorithms to probe the jet energy distribution are still rapidly growing.


3.1.3 Heavy-flavour jet properties

     Identifying heavy flavour jets, i.e. those initiated from a 𝑏 or 𝑐 quark, is a common practice
for small-𝑅 jet tagging. A 𝑏 or 𝑐 quark produced in the hard-scattering process will form a 𝑏- or
𝑐-hadron, and they will subsequently decay via the weak interaction to the light hadrons (pions
or kaons), potentially along with the production of a nonprompt lepton with low 𝑝T (known as
the soft leptons). This results in a relatively longer lifetime for these hadrons, so their decay
produces tracks which are displaced from the PV. Also, an SV can result from those displaced
tracks. Figure 3.3 illustrates the key features of typical heavy flavour jets.
     Efforts in CMS heavy-flavour jet tagging rely heavily on the track displacement, the ex-
istence of soft leptons, and the SV features. Below is a brief overview of these approaches.
First, algorithms such as jet probability (JP) and jet 𝑏 probability (JBP) for AK4 tagging have
used the impact parameter significance of a track to reconstruct a track probability[75] . This
                                                 40
                              Chapter 3   An Overview on Algorithms


                                                      displaced
                                                        tracks        charged
                             jet                                       lepton


                                                                  heavy-flavour
                                                       SV              jet



                                                 IP
                                          PV
                jet


Figure 3.3 Illustration of a heavy-flavour jet initiated from the decay of a 𝑏 or 𝑐 hadron re-
sulting in tracks that are displaced with respect to the primary vertex (PV), and possibly re-
constructed SV within the jet[75] .


track property will differentiate tracks decayed from heavy-flavour hadrons with light-flavour
ones, and it is used to construct a jet probability for 𝑏 or 𝑐 jet tagging. Besides, the existence
of soft leptons has been used as a requirement to select jets from QCD multijet events with
less light jet contamination, aiding the calibration of AK4 𝑏 or 𝑐 taggers in CMS[75-77] . In
the CMS experiment, due to the high efficiency and accuracy in identifying final state muons,
dedicated HLT paths are designed to select muon-enriched AK4 or AK8 jets[78] . The features
of SV are also good discriminants to select 𝑏 jets. These include the corrected SV mass, the
number of tracks included in SV, and the SV’s azimuthal relation to the jet. In the CMS jet tag-
ging practice, these criteria are often combined in a multivariate approach thanks to machine
learning techniques. These techniques to identify jets initiated from a single 𝑏 or 𝑐 hadron are
also extendable to tag large-𝑅 jets, such as jets initiated from the 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 or 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signature.



3.2 Shallow machine-learning algorithms
     As many hand-crafted variables can be used to reflect some aspects of jets, multivari-
ate analyses can be performed to combine the benefits from all variables. This approach is
referred to as “shallow machine-learning algorithms” in this dissertation and the method has
been widely adopted in the CMS experiment to determine the strongest tagger. For example,
early top tagger exploits all 𝑚SD , 𝜏32 and 𝑏-tagging properties to enhance the distinguishing
ability for top jets versus other jets[70] . The series of 𝑁-subjettiness ratios and generalized EFP
                                                41
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


ratios have also been exploited together to construct a boosted design tree (BDT), achieving
superior results compared to every single one.
    In addition to these developments, the variables can be constructed in a neural network
approach using a few-layer MLP. Using a neural network also allows for multiclass classifi-
cation, paving the way towards a unified tagger capable of identifying several types of jets.
Such algorithms include the boosted event shape tagger (BEST)[70] that is able to classify all
𝑊 /𝑍/𝐻/𝑡 jets and QCD jets. The input to the MLP algorithm includes the jet kinematic vari-
ables and 𝑚SD , the 𝑏-tagging properties, the invariant masses for the different pairing of its
subjets, and other high-level jet features such as sphericity and trust. The variables are calcu-
lated several times by Lorentz-transform the jets into the rest frame in assumption that the jet
mass is configured to different resonant mass.



3.3 Deep learning algorithms
    The methodologies for jet tagging have been revolutionized in the age of deep learning.
Because a DNN can proceed directly with the input as a low-level representation of the jet, there
is less need to construct hand-crafted variables. Instead, one can directly input the underlying
low-level variables and design an efficient network to extract useful features. It is worth noting
that these methods are beyond the early MLP-based deep learning approach, which works like
a multivariate analysis. Instead, they exploit the larger, deeper network with an architectural
upgrade to proceed directly with the hierarchical particle-format input within a jet.

3.3.1 Jet representation of images and sequences
    The initial work of applying modern DNN architectures in jet tagging utilizes networks
like CNNs and RNNs. The initiative to use CNN and proceed with the jet as an image is
rooted in the fact that one fundamental form of the raw jet input is the calorimeter energy
deposit, which has a grid-like structure. For the use of RNN, there is an analogue that jets can
be viewed as sentences where the QCD is the “grammar” of the language. Early work adopts
RNN to deliver jet tagging to attempt to understand the jet’s grammatical structure.
    For the CNN architecture, in addition to representing a jet on the 2D 𝜂-𝜙 grid, it can also
be presented on the 1D sequence of the particles, usually ordered inversely by 𝑝T , such that the
convolutional filter acts on a particle its neighbouring particle (with similar 𝑝T ) that follows
the predefined order.
    A variety of studies has been presented using CNNs[79-82] or RNNs[83, 84] for jet tagging.
                                                42
                                                                     Chapter 3               An Overview on Algorithms


Experimentally, applications of the CNN via the image-like representation include the Im-
ageTop tagger[70, 82] developed in CMS. As briefly demonstrated in Fig. 3.4, this approach
defines customized pixels to “rasterize” the particles to extract the top-jet properties. At the
same time, it also includes a second neural network path to embed the features related to the
𝑏-tagging of subjets. The typical CNN developed under the particle’s 1D representation in
CMS is the DeepAK8 algorithm[70] . The DeepDoubleX algorithm[85, 86] developed in CMS
combines CNN and the gated recurrent unit (GRU, a type of RNN layer) and achieves an ex-
cellent performance among methods under these representations. A schematic architectural
outline can be found in Fig. 3.5.
                            top                                                   Inputs
              CMS Simulation                                                     6x37x37
                                                                                PFIDColors   128x37x37       64x36x36
                                                                                                                             64x18x18     64x17x17   64x17x17

3.3.2 Jet representation of sets and graphs
              It has been recognized that using images or sequences to represent jets had inherent defi-
ciencies regarding how the data is presented. For instance, jet images are sparse, i.e. many grid
points remain unfilled by jet particles, making image representation less efficient. In addition,
                                                                                                             43
                               Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


when using RNNs or CNNs based on 1D sequential representations to process jets, a major
issue is that a predefined sequence must order them. This suggests that unlike text structured
by specific grammar, the particles representing a jet are inherently permutation symmetric and
do not require a dedicated sequence to confer meaning.
     This insight led to the representation of jets through sets (equivalently referred to as point
clouds) or graphs. This approach eliminates redundant data and adheres to the permutation
invariance. Early notable works include the Particle Flow Network (PFN)[87] . It first performs
elementwise MLP embedding to all particles, and then pooling on the particle’s dimension,
followed by a final further MLP embedding. This is an early attempt to respect permutation
symmetry, yet it succeeds on the quark–gluon tagging benchmark. Meanwhile, as GNNs (typ-
ically full-connected GNNs) have become mature in deep learning and naturally provide a per-
mutation invariant way to process jets, their applications to jet tagging have seen a boost (e.g.
Refs.[88, 89] ). ParticleNet[90] is among those that bring jet tagging to the next performance level,
and it also witnesses a large success when applied in the CMS experiment for the 𝑊 /𝑍/𝐻/𝑡
tagging and for heavy-resonant 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 and 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagging (technical details will be revis-
ited in Section 5.1). Based on the successful Dynamic Graph CNN (DGCNN) developed for
processing point clouds[91] , ParticleNet dynamically identifies and connects each particle to its
𝑘 nearest neighbours in 𝜂–𝜙 space, extracting local features efficiently. Figure 3.6 provides a
detailed explanation of the ParticleNet architectural design.
     Studies at this stage highlight the importance of choosing representations close to the
data for designing efficient and performative networks. Following ParticleNet, graph-based
networks like LundNet[92] , ABCNet[93] , LorentzNet[94] , HMPNet[95] etc. further iterated on
this approach, yielding improvements or similar performance on the jet tagging benchmarks.
They prompt reflection on the key to these effective strategies for jet tagging with the modern
DNN approach.




                                                 44
                   Chapter 3      An Overview on Algorithms




                                                              build “edges” by finding k-
                Point cloud                                   nearest neighbours of each
                representation of jet                         particle, and gather features
                                                              from them




Figure 3.6   A detailed explanation of the ParticleNet architectural design.




                                        45
                      Chapter 4   New Advances in Deep Learning Algorithms




Chapter 4
New Advances in Deep Learning Algorithms
    Following the discussion above, the community for jet tagging using DNNs has widely
recognized that sets (point clouds) or graphs are more effective for representing particle-format
data. This chapter will highlight new insights in deep learning algorithms developed for jet
tagging, after this phase of progress. The topic will be brought up from two aspects, the con-
sideration of symmetry preservation within the network design, and the advances driven by
the new architecture, Transformers.


4.1 Algorithms preserving Lorentz symmetry
4.1.1 Preserving symmetries
    It is commonly understood that designing networks to adhere to the intrinsic symmetries
within data acts as an inductive bias, and the principle works as follows. For data in any domain,
it may have an inherent property that its attribute can remain unchanged if it undergoes a certain
type of transformation. This is regarded as an inherent symmetry that the data possesses.
If the network is designed so that its neuron activations, or even its overall output, remain
unchanged when the input data are transformed in this manner, it becomes more robust to
these transformations and thus aligns with the properties of the data. This provides specific
knowledge to the network algorithm, which can often improve the network’s performance.
An example from early deep learning experiences is the notable success of CNN for image
recognition. The success can be attributed to the symmetry preservation property, i.e. the
CNNs are robust to variations in images, such as shifts and distortions of some elements within
the image, due to their unique property of convolutional filter design.
    From this viewpoint, the permutational symmetry of particles within jet data can be rec-
ognized as the fundamental one in particle-format data. This leads to a aforementioned change
in how to represent a jet, i.e. from an ordered list towards an unordered set.
    It has also been recognized that a jet’s property should remain invariance when the entire
jet undergoes a boost along the beam direction, i.e. the 𝑧 axis (denoted as 𝑧–𝑡 boost), or a rota-
tion around that axis (denoted as 𝑥–𝑦 rotation). There is an inherent aspect of hadron colliders
                                               47
                               Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


that the physics properties of an event should remain unchanged when all postcollision parti-
cles undergo 𝑧-axis boost and 𝑥–𝑦 rotation. Therefore, many early algorithms (e.g. Refs.[79, 80] )
have already brought in a data preprocessing step to undergo these two transformations into a
reference direction. Viewing a jet on the 𝜂–𝜙 plain, this procedure means to shift the input jet
towards the centre of the plane.


     The works based on jet image representation consider another preprocessing that includes
an additional rotation on the jet image[79, 80] , or equivalently, on the 𝜂–𝜙 plane. Studies based
on set (point cloud) or graph representation that emerged in recent years also consider retaining
invariance under dedicated jet transformations via special network designs. These includes
preserving symmetries under boosts on 𝑧 axis or rotation on the 𝑥–𝑦 plane[96] , rotation on the
𝜂–𝜙 plain[97] (or similarly, around the jet axis[98] ), boost along the jet axis[98] , or even under
full Lorentz transformations[94, 99] . The experiment in each work reveals some gain when such
symmetry is incorporated.


     It can be easily recognized that all these transformations belong to the general Lorentz
transformations; hence, the symmetry group that this network is designed to adhere to is a
subgroup of the Lorentz group. Considering a general way to describe the symmetries, Fig. 4.1
provides a basis for how additional symmetries on top of the invariant under 𝑧–𝑡 boost and 𝑥–𝑦
rotation can be classified into four types. Note that, as the 2 degrees of freedom are frozen
out of 6, there are 4 additional degrees of freedom to Lorentz transform the jet, and they are
the 𝑦–𝑧 rotation, 𝑥–𝑡 boost, 𝑧-tilt, and 𝑦-tilt. The images in Fig. 4.1 represent all constituents
within the jet on the 𝜂–𝜙 space, with the marker area proportional to the constituent’s 𝑝T . It is
also worth noting that the latter two are a mixture of 𝑧–𝑡 boost with 𝑥–𝑧 rotation and a mixture
of 𝑦–𝑡 boost with 𝑦–𝑧 rotation—the 𝑧–𝑡 boost and 𝑥–𝑧 rotation are not commutable, similar to
𝑦–𝑡 boost with 𝑦–𝑧 rotation, hence a convention is made first to deliver the boost, followed by
the rotation in the following context.


     There are several interesting relations one can find with the basis presented in Fig. 4.1 and
the symmetries studied in the previous works[94, 96-99] . First, in the relativistic limit,
                                                    𝑝
                                       𝜂 = arctanh 𝑧 ,
                                                     𝐸
                                                   𝑝𝑦                                         (4.1)
                                      𝜙 = arctan .
                                                   𝑝𝑥

Figure 4.1 Illustration of a toy jet on the 𝜂 –𝜙 plane (a) and its behaviour when it undergoes
the four types of Lorentz transformation with its centre fixed at the origin of the 𝜂 –𝜙 plane,
which is 𝑦–𝑧 rotation (b), 𝑥–𝑡 boost (c), 𝑧 tilt (d), and 𝑦 tilt (e).



In the limit of 𝑝𝑦 ∼ 𝑜(𝐸) and 𝑝𝑧 ∼ 𝑜(𝐸) (i.e., 𝑝𝑦,𝑧 ≪ 𝐸). Define rapidities

                                                  𝑦𝑦 = arctanh   ,

                                                              𝑝
                                                  𝑦𝑧 = arctanh 𝑧 ,
                                                               𝐸
they should satisfy 𝑦𝑦,𝑧 ∼ 𝑜(1). It is easy to see that
                                                    𝑝𝑧
                                                 𝜂=    + 𝑜(𝑦𝑧 ),
                                                    𝐸                                           (4.3)
                                                    𝑝𝑦
                                                 𝜙=    + 𝑜(𝑦𝑦 , 𝑦𝑧 ),
                                                    𝐸
and this proves that 𝜂–𝜙 rotation is essentially an approximate 𝑦–𝑧 rotation, which is illustrated
also in Fig. 4.1 (b). The approximation holds better when the jet constitutes are concentrated
within a small cone. In this regard, given that the jet axis should always point to the 𝑥 axis (or
its centre should point to (0, 0) on the 𝜂–𝜙 space), a Lorentz boost on the jet axis essentially
corresponds to a 𝑥–𝑡 boost, illustrated in Fig. 4.1 (c). On top of these two transformations,
there are an additional two indicated in (d, e).

     This discussion provides a general framework to quantify which subsymmetries previ-
                                                          49
                               Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


ous networks have adhered to. This implies that adhering to the full Lorentz symmetry in the
network has the most potential for achieving high performance. Therefore, it inspires the com-
munity to design a systematic study to understand the effect of preserving these symmetries in
the network. Section 4.1.2 first showcases the design of LorentzNet, a specific high-performing
network design that incorporates full Lorentz symmetry. Section 4.1.3 will then introduce a
systematic study on Lorentz symmetry preservation, where one can see how the performance
evolves when the network progressively incorporates more types of the four subsymmetries.

4.1.2 LorentzNet
     LorentzNet is a recent GNN-based network with built-in Lorentz symmetry preservation.
It closely follows the prototype of GNNs, i.e. each layer has its node features and edge featuers
with message-passing performed between them. In particular, the graph for LorentzNet is
fully connected. The difference is that, to preserve full Lorentz symmetries, the node features
belong to either Lorentz scalars or Lorentz vectors, while the edge features are pure Lorentz
scalars. The massage-passing neural network functions in Eq. (2.1) are designed to respect the
invariant or equivariant properties of a scalar and vector, respectively.

The Lorentz group equivariant block

     The unit block of LorentzNet is the Lorentz group equivariant block (LEGB). It proceeds
with three steps, namely, the feature updates of the (1) edge features, (2) the vector node fea-
tures, and (3) the scalar node features. The procedures are outlined in Fig. 4.2 and highlighted
in three individual parts.
     Specifically, for the 𝑙-th layer, denoting the edge features connecting nodes 𝑖 and 𝑗 as 𝑚𝑙𝑖𝑗 ,
the vector features as 𝑥𝑙𝑖 , and the node features as ℎ𝑙𝑖 , the update procedures are as follow.
    1. The first step in Fig. 4.2 aims to update 𝑚𝑖𝑗 as

                              𝑚𝑙𝑖𝑗 = 𝜙𝑒 (ℎ𝑙𝑖 , ℎ𝑙𝑗 , 𝜓((𝑥𝑙𝑖 − 𝑥𝑙𝑗 )2 ), 𝜓(𝑥𝑙𝑖 ⋅ 𝑥𝑙𝑗 )),        (4.4)

       where 𝜙𝑒 (⋅) represents a neural network function and 𝜓(⋅) = sgn(⋅) log(| ⋅ | + 1) serves
       for feature standardization to ease the training. To maintain that 𝑚𝑖𝑗 is a scalar, the
       last two input arguments of 𝜙𝑒 (⋅) should also be scalars, and they are constructed from
       Minkowski dot product 𝑥𝑙𝑖 ⋅ 𝑥𝑙𝑗 and (𝑥𝑙𝑖 − 𝑥𝑙𝑗 )2 using the vectors.
    2. The second step to update the vector 𝑥𝑖 writes
                                                 ∑
                                 𝑥𝑙+1
                                   𝑖  =  𝑥 𝑙
                                           𝑖 + 𝑐      𝜙𝑥 (𝑚𝑙𝑖𝑗 ) ⋅ 𝑥𝑙𝑗 ,                       (4.5)
                                                         𝑗∈𝒩 (𝑖)

                                                    50
                        Chapter 4   New Advances in Deep Learning Algorithms


       where 𝜙𝑥 (⋅) is a neural network function, 𝒩 (𝑖) is the set of neighbours of the node 𝑖 (i.e.
       the full nodes in the case of a fully connected graph). This step is a pooling step that
       gathers the features of neighbouring nodes or the edge connecting them to the node 𝑖.
       To maintain the Lorentz vector property, the output vector can be constructed only with
       the weighted sum of all input vectors, where weights can be learned from the scalar
       features 𝑚𝑖𝑗 .
   3. The third step to update the scalar ℎ𝑖 writes
                                                                ∑
                                    ℎ𝑙+1
                                     𝑖   = ℎ𝑙𝑖 + 𝜙ℎ (ℎ𝑙𝑖 ,              𝑤𝑖𝑗 𝑚𝑙𝑖𝑗 ),           (4.6)
                                                             𝑗∈𝒩 (𝑖)

       where 𝜙ℎ (⋅) again models a neural network function, and the weight 𝑤𝑖𝑗 is modelled by
       𝑤𝑖𝑗 = 𝜙𝑚 (𝑚𝑖𝑗 ) ∈ [0, 1]. This is again a pooling step, but the output is guaranteed to be
       a scalar.
    One can see that the edge features are constructed immediately to facilitate updating the
node features, and they are not passed through different LGEBs.

LorentzNet, highlighted by three steps. Image adapted from Ref. .


LorentzNet implementation

    The LorentzNet model is constructed by stacking 𝐿 = 6 LGEBs to convert the initial node
features (ℎ0 , 𝑥0 ) to (ℎ𝐿 , 𝑥𝐿 ). Before proceeding to the first LEGB, the scalar is embedded
                                                      51
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


through a single-layer MLP, denoted as Linear(scalar_dim, 72), where scalar_dim is the
input scalar dimension. Within each LEGB, the 𝜙ℎ , 𝜙𝑒 , 𝜙ℎ is implemented as

             Linear(72, 72) → ReLU → BatchNorm1D(72) → Linear(72, 72),                      (4.7)

and 𝜙𝑚 is implemented as Linear(72, 72) → SigMoid, using the pseudocode covention of
PYTORCH. After passing 𝐿 LEGBs, the fully-connected MLP is implemented as

                    Linear(72, 72) → ReLU → Linear(72, output_dim),                         (4.8)

where a dropout rate of 0.2 is adopted, and the final output logits are in the dimension of
output_dim.

Experiments

LorentzNet on Top and QGfull dataset The performance of LorentzNet is benchmarked
on the Top and QG datasets (see the definition of datasets in Section 2.3), with details in
Ref.[94] . Initial scalar and vector node features are constructed as follows. The four-momenta
of particles in both datasets are the initial vector features. For the scalar node features, the
PID information for the QG dataset is used, implemented through the one-hot encoding, hence
falling to the QGfull scenario when using this information. In the Top dataset, the zero vector
is used due to the lack of scalar variables presented.
    The LorentzNet training on the Top and QGfull datasets adopt the ADAMW optimizer,
with a weight decay of 0.01 and a target to minimize the binary cross-entropy loss. It is trained
with 35 epochs with an initial learning rate (LR) 1 × 10−3 , and the COSINEANNEALINGWARM-
RESTARTS[100] scheduler equipped with a linear warm-up at the beginning and exponential
decay at the end (detailed can be found in Ref.[94] ).
    The jet tagging performance on the Top and QG dataset in terms of the classification ac-
curacy, the area under receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve (abbreviated as AUC),
and background rejection rate at certain signal efficiency working points are shown in Ta-
bles 4.5 and 4.6, also included with performance from other models. It can be found that
LorenzNet achieves superior performance, surpassing ParticleNet by a large margin. In addi-
tion, as LorentzNet is built with full Lorentz symmetry preservation, Fig. 4.3 studies the impact
of tagging performance when the input jets undergoing a Lorentz boost on 𝑥 axis (showcased
in Fig. 4.1 (c)) with the parameter 𝛽 = 𝑣/𝑐. It shows the performance remains constant as
expected, while the algorithms for comparison, including ParticleNet, manifest a performance
drop.
                                                52
                          Chapter 4       New Advances in Deep Learning Algorithms


                              1.0

                              0.9

                              0.8
                   Accuracy
                              0.7         LGN
                                          P-CNN
                                          PFN
                              0.6         ParticleNet
                                          ResNeXt

                              0.5         LorentzNet

                                                              = vc
Figure 4.3 Comparison of jet tagging performance in terms of tagging accuracy with different
algorithms when input jets undergo a 𝑥–𝑡 boost with parameter 𝛽 = 𝑣/𝑐 [94] .


LorentzNet on JetClass dataset With the recent development of the JETCLASS dataset,
LorentzNet has also been benchmarked on this dataset to compare with other algorithms, espe-
cially ParticleNet and Particle Transformer (detailed in Section 4.2). The input variables differ
from the implementation version in Top and QG datasets as introduced in Ref.[94] . It is ob-
served that supplementing the scalar features with the full list of inputs shown in Table 2.1 does
not impact the LorentzNet performance, although this breaks the perfect Lorentz equivariance
property. This is understandable because, in practice, it is unnecessary to strictly require the
network to adhere to the Lorentz symmetry to achieve high performance. In contrast, it works
fine to hint at the Lorentz-symmetric properties by providing an inductive bias to the network.
This supplementation of inputs allows one to synchronize the features used to compare the
ParticleNet, LorentzNet, and ParT network performance on JETCLASS and adapt LorentzNet to
the JETCLASS dataset where the nonscalar track trajectory features are present.
    The LorentzNet training on the JETCLASS dataset is synchronized with the training con-
figuration of ParticleNet[90] and ParT[65] . Specifically, The training is performed on the 100 M
dataset for 50 epochs, each defined as iterating over 10 M samples. The training adopts the
LOOKAHEAD optimizer[101] with 𝑘 = 6 and 𝛼 = 0.5 and the inner optimizer is RADAM[102]
with 𝛽1 = 0.95, 𝛽2 = 0.999, and 𝜖 = 10−5 . The batch size of 256 and initial LR of 2 × 10−3 is
adopted. The LR starts to decay exponentially when finishing 70% of the epochs, changing at
the beginning of every epochs and dropping to 1% of its initial rate in the last epoch.
    The performance on LorentzNet can be found in Table 4.7. Again, a substantial perfor-
                                                         53
                                        Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


mance gain can be found compared with ParticleNet. Further discussions will be provided in
Section 4.2.




4.1.3 A systematic study in preserving Lorentz symmetry

      The success of LorentzNet implies the importance of preserving the Lorentz symmetry
within a high-performant network to achieve even higher performance. Meanwhile, an up-
graded version of ParticleNet, named ParticleNeXt, indicates that incorporating the pairwise
particle invariant masses can bring a visible improvement. Given that these pairwise masses
span a basis to construct all Lorentz scalars available for each jet, it leads one to suspect that
Lorentz symmetry preservation is the underlying fact that helps boost the network performance.
This motivates a systematic study on Lorentz symmetry preservation.

      The design of the study adopts the paradigm as follows. First, a baseline network is
adopted without emphasizing the Lorentz symmetries or the subsymmetries corresponding
to the four transformations in Fig. 4.1. Building upon this baseline, the study focuses on only a
specific part of the network, a “subnetwork”, which is invariant under specific types of Lorentz
subsymmetries or full symmetry. This paradigm should cover both means to preserve sym-
metries as introduced above, either using symmetry-invariant input or realizing the invariance
by introducing decimated network modules. The outcome is consistent: a part of the network,
along with all its neurons, remains invariant under some types of transformations 1 .

      Three baseline networks are considered in this study: the PFN, ParticleNet, and a “weak-
ened” LorentzNet model named LorentzNetbase . LorentzNetbase supplements the scalar fea-
tures by full feature list as mentioned above while modifying the Lorentz-invariant edge fea-
tures (in Eq. (4.4)) as
                                                 𝑚̃ 𝑙𝑖𝑗 = 𝜙𝑒 (ℎ𝑙𝑖 , ℎ𝑙𝑗 , 𝑃 ),                                            (4.9)

where 𝑃 is the placeholder to insert either 𝜓(𝑥𝑙𝑖 ⋅ 𝑥𝑙𝑗 ) to preserve full Lorentz symmetry, or
other variables preserving certain subsymmetries.

      These baselines are incorporated with two network-modifying recipes to incorporate dif-
ferent types of symmetries, which are detailed below.


1   Note that, for the latter case, it is possible to incorporate symmetric design using an equivariant module. The studies that
    embed Lorentz equivariance include, e.g. Refs.[99, 103] , but they are often specialized in designs and hard to generalize to
    a broad paradigm. This is not discussed in this section.

                                                               54
                         Chapter 4   New Advances in Deep Learning Algorithms

Table 4.1 Invariance property of the pairwise variables between particles when the jet un-
dergoes a certain type of Lorentz transformation.


                                                 𝑦–𝑧 rotation    𝑥–𝑡 boost       𝑧 tilt        𝑦 tilt
 Pairwise variable      𝑧–𝑡 boost 𝑥–𝑦 rotation
                                                 (𝑦𝑦,𝑧 ∼ 𝑜(1)) (𝑦𝑦,𝑧 ∼ 𝑜(1)) (𝑦𝑦,𝑧 ∼ 𝑜(1)) (𝑦𝑦,𝑧 ∼ 𝑜(1))
 𝑚2𝑖𝑗                      ✓           ✓              ✓             ✓             ✓              ✓
 Δ𝑅𝑖𝑗                      ✓           ✓              ✓
 Δ𝑅𝑖𝑗 (𝑝T,𝑖 + 𝑝T,𝑗 )       ✓           ✓              ✓             ✓
 𝐸𝑖𝑗 (ablation study)                  ✓              ✓




Incorporating pairwise features

     The first study incorporates different pairwise features into ParticleNet or LorentzNetbase .
For pairwise features, four of them are considered that preserve certain symmetry or subsym-
metries, as detailed in Table 4.1. They are elaborated below.

    1. Pairwise mass. 𝑚𝑖𝑗 = √𝑝𝑖 ⋅ 𝑝𝑗 is known to be invariant under full Lorentz transforma-
       tions.
    2. Pairwise Δ𝑅. Δ𝑅𝑖𝑗 = √(Δ𝜂𝑖𝑗 )2 + (Δ𝜙𝑖𝑗 )2 is the angular separation of particles in 𝜂–𝜙
       space. Considering the Lorentz transformations defined in the basis in Fig. 4.1, one sees
        that it remains invariant under 𝜂–𝜙 rotation, which means that Δ𝑅𝑖𝑗 is approximately
        invariant under 𝑦–𝑧 rotation when the jet direction points to the 𝑥 axis.
    3. Pairwise 𝑝T -weighted Δ𝑅. Δ𝑅𝑖𝑗 (𝑝T,𝑖 + 𝑝T,𝑗 ) is constructed on the basis of Δ𝑅𝑖𝑗 , which
        preserves one more type of symmetry approximately, under the transformation of 𝑥–𝑡
        boost. From Figs. 4.1 (a) and (c), one sees that an 𝑥–𝑡 boost in the positive direction
        reduces the distance between particles while raising the 𝑝T . It can be proven that they
        are in inverse proportion in the limit of 𝑦𝑦,𝑧 ∼ 𝑜(1) for all particles.
    4. Pairwise energy. To design the ablation experiment, the pairwise energy variables
        𝐸𝑖𝑗 = 𝐸𝑖 + 𝐸𝑗 are chosen that, in general, violate the Lorentz symmetry. They do not
        obey the two basic symmetries, i.e., the 𝑧–𝑡 boost and the 𝑥–𝑦 rotation.

     These pairwise variables are inserted into the ParticleNet and LorentzNetbase baselines
using the following network-modifying recipes.

    1. Modifying ParticleNet. The EdgeConv[91] layers at the heart of ParticleNet include an
        intrinsic message-passing mechanism for each node to communicate with their 𝑘 near-
        est neighbouring particles. Specifically, for each node 𝑖 carrying features x𝑖 , consider
                                                   55
                                             Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


       its neighboring nodes 𝑖𝑗 (𝑗 = 1, ..., 𝑘), the message (x𝑖1 , ..., x𝑖𝑘 ) is passed to target node
       𝑖. The designed pairwise features between node 𝑖 and 𝑖𝑗 can, therefore, be incorpo-
       rated along with existing message-passing. First, the 𝑁(𝑁 − 1)/2 pairwise features are
       calculated, and each of them is embedded with

                                      Linear(1, 64) → ReLU → BatchNorm1D(64)

                                [ →Linear(64, 64) → ReLU → BatchNorm1D(64) ]×2                                                                  (4.10)

                                  →Linear(64, 64),

       resulting to U𝑖𝑗 . The new message conveyed from neighbouring nodes can be con-
       structed by (U𝑖,𝑖1 , ..., U𝑖,𝑖𝑘 ). The feature vectors of U𝑖𝑗 are directly added to the original
       message, after embedded by an additional layer Linear(64, 64), written as

                                      x′𝑖 = x𝑖𝑗 + 𝑓Linear(64, 64) (U𝑖,𝑖𝑗 )                                   (𝑗 = 1, ⋯ , 𝑘).                    (4.11)
                                         𝑗

Experiments

    Experiments are delivered for both ParticleNet and LorentzNetbase baselines, with differ-
ent pairwise features exploited. They are performed on both Top and JETCLASS datasets, with
the training data size varying across a broad range, from 6 k to 100 M.
    The default experiment is performed with the 60 k training sample on the Top dataset.
By evaluating the performance in terms of accuracy, AUC, and background rejection rates,
shown in Table 4.2, it can be seen that incorporating variables invariant under more types of
symmetries boosts network performance. This is the trend as observed from using Δ𝑅𝑖𝑗 to
Δ𝑅𝑖𝑗 (𝑝T,𝑖 + 𝑝T,𝑗 ), and eventually to 𝑚𝑖𝑗 , when removing the effect from the fluctuation. The
trend is clearer when the training data size shrinks, as shown in Fig. 4.5 on the top plots. This
proves that incorporating the Lorentz symmetries should serve as an inductive bias to the task
because it especially helps to improve performance in data-constraint conditions, effectively
augmenting input data under the case. Figure 4.5 also shows a trend of performance saturation
when data size reaches the full size of 1.2 M. This is found to be an issue of the Top dataset
as the high-performing network all reaches near-saturated performance. Hence, studies are
also performed on the JETCLASS dataset with a more complex 10-class classification task and
larger data size, as shown in Fig. 4.5 on the bottom plot. The conclusion still holds with less
saturation found. A detailed analysis of the results can be found in Ref.[104] .

which incorporate different pairwise features. For each metric, the best-performing network
from each baseline type is highlighted in bold text.

robustness of the network with respect to the certain transformation of inputs, similar to the
study delivered in LorentzNet in Fig. 4.3. Figure 4.6 illustrates the robustness when input jets
undergo 𝑦–𝑧 rotation, 𝑥–𝑡 boost, and the 𝑧 tilt, namely the procedures depicted in Fig. 4.1 (b),
(c), and (d). The expected conclusion can be drawn, i.e. when incorporating features that are
invariant under certain symmetries, the entire network manifests robustness under the corre-
sponding transformations. Note that the modification of the network does not guarantee that it
fully adheres to that symmetry. Hence, upon its learning, the invariant properties manifest on
a “subnetwork” are somehow reflected in the entire network, and this illustrates the important
role that symmetry-preserving variables play in shaping the network’s behaviour.

types of Lorentz transformation applied to the test dataset. They correspond to the input jet
undergoing a 𝑦–𝑧 rotation with angle 𝛼𝑥 (left), an 𝑥–𝑡 boost with rapidity 𝑤𝑥 (middle), and a
𝑧-tilt transformation, i.e., a 𝑧–𝑡 boost with rapidity 𝑤𝑧 followed by a 𝑥–𝑧 rotation to restore the
jet direction (right). The baseline is chosen as ParticleNet (top) or LorentzNetbase (bottom).




Incorporating nodewise features

                To ensure that the modification fits a more generic scenario, a second network-modifying
recipe is designed to incorporate nodewise features that every set-based or graph-based jet net-
work should possess. The recipe is outlined in Fig. 4.7. The recipe reads that the nodewise
features, which are specially designed to incorporate symmetries, are calculated at the begin-
ning, embedded, and then integrated by “summation” to the latent space features layer by layer.
This works for both PFN, ParticleNet, and LorentzNetbase as the baseline: for PFN, the unit
block is Φ(𝑥)[87] , the elementwise MLP for each particle; For ParticleNet, it is the EdgeConv
                                                                                                                                   59
                                         Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


operation[90] ; for LorentzNet, it is the LGEB[94] .
     On the nodewise feature design, consider the following mass-related variable as a Lorentz
invariance. The invariant mass of a particle 𝑖 with its selected neighbours, 𝐺𝑖 , also chosen
from a Lorentz-invariant way, can be calculated as
                                                                      ∑          2
                                                 𝑚𝐺𝑖 =            (          𝑝𝑗 ) ,
                                                             √        𝑗∈𝐺𝑖                                              (4.12)
                  where 𝐺𝑖 = {𝑗 |𝑝𝑖 ⋅ 𝑝𝑗 is among the 𝑘 largest values for all 𝑗}.

As an ablation study, the sum of energies of the selected particle is calculated, resulting in
𝐸𝐺𝑖 . Table 4.3 summarizes the performance for each baseline network and its variant. It again
demonstrates that incorporating Lorentz-symmetry-preserving features improves the network
performance. Additional checks similar to the pairwise features studies are delivered and can
be found in Ref.[104] .



             Input

                                                                        Unit block                 Unit block       …

                                                             xi                               xi

             node-wise
              features


                     Element-wise embedding         Linear                           Linear


             embedded
             node-wise
                                    ui                                                                          …
              features




Figure 4.7 A schematic diagram of the universal network-modifying recipe for all baseline
networks to incorporate additional nodewise features. The modification is drawn in a red back-
ground to be distinguished from the original network structure.



     This section will end with a brief outlook. If one reviews the development of the jet tag-
ging network in a broad picture, one sees that the community progressively move from the
image or sequence representation to the recent set or graph representation. This indicates a
common sense to adhere to the jet’s permutational invariance within the network design. Sim-
ilarly, recent years have witnessed the birth of high-performing networks to incorporate jet’s
Lorentz symmetry, such as LorentzNet discussed in this dissertation or PELICAN proposed
                                                                  60
