6.4.2 Fit strategy

                     For each SF derivation point, the “pass” and “fail” region is defined by jets passing or
failing the specific tagging discriminant WP. The fit is done simultaneously for both the “pass”
and “fail” regions. As mentioned, three data-to-MC SFs, one for each flavour template, are
                                                                                                                                                                   105
                                   Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


allowed to float individually in each fit. The SFs are determined as
                          𝜖Data, 𝑖
                  SF𝑖 =              ,   𝑖 stands for flvB, flvC, and flvL template.        (6.7)
                          𝜖MC, 𝑖
The three SF𝑖 parameters are implemented as free-floating parameters, which changes the tag-
ging efficiency while keeping the total yields (pass+fail) unchanged. The SF of the targeted
flavour is constrained within the range [0.1, 10], while the other two SFs are constrained within
the range [0.5, 2] due to the instability of constraining these SFs.
    The three parameters are considered as the parameters of interest (POIs) and they are deter-
mined with other nuisance parameters that account for uncertainties in a maximum likelihood
fit. The systematic uncertainties are summarized in Section 6.4.3. In addition, since there are
81 choices of selections introduced to deliver calibration at a given discriminant WP, 𝑝T bin,
and data condition, a postprocessing procedure is necessary to summarize all 81 SFs, incorpo-
rating their variations and forming the final SF. The procedure is detailed in Section 6.4.4.

6.4.3 Systematic uncertainties
    The derivation of the SFs considers various systematic uncertainty sources. The theoret-
ical and experimental uncertainties are discussed below.

Theoretical uncertianties

    The theoretical uncertainties correct the potential mismodelling in simulation compared
with data. For the simulated QCD multijet events, the uncertainty in the fractions of 𝑏-, 𝑐-, and
light-flavour jets are considered. The proportion of heavy flavour content is largely dependent
on the phenomenological model within the generator and can potentially lead to mismodelling
of the flavour proportions. As a conservative estimate, the study introduces three uncertainty
sources accounting for the fractions of 𝑏-, 𝑐-, and light-flavour jets on the mixture of all sim-
ulated events, whereas the ±1 standard deviation corresponds to varying the yields for that
flavour by 20%.
    Besides, to account for the inaccuracy of modelling on initial- and final-state radiation
(ISR and FSR) in the parton shower, the renormalization scale of QCD emissions in the ISR
and FSR in the parton shower simulation is individually varied by 2 and 0.5.

Experimental uncertainty

    The experimental uncertainty sources that participate in the fit as individual nuisance pa-
rameters are presented below.
                                                    106
                           Chapter 6   A Novel Calibration Method: sfBDT


    • Jet energy scale: the uncertainty in the energy scale of large-𝑅 jet is considered and
       propagated to the fit template by varying with ±1 standard deviation from its nominal
       value[34, 144] .
    • Jet energy resolution: the large-𝑅 jet energies in the simulation are smeared accord-
       ing to a Gaussian function to accommodate the slightly worse resolution in data in
       the normal measurement of the SF. The uncertainty in the JER is propagated to the fit
       template by varying with ±1 standard deviation of the Gaussian function by its uncer-
       tainty[34, 144] .
    • Integrated luminosity: the integrated luminosity is varied by 1.2–2.5% in the 2016–
       2018 data-taking eras[145-147] .
    • Pileup reweighting: the uncertainty in the pileup reweighting procedure is determined
       by varying the minimum bias cross section used to produce the pileup profile away from
       the measured central value by 5%[148] .

     In addition to these uncertainties contributing to the change of fit templates, two external
uncertainty sources are considered.

     The first uncertainty accounts for the mismodelling of the sfBDT discriminant score and
the fit variable. Increased uncertainty is needed to cover the difference between data and sim-
ulated events. A “reweighting” approach is used to account for the mismodelling. The SF
derivation procedure is repeated in two additional schemes. In the first scheme, a reweighting
on sfBDT discriminant score is performed from the entire simulated event to data before any
selection on sfBDT is applied. In the second scheme, the fit variable is reweighted from simu-
lation to data before the template is split into the “pass” and “fail” regions. This results in two
additional sets of SFs. The nominal SF is assigned an external uncertainty, determined from
the maximum deviation of the central SF values in the new sets from the nominal one. This
source of uncertainty is found to be the dominant contributor to the final uncertainty.

     The second uncertainty assesses the effect of varying the sfBDT selection thresholds.
The dependence of the sfBDT selection on the calibrated SF is crucial to the validity of this
approach. Therefore, the 81 choices of selections are adopted, each resulting in an SF, and the
results are combined. Unlike the conventional combination of measured results delivered in the
same condition, the 81 SF measurements originate from different conditions, which is expected
to yield a larger uncertainty to the merged SF than that of the individual uncertainty. The next
subsection illustrates the determination of the central value and error bar of the merged results.
                                                107
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


6.4.4 Merging of individual measurements

    The 81 measured SFs are merged based on their central value and upper and lower bounds
in a statistical procedure. The new SF, along with its upper and lower bounds, is determined
by randomly selecting one SF from a set of 81 and sampling SF values from the probability
density function (PDF) defined by its central value and boundaries. This results in a new PDF
with an infinite number of samples, and the central value and the upper (lower) bounds of the
new PDF will be used to define the merged SF value and its uncertainty. Mathematically, the
cumulative distribution function (CDF) of this process can be calculated from

                                            𝑥 − 𝑐𝑖        𝑥 − 𝑐𝑖
                      𝐹SF (𝑥) = mean𝑖 [Φ(          𝜂(𝑥) +         𝜂(−𝑥))],                  (6.8)
                                             𝑒𝑖,up        𝑒𝑖,down
where 𝑥 represents the variable for the SF value, 𝑐𝑖 , 𝑒𝑖,up(down) (𝑖 = 1, ⋯ , 81) denote the cen-
tral value and the upper (lower) bound of the 81 measured SFs, and 𝜂(⋅) symbolises the step
function. The final central value 𝑐, as well as 𝑐 + 𝑒up and 𝑐 − 𝑒down are derive from the CDF at
0 and ±1 𝜎 respectively.



6.5 Calibration results and discussions
    First, to illustrate the SF measurement from a single fitting, Fig. 6.22 pre-fit and post-fit
templates for the regions passing and failing the tagger selection. The illustration takes the
measurement of ParticleNet 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 and 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant SFs as an example, delivered in
the 2018 data-taking conditions and under the tightest working point (HP). The fit displayed
in the figure corresponds to applying the middle sfBDT coastline selection in both the “pass”
and “fail” regions among the 81 choices. It can be observed that, with a tight 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 (𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐)
discriminant, the remaining events passing the tagger selection are almost entirely dominated
by the flvB (flvC) subcomponent.
    As a summary, the calibration results are derived in terms of SFs, based on seven tagging
discriminants (four for 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 taggers and three for 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 taggers), in three working points
(HP, MP, and LP), three exclusive 𝑝T bins, and four data-taking eras. Tables 6.8 and 6.9 sum-
marize the SFs for the ParticleNet-MD 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 and 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagging discriminant SFs. The
calibration results for the rest of the five 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 or 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminants are summarized in
Appendix A. These SFs are part of the published results from the CMS experiment in Ref.[124] .
    For better illustration, their SFs for ParticleNet-MD 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 and 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminants are
summarized in a plot format in Figs. 6.24 and 6.25. The derived SF is around unity with an

uncertainty of around 4–8% (10–40%) for 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 (𝑐𝑐) discriminants. No obvious discrepancy

    By comparing the derived ParticleNet-MD 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 and DeepDoubleX 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 SFs with
those presented in Refs.[113, 114, 125] derived for all detector conditions in Run 2, reasonable
agreement is observed regarding the central SFs, the SF dependence on the year of data taking,
and the level of uncertainties. This provides a good validation of the sfBDT method.
    Overall, SFs derived from the sfBDT method manifest robustness over the 252 set of
results. This is the key feature of the sfBDT method, as it has been developed as a unified
approach for deriving reliable SF across all detector conditions and tagger discriminants. It
is applicable from the early tagger discriminant to the latest ones that have the strongest dis-
crimination power between 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 (𝑐𝑐) signal and backgrounds. In the CMS experiment, this
method provides a justified and robust baseline.

Application in Higgs-Charm Coupling

                115
                   Chapter 7   Introduction to Higgs-Charm Coupling Measurement




Chapter 7

Introduction to Higgs-Charm Coupling
Measurement

7.1 Physics motivation
     Since the discovery of the Higgs boson at the ATLAS and the CMS experiment[149, 150] in
2012, the property measurement of the Higgs boson has been at the core of the LHC physics
programmes. One of the most important research targets is to validate whether the Higgs boson
couples to the fermion fields with a strength proportional to the corresponding fermion mass,
a fundamental feature of Yukawa couplings (see the theoretical background in Section 1.1.3).
By far, the ATLAS and CMS experiments have announced the observation of Higgs bosons
coupling to third-generation quarks (𝑡 and 𝑏 quarks)[151-154] , and the tau leptons[155] . Therefore,
the next milestone lies in the study of Higgs bosons coupling to the second-generation fermions,
including the 𝑐 and 𝑠 quarks and muons. The measurement of Higgs bosons coupling to muons
has proceeded rapidly, and by far, evidence has been found in the Higgs boson decaying to a
𝜇+ 𝜇− pair[156] . Figure 7.1 shows the measured reduced Higgs-coupling modifier for fermions
and weak bosons in the CMS experiment, indicating that the several Yukawa coupling strengths
have been well examined to be proportional to the fermion mass. Meanwhile, it is crucial
to push the progress in measuring the coupling of the Higgs boson to the charm quark, an
ambitious and challenging subject for the Higgs physics programmes. From the theoretical
perspectives, several BSM theories suggest that the Higgs-charm Yukawa coupling can be
significantly modified[157-160] ; hence, measuring this coupling constant and validate it with
the SM prediction is crucial to finding evidence of new physics.
     At the LHC experiments, a direct measurement of the coupling strength is done by choos-
ing a dedicated physics process, where our target interaction between quantum fields plays a
role. In processes dominated by the Higgs boson Yukawa coupling to the charm quark, one of
the most direct process is the Higgs boson decaying to a 𝑐𝑐 pair (measurements via other chan-
nels are introduced in Section 7.2). This is similar to the measurement of the Higgs-bottom
coupling done by exploiting the 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 decay process, but not to the measurement of Higgs
                                                117
                                                Peking University Doctoral Dissertation

                                                                                 35.9-137 fb-1 (13 TeV)




                         κ F vF or κ V vV

                                Ratio to SM




                                               1.5

                                                                                 particle mass (GeV)

Figure 7.1 The measurement for the reduced Higgs-coupling modifiers extracted for fermions
and weak bosons[156] .


boson and top quarks coupling because the heavy top quark mass prevents the process from
happening. In the case of 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 or 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏, the measured quantity in the analysis is the sig-
nal strength 𝜇 of the signal process, defined as the measured cross section of the target process
over its SM prediction. The measurement on 𝜇 can be converted to the measurement on the
charm Yukawa coupling strength, where the directly measured property is the coupling strength
modifier 𝜅𝑐 , defined as the ratio of the measured coupling strength over its SM prediction, i.e.
𝑦𝑐 /𝑦𝑐,SM .
     Despite the clear physics picture in delivering a 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 measurement, there are three
main challenges to searching for this process at the LHC. First, the identification of the 𝑐𝑐
final state process is difficult due to the intermediate property of jets initiated from a 𝑐 quarks
between 𝑏 quark and the light quark. Second, the 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 process final state is purely hadronic.
Hence, its search is especially challenging at the LHC as vast contamination from the QCD
multijet process exists. Third, the SM-predicted branching ratio of the 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 decay mode is
around 2.9%, which is very small (around a factor of 20 smaller than the 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏[17] ) because
the 𝑐 quark mass is much smaller compared to 𝑏 quark mass in the SM.
     These inherent challenges have induced several practical solutions to improve the 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐
measurement sensitivity. To address the second challenge, the measurement presented in this
chapter targets an associated production of the Higgs boson decaying to 𝑐𝑐 with a vector boson,
𝑊 or 𝑍, with their leptonic decay, which can be used for triggering the event and veto majority
of the QCD multijet background. This is commonly recognized as the most sensitive channel
                                                                           118
                   Chapter 7    Introduction to Higgs-Charm Coupling Measurement


to search for 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐, abbreviated as 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐, in the following text. The Feynman diagrams
that initiate the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 process at the leading order are shown in Fig. 7.2. The first challenge
implies that enhanced identification techniques, improving the selection efficiency of the 𝐻 →
𝑐𝑐 signal process while largely vetoing the QCD multijet background, can be especially helpful
to this analysis. This is where the modern deep learning method comes into play and releases
a significant potential in search of 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 at the LHC, which we will highlight in Section 7.3.




      Figure 7.2       The Feynman diagrams of the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 process at the leading order.




7.2 Overview of relevant measurements
     Both the ATLAS and CMS experiments have measured the Higgs-charm coupling via the
𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 in the early stage, using partial Run-2 data taken in 2016. The ATLAS analysis
targeted the 𝑍𝐻 (𝑍 → ℓℓ) channel, obtaining an observed (expected) upper limit on the
signal strength 𝜇 at the 95% CL of 110 (150)[161] . The CMS analysis, on the other hand,
exploited multichannels based on the leptonic decay of both 𝑊 and 𝑍 bosons, resulting in
tighter constraints on 𝜇 corresponding to an observed (expected) upper limit on 𝜇 at the 95%
CL of 70 (37)[112] .
     Recently, the analysis was delivered by ATLAS using the full Run 2 data, corresponding
to 139 fb−1 of data, exploiting multiple leptonic channels of the 𝑊 and 𝑍 decay, resulting in
an upgrade on the observed (expected) upper limit on 𝜇 at the 95% CL of 26 (31)[162] . This
corresponds to an observed (expected) constraint on the charm Yukawa coupling modifier 𝜅𝑐
at |𝜅| < 8.5 (12.4).
     The CMS experiment also explores the search for 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 in the ggF Higgs boson pro-
duction mode instead of the 𝑉𝐻 mode[114] . Taking advantage of the recent advanced large-𝑅
jet tagger to identify 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 signature, the analysis yields competing results on the upper limit
𝜇 of the ggF 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal as 47 (39) at the 95% CL, serving as an important complement to
the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 search.
                                                119
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


     Besides the conventional 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 channel, ATLAS, CMS, and early LHCb experiments
also explored the radiative decay of the Higgs boson into charmonium states such as 𝐽 /𝜓. For
the Higgs boson decay process to 𝐽 /𝜓𝛾, the branching ratio is at the 10−6 level, but its width is
dependent on 𝜅𝑐 , providing a mean to extract the constraints on the charm Yukawa coupling.
Using the partial Run-2 data collected in 2016 (corresponding to around 36 fb−1 of data), the
measurements yield an observed (expected) upper limit of the measured branching ratio over
the SM-predicted value at 95% CL as 260 (170), for the CMS measurement[163] , and around
120 (100), for the ATLAS measurement[164] .
     The 𝐻-𝑐 coupling can also be measured indirectly from the Higgs boson production dif-
ferential cross section as a function of transverse momentum[165] . Specifically, the varying 𝜅𝑏
or 𝜅𝑐 constant modifies the quark-initiated Higgs contributions (denoted 𝑞𝑞𝐻) initiated by the
𝑏 or 𝑐 quarks and also the contributions of the 𝑏 or 𝑐 quark within the loop-induced ggF process.
This allows the 𝑝T distribution of the Higgs boson to change its shape and normalization. Us-
ing the full Run-2 data and exploiting the 𝐻 → 𝛾𝛾 and 𝐻 → 𝑍𝑍 ∗ channels, ATLAS and CMS
have delivered the differential cross section measurement and set boundaries to 𝜅𝑐 [166, 167] . Us-
ing both shape and normalization of the 𝑝T spectrum as a constraint, the observed (expected)
exclusion limit of 𝜅𝑐 at 95% CL is −2.5 (−3.0) < 𝜅𝑐 < 2.3 (3.1), for the ATLAS results
using the 𝐻 → 𝛾𝛾 channel[166] , and −5.3 (−5.7) < 𝜅𝑐 < 5.2 (5.7), for the CMS results using
𝐻 → 𝑍𝑍 ∗ → 4ℓ channel[167] . Figure 7.3 shows the CMS result in the indirect simultaneous
constraints on 𝜅𝑏 and 𝜅𝑐 under such conditions.
     There are also phenomenological proposals to constrain the charm Yukawa coupling in
other channels, e.g. in the Higgs boson production associated with a single 𝑐 quark[168] , or
utilizing the asymmetry of the 𝑊 +𝐻 and 𝑊 −𝐻 production[169] . The 𝐻-𝑐 Yukawa coupling
vertex exists in the diagrams that dominate these processes, which provides a handle for mea-
surements. Experimental realizations of these proposals are future aspects to explore.



7.3 Implications of deep learning
     The main challenge of the 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 search lies in the difficulty in accurately identifying
the complex hadronic final states initiated by 𝑐 quarks from the vast background events; this
implies a complex classification task and thus enters the territory of deep learning.
     For a decade, it has been known that supervised machine learning methods are experts
at the classification problem, especially since the input features are multi-dimensional. The
techniques are often referred to as multivariate analysis approaches. However, major advances
                                               120
                  Chapter 7    Introduction to Higgs-Charm Coupling Measurement


                                    CMS                           138 fb-1 (13 TeV)
                               2                                                        7




                    κb




                                                                                            -2∆ ln L
                              1.5                                                       6

                                1                                                       5

                              0.5                                                       4

                               0
                                                                                        3

                          −0.5
                                                                                        2
                              −1
                                                                                        1
                          −1.5       Best fit   SM   95% CL       68% CL   Β (κc, κb)
                                                                                        0
                               −8 −6 −4 −2                    0     2      4   6    8
                                                                                   κc

Figure 7.3 The simultaneous fit result of 𝜅𝑏 and 𝜅𝑐 , which are indirectly constraint by the
shape and normalization of the differential Higgs boson cross section on 𝑝T [167] .


in modern deep learning include the ability to deal with data with more complexity and deeper
hierarchy. In particular, the more complex the data, the better one can proceed with the means
of DNNs. It should be surely noted that this is often accompanied by larger training data size,
more sophisticated DNN architecture and training setup, as the deep learning community has
revealed (discussed in Section 3.3), but when those advancements are incorporated, the power
of DNN in this realm is incomparable.
    Therefore, to fully utilize the power of DNN to release the potential of this classification
problem, one sets the sight on the large-𝑅 jet object as it contains more particles (∼ 3 times
more than constituents contained in a small-𝑅 jet) and the object reveal more complexity that
the neural network can explore. The jet data also reveals a hierarchical structure as it is rep-
resented by the features of its constituent particles or other associated objects. This implies
the potential of using advanced DNNs to tag the large-𝑅 jets that cluster all particles as 𝑐𝑐
hadronization products, the case when the searched Higgs boson is highly Lorentz-boosted.




                                                       121
                                   Chapter 8   Analysis Method




Chapter 8

Analysis Method

    In this chapter, we introduce the physics search of the Lorentz-boosted 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 in associa-
tion with a vector boson in the CMS experiment, using the full Run-2 data. We will specifically
highlight the development and usage of the advanced large-𝑅 jet tagger and its mass regression
model based on ParticleNet. This is the main driver of the progress of this search compared to
its earlier version and its counterpart searches.

    A streamlined introduction to the full picture of the analysis is presented as follows. In
search of 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 events where 𝑉 decays leptonically, this analysis exploits three orthogonal
categories defined by the lepton multiplicity, i.e. the 2-lepton (2L) channel for identifying 𝑍𝐻,
𝑍 → ℓℓ, the 1-lepton (1L) channel for identifying 𝑊𝐻, 𝑊 → ℓ𝜈, and the 0-lepton (0L) channel
for identifying 𝑍𝐻, 𝑍 → 𝜈 𝜈.̄ The 1L and 2L channels are further divided into the 𝑒 category
and 𝜇 category, based on the flavour of leptons. Requirement on the leptons or the missing
transverse momentum (𝑝miss
                      T ) is imposed for event triggering and for vetoing the backgrounds.
The Higgs boson is constructed by selecting the leading large-𝑅 jet with 𝑅 = 1.5 (AK15
jet). It is required to pass high ParticleNet 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant thresholds as a crucial step
to suppress the backgrounds. An event BDT is trained to mildly separate 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 and all
types of backgrounds using the event kinematical features. The BDT is designed to decouple
with any flavour or the mass property of the jet. Finally, a maximum likelihood template fit
is performed to extract the signal strength on the binned variable of regressed AK15 jet mass,
which is again trained with ParticleNet. Event control regions are defined by inverting the
BDT selection and basic event selections to provide a better background event estimate during
the fit. As a validation, the same workflow is performed to extract the 𝑉𝑍 → 𝑐𝑐 signature and
compare it with the SM predictions.

    This analysis documented in this chapter is denoted by the “merged-jet” analysis, as a
contraray to the “resolved-jet” analysis in which the Higgs boson is reconstructed by two sep-
arate AK4 jets. The two analyses are made orthogonal on their event phase spaces, and the
results are combined to set a most stringent limit on 𝜅𝑐 .
                                               123
                               Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


8.1 Data and simulated samples

     This analysis uses the proton-proton collision data at √𝑠 = 13 TeV collected from 2016
to 2018 at the LHC Run 2 period. The selected data corresponds to an integrated luminosity
of 138 fb−1 .
     In the 0L channel, data are collected with the HLTs related to 𝑝miss
                                                                     T . In 1L and 2L channels,
the HLTs requiring a single electron or a single muon are selected.
     For the signal process, the 𝑊𝐻 and quark-induced 𝑍𝐻 signal processes are generated at
QCD NLO order accuracy using the POWHEG v2[26, 27, 129, 130] event generator extended with
the multi-scale improved NLO (MiNLO) procedure[170] , and the gluon-induced 𝑍𝐻 process is
generated at LO with POWHEG v2. The production cross sections of the signal processes are
corrected as a function of 𝑝T (𝑉 ) to next-to-next-to-leading order (NNLO) QCD + NLO EW
accuracy combining the VHNNLO[171, 172] , VH@NNLO[173, 174] , and HAWK v2.0[175] generators.
     The 𝑉 +jets background samples are generated with MG v2.6.0[25] at NLO with up to two
additional partons. The top quark pair (𝑡𝑡) and single top quark production processes[132, 133, 135]
are generated to NLO accuracy with POWHEG v2. The production cross sections for the 𝑡𝑡 sam-
ples are scaled to the NNLO prediction with the next-to-next-to-leading logarithmic resum-
mation result obtained from TOP++ v2.0[131] , and the differential cross sections as a function
of top quark 𝑝T are corrected to the NNLO QCD + NLO EW prediction[176] . Diboson back-
grounds are generated at NLO with POWHEG v2 (MG v2.4.2) for the 𝑊𝑊 (𝑊𝑍 and 𝑍𝑍)
process. Production cross sections of the diboson processes are reweighted as a function of the
subleading vector boson 𝑝T to NNLO QCD + NLO EW accuracy[177] .




8.2 Jet tagging and mass regression techniques

8.2.1 The ParticleNet-MD tagger for AK15 jets

     The analysis uses a customized version of the ParticleNet-MD tagger, trained on the AK15
jets. A brief description of the ParticleNet-MD tagger development can be found in Section 5.1,
and more specific details on the sample production and input features will be the focus of this
section. It is also worth noting that the training of the GloParT model, which will be discussed
later, shares many similarities in terms of development. One can refer to Section 11.2 for more
details on the deep learning specifics.
                                                124
                                    Chapter 8   Analysis Method


Training samples and jet labels

     The training samples are simulated datasets composed of the QCD multijet process and
a dedicated diresonance production process to acquire the resonance jets, including 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐.
Samples are produced under both 2017 and 2018 detector conditions. The QCD multijet sample
is generated in exclusive 𝑝T bins in a 2 → 2 QCD process, simulated by PYTHIA v8.230. The
diresonance production is initiated by the 𝐺̃ → 𝐻𝐻 process with the masses of 𝐺 ̃ and the
                                           ̃ is a graviton defined in a BSM model; the
scalar 𝐻 varied across a wide range. Here, 𝐺
specific process is less important because our aim is to collect two large-𝑅 jets, each initiated
by a 𝐻 resonance. Specifically, 𝑚𝐺̃ is specified within 600–6000 GeV with a step size of 100
GeV, and 𝑚𝐻 is set to 15–250 GeV: within the range of 15–30 GeV, the step size is 5 GeV, and
within 30–250 GeV, the step size is 10 GeV. The produced jet initiated from 𝐻 thus spans a
wide range in 𝑝T and mass, as the 𝑝T is roughly dependent on 𝑚𝐺̃ and its mass on 𝑚𝐻 . The 𝐻
decay is processed in PYTHIA with the default two-body decay configuration. The decay modes
                                                                                 1       1
only cover 𝑏𝑏, 𝑐𝑐, 𝑠𝑠, 𝑢𝑢, and 𝑑𝑑, and their production rate is set as 1 ∶ 1 ∶   3
                                                                                     ∶   3
                                                                                             ∶ 13 .
     The large-𝑅 jets initiated from these processes are assigned a dedicated jet label used for
training. Jets from the QCD process are assigned one label exclusively from the five categories:
“bb”, “b”, “cc”, “c”, “others”, following the same truth-matching definition given in Table 6.6.
Jets from the diresonance production process are assigned one of the 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏, 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐, and 𝑋 →
𝑞𝑞 categories based on the 𝐻 decay mode and pass the requirement that both two generator-
level quarks are matched within the jet axis by Δ𝑅 < 1.5. Jets are discarded if they fail to meet
these matching criteria.



Sample reweighting

     When proceeding to the training, jets from the 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏, 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐, 𝑋 → 𝑞𝑞, and the inclusive
QCD categories are sampled according to a predetermined probability binned by 𝑝T and 𝑚SD .
These probabilities are calculated such that all the above categories result in uniform distribu-
tions in the 2D binning, and the total jet counts in the four categories are equal. This is referred
to as a “reweighting” procedure, although the weight of each selected jet is not reflected in an
actual weight value in training, but in the probability for sampling jets to form the training
batch. It guarantees that the resulting tagger discriminant is decorrelated with the 𝑝T and 𝑚SD .
In other words, a selection on the tagger discriminant will not sculpt the background event 𝑚SD
distribution.
                                                125
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


Training

    The inputs to the network include features carried on the constituent PF candidates and
the SVs that are Δ𝑅-matched to the jet. A summary of variables used for training can be found
in Table 8.1. The final 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagging discriminant is defined as
                                                            𝑝(𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐)
                  ParticleNet-MD ccvsQCD disc. =                           ,                (8.1)
                                                        𝑝(𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐) + 𝑝(QCD)
where 𝑝(QCD) is the sum of scores for five QCD subcategories.
    For the AK15 ParticleNet-MD tagger developed for this analysis, an ensemble of three
networks, each trained with the same setup but with different weight initialization, is used.
The output score is taken as the mean of the corresponding output node, leading to a ∼10–15%
improvement in the signal efficiency compared to a single training. The training specifics are
adapted from Refs.[65, 90] where a detailed description can be found later in Section 11.2 in the
training of the GloParT model.

Performance and working points

    The performance of the AK15 ParticleNet-MD tagger for identifying 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 jets gen-
erated from the SM Higgs boson production sample, using the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant defined
above, is displayed in Fig. 8.1 in terms of the ROC curve. Compared with the DeepAK15-MD
𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagging discriminant used for the early 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 analysis using 2016 Run-2 data, the
ParticleNet algorithm achieves almost a factor of 5 times reduction in misidentification rates
for 𝑉 +jets and 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 backgrounds.
    Defined on the tagger discriminant, the analysis selects three exclusive regions with dif-
ferent purity of 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 signal jets, as shown in Table 8.2.

Calibration

    The calibration of the AK15 ParticleNet-MD 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant in data is based on the
sfBDT method, which uses 𝑔 → 𝑐𝑐 jets selected from the QCD multijet events with additional
BDT selection to represent a proxy of the 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal. The early development of the method
is used[125] , where the BDT is trained to separate two classes of QCD jets, distinguished by the
parton-level variables, 𝜅𝑔 (see Section 6.3.3.1). Besides, the sfBDT selections are determined
using a constant threshold on the sfBDT discriminant score, which is explained in Section 6.3.4
as the early adopted method and described by the dashed straight line in Fig. 6.8.
    In addition, this analysis adopts exclusive working points. The derived 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discrimi-
nant SFs for analysis are summarized in Table 8.3. They are applied to the 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal and
                                               126

𝑍 → 𝑐𝑐 jets to correct their 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagging efficiencies under the given working points. The
measured SFs typically range from 0.9 to 1.3 with around 20–30% uncertainties.

     As the 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 jets is a main background that contaminates the 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal, it is also
crucial to correct the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 mistagging efficiency for 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 jets and assign uncertainties
on it. However, the sfBDT and existing calibration methods are not able to derive reliable
mistagging SFs for the 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 jet because of the extremely low fraction of events in the flvB
category surviving the tight ParticleNet-MD 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant. Therefore, the analysis
takes a conservative approach and assigns 100% uncertainty in the 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 mistagging rate.

8.2.2 Mass regression with ParticleNet

     An accurate mass reconstruction of the Higgs boson candidate leads to better search sen-
sitivity, as the signal is extracted from the reconstructed Higgs boson mass distribution. The
former 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 analysis adopts the soft-drop mass of the Higgs boson, which works effec-
tively to subtract the additional contributions from the soft QCD radiations within the large
jet cone. These contributions may come from initial state radiations or pile-up. However, the
soft-drop algorithm also often over-subtract genuine hard jet constituents that account for a
large fraction of the jet momentum, which can result in 𝑚SD ≳ 0. Moreover, the application of
the soft drop algorithm introduces a long tail at large masses (in particular for highly boosted
jets), which results in additional loss of sensitivity. These two effects are illustrated in Figs. 8.2
and 8.3 in 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 and QCD jets for various 𝑝T ranges.
     To improve the mass reconstruction resolution, a regression technique using the advanced
ParticleNet neural network architecture is adopted to estimate the generator-level resonance

mass. Unlike the ParticleNet-MD tagger, there is only one output node, and the softmax unit
is removed after the last layer to assist the single-output regression task. Specific studies were
done for the regression task, including the training samples and the training objective, as doc-
umented below.


Samples and regression target

    The signal jets in this analysis are matched to a genuine 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signature with 𝑚𝐻 at the
SM value. Although it is expected that the regression model will predict the 𝑚𝐻 value right
at 125 GeV for the signal, one cannot use pure signal jets for training the model because the
other model will only learn to regress towards 125 GeV. Therefore, the training jets should
include 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signature with 𝑚𝐻 spanning from a wide range. The same training sample
                   ̃ → 𝐻𝐻 process is exploited, where 𝐻 decays into 𝑏𝑏, 𝑐𝑐, 𝑠𝑠, 𝑢𝑢, and 𝑑𝑑,
resulting from the 𝐺
and initiate resonant large-𝑅 jet with a wide spread of resonance masses.
    It is also found important to include the QCD jets in training, as they contribute signifi-
                                               130
                                    Chapter 8   Analysis Method


cantly in the analysis search of 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐, hence their regressed mass distribution can be made
predictable if the model is also trained on their phase spaces and regress on proper values
consistent with the GEN-level resonance mass for the resonant jets. A viable solution is to
choose a continuous target for these QCD jets, such as the generated soft-drop mass 𝑚SD . This
is defined as the 𝑚SD computed using hadron-level constituents of the GEN-jet (defined in
Section 1.5.7). The following mass target is selected:

                            ⎧
                            ⎪ GEN-level resonance mass           for resonant jets,
                  𝑚target = ⎨                                                                  (8.2)
                            ⎪
                            ⎩ GEN-jet 𝑚SD                        for QCD jets.


8.2.2.1   Training

     Similar to the ParticleNet-MD tagger training, the sampling-based reweighting is applied
on the fly to yield smooth and similar distributions on 𝑝T and 𝑚SD , for QCD jets and the
resonant jets. The input variables are also the same, including features from the associated PF
candidates and matched SVs, with a full feature list provided in Table 8.1.
     The training is performed by minimizing a log-cosh loss for every batch of training. The
log-cosh loss function is defined by

                                     𝐿 = log (cosh(𝜃 ̂ − 𝜃)) .                                 (8.3)

The log-cosh loss can be viewed as a combination of L2 loss (𝐿reg,L2 = (𝜃 ̂ − 𝜃)2 ) when the
estimated 𝜃 ̂ is in the vicinity of 𝜃, and the L1 loss (𝐿reg,L1 = |𝜃 ̂ − 𝜃|) when the estimated value
is further. This can be seen from the shape of the log-cosh function in Fig. 8.4.

                                       5



                                       4



                                       3                                    log(cosh(x))
                                                                            x
                                       2                                    x2
                                                                            2


                                       1




                   -4        -2            0      2         4


Figure 8.4   Comparison of the log-cosh loss function shape with the L1 and L2 loss function
shapes.


                                                131
                                    Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


Performance

     The performance of the mass regression is shown in Figs 8.2 and 8.3 on the right plots
in 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 and QCD jets for various 𝑝T ranges. The effective mass resolution as a function of
𝑝T , for the soft-drop and the mass regression algorithms is shown in Fig. 8.5 on the left plot,
for Higgs and QCD jets. The effective mass resolution 𝜎eff (𝑚)/𝑚 is defined as the half of the
smallest interval containing 68% of the area under the curve. The mass regression substantially
improves the resolution over the full kinematic range compared to the soft drop algorithm. The
improvement is larger for 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 jets compared to 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 due to the larger missing energy
carried out from the neutrinos. It is also observed that the improvement in resolution is mild
at best for QCD jets, which is most likely due to the target mass being relatively similar to the
reconstructed soft drop mass. In Fig. 8.5 on the right plot, the efficiency of a hypothetical event
selection |𝑚 − 𝑚𝐻 | < 2𝜎eff is shown. This also shows a relative improvement, as the regressed
mass is able to recover signal events lost in the tails of the jet mass distribution after applying
the soft drop algorithm. Besides, it is interesting to see that the overall efficiency is smaller
for QCD jets. This indicates a gain both in the signal yield and in the background rejection
by using this technique. he total expected gain in sensitivity is shown in Fig. 8.6 for 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏,
𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 and 𝐻 → 𝑞𝑞 jets. In the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 analysis, the improvement in the significance is
expected to be around 20% by applying the mass regression to replace the soft-drop mass.

Calibration

    The performance of the mass regression is validated in data using a boosted semi-leptonic
𝑡𝑡 control sample (single-𝜇).The goal is to measure the regressed mass scale (JMS) and reso-
lution (JMR) on the 𝑊 → 𝑐𝑥 component of the top quark jet, that we identify as a proxy for
the 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 jet.
    Such a sample is selected by first requiring at least one large-𝑅 jet (the hadronic top can-
didate) with 𝑝T > 200 GeV. The leptonic 𝑊 , constructed by combining the momenta of the
missing energy and the muon, is required to have a 𝑝T > 150 𝐺𝑒𝑉 .
    In simulation, the template distribution of the large-𝑅 jet regressed mass is constructed
for each background process, and the 𝑡𝑡 contribution in three components:
    • Top-merged: all three quarks from the hadronic top quark decay are contained in the
       large-𝑅 jet, i.e. satisfying Δ𝑅(large-𝑅 jet, 𝑞) < 1.5.
    • 𝑊 -merged: only the two quarks from the hadronic 𝑊 boson decay are contained in
       the large-𝑅 jet, i.e., satisfying Δ𝑅(large-𝑅 jet, 𝑞) < 1.5, while the 𝑏 quark from the top
       decay is outside the jet cone.
    • Non-merged: if the large-𝑅 jet is neither top-merged nor 𝑊 -merged.
    The JMS and JMR are constructed as two parameters of interest, which contribute to the
                                                         133
                                                            Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


template change by shifting or smearing the 𝑊 peak on the regressed mass distribution. They
are extracted by performing the maximum likelihood fit, where three 𝑡𝑡 components can vary
simultaneously, for events passing and failing the low purity ParticleNet-cc tagger score. The
post-fit regressed mass distributions are shown in Fig. 8.7.
      Events / bin

Figure 8.7 The post-fit distributions and data–simulation agreement in the single-muon 𝑡𝑡
control sample as a function of leading large-𝑅 jet regressed mass after passing (top) and failing
(bottom) the ParticleNet 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagging discriminant at LP.


     The fit results are summarized in Table 8.4. While the JMS is compatible with 1 across
all datasets, the fit is able to constrain the JMR factor only to approximately 5% (i.e., to 50% of
its initial value). This implies that, to match the resolution observed in the data, the regressed
mass in simulation needs to be smeared by an additional 5%.

tained from the template fit of the 𝑊 → 𝑐𝑥 component.

                                         JMR                   0.44 ± 0.11 0.39 ± 0.11   0.48 ± 0.11




8.3 Reconstruction of objects and events
     In the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 analysis, the reconstruction of PF candidates and SVs follows the de-
scription in Section 1.5. These objects are crucial to the training of the ParticleNet tagging and
regression model.
                                                                                        134
                                    Chapter 8   Analysis Method


8.3.1 Jets

     The large-𝑅 jet collection used in this analysis is clustered from PF candidates and re-
constructed with the anti-𝑘T algorithm[33] using a distance parameter 𝑅 = 1.5. As described
in Section 1.5, the PUPPI algorithm[36] is used to correct pileup effects, and the soft drop is
applied as a grooming algorithm to acquire the soft-drop mass. The jet energy scale (JEC)
and resolution (JER) corrections derived within CMS for AK8 jets are applied to the described
AK15 jets. The AK15 jets considered in this analysis have 𝑝T > 200 GeV, |𝜂| < 2.4. These
jets are also required to satisfy the tight jet identification criteria recommended within CMS.
The AK15 jets overlapping with any loose electrons or muons, i.e., Δ𝑅(large-𝑅 jet, ℓ) < 1.5,
are discarded.
     An additional jet collection, the small-𝑅 jet collection, is used in the event selection,
clustered from PF candidates using anti-𝑘T algorithm with 𝑅 = 0.4. Jet energy and resolution
corrections supplied within the CMS group are applied. The selected jets are required to have
𝑝T > 25 GeV and to be within |𝜂| < 2.4. The small-𝑅 jets are also required to satisfy the tight
lepton-veto identification criteria and the tight pileup jet identification criteria within CMS. The
small-𝑅 jets overlapping with any loose electrons or muons, i.e., Δ𝑅(small-𝑅 jet, ℓ) < 0.4, are
discarded.


8.3.2 Leptons

     Two sets of selection criteria are used in the analysis of electrons and muons. A set of loose
criteria (“loose” leptons) is used to categorize events into the 0L, 1L, and 2L channels and also
for jet-lepton cross-cleaning. More stringent requirements (“tight” leptons) are imposed in the
1L channel to select high-purity samples of isolated leptons.
     Electron candidates are preselected by requiring 𝑝T > 20 GeV and |𝜂| < 2.5. Additional
identification criteria are imposed to suppress background. Electron candidates are identified
via a set of selection criteria based on the “MVARun2” identification method within CMS. The
Electron MVARun2 approach forms a single discriminator variable that is computed based
on multiple parameters of the electron object and provides the best separation between the
signal and backgrounds. The loose and tight electron definitions are then defined based on the
selection on the Electron MVARun2 discriminant. The loose electrons are required to pass the
working point with a signal efficiency of around 90%. For the tight electrons, a tighter working
point with a signal efficiency of around 80% is chosen.
     Muon candidates are preselected by requiring 𝑝T > 20 GeV and |𝜂| < 2.4. In addition,
                                                135
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


we require the muon candidates to pass the cut-based loose ID criterion defined within CMS
and have a relative PF-isolation smaller than 0.25. These define the loose muon selection
criteria. For the 1-lepton channel, tight muons are required to satisfy a more stringent isolation
criterion, with a relative isolation of less than 0.06, and additional requirements on the impact
parameters, |𝑑𝑥𝑦 | < 0.05 cm and |𝑑𝑧 | < 0.2 cm. In addition, the muon momentum in data and
simulation is corrected with the Rochester corrections.



8.4 Analysis selection
    A typical 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal event has a clear signature of a vector boson recoiling against
a Higgs boson. This analysis focuses on the leptonic decays of the vector boson. By selecting
events with one or more well-identified leptons or high 𝑝miss
                                                         T originating from the neutrinos, the
large QCD multijet background can be effectively suppressed. This analysis defines three anal-
ysis channels, the 2L, 1L, and 0L channels, based on the number of leptons passing the loose
lepton selection criteria. The event selection criteria are described separately for each channel
in Sections 8.4.1 to 8.4.3. Finally, the event selection criteria are summarized in Section 8.4.4,
Table 8.6.

8.4.1 The 2-lepton channel
    The 2L channel targets 𝑍𝐻 events with the 𝑍 boson decaying into a pair of electrons
or muons. Events are selected with at least two electrons or muons passing the loose lepton
selection criteria described in Section 8.3.2. The leading two leptons are required to have
opposite electric charges and are of the same flavour, and the invariant mass of the lepton pair
is compatible with the 𝑍 boson mass, with 75 < 𝑀(ℓℓ) < 105 GeV. The 𝑍 boson is thus
reconstructed from the two leading in 𝑝T leptons.
    In this boosted analysis, only events with 𝑝T (𝑍) > 150 GeV are selected. Events are
required to have at least one large-𝑅 jet with 𝑝T > 200 GeV, and the leading large-𝑅 jet
is selected as the Higgs boson candidate. In 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal events, the vector boson and
the Higgs boson are typically back-to-back with each other. Therefore, the difference in az-
imuthal angle between the reconstructed 𝑍 and 𝐻 is imposed, i.e. Δ𝜙(𝑉 , 𝐻cand ) > 2.5. To
avoid double-counting, small-𝑅 jets are removed from the event if they overlap 𝐻cand with
Δ𝑅(jet, 𝐻cand ) < 1.5. The number of small-𝑅 jets is required to be less than 3 in the 2L
channel.
    After the baseline selection, the dominant background process is 𝑍+jets, with a small
                                               136
                                   Chapter 8   Analysis Method


contribution from diboson production. The 𝑡𝑡 and single top contributions are suppressed to
negligible levels due to the requirement of 𝑝T (𝑍) > 150 GeV. The 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 process is a very
small but largely irreducible background.

    To better distinguish the 𝑉𝐻 signal process from the main backgrounds, a BDT discrim-
inant, named the “kinematic BDT”, is developed. It is designed using only the kinematics
properties of the event, i.e. properties that do not include intrinsic properties like the flavour
content and the mass. The goal of the kinematics BDT is to improve the separation between the
VH signal and the main backgrounds (i.e., 𝑍+jets in the 2L channel) while remaining largely
independent of the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant and the 𝐻cand mass. This is achieved by selecting
only kinematic input variables for the BDT training. The input variables used for training the
BDT in the 2L channel are displayed in Table 8.5, and the distribution of these variables in the
𝑉𝐻 signal and in the backgrounds are shown in Fig. 8.8.

    The kinematic BDT is trained with XGBOOST[140] on the simulated sample in the 2017
detector conditions. The 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 sample is used as the signal sample, and only the main
background, 𝑍+jets, is used as the background sample in the case of the 2L channel. To avoid
overfitting, a 10-fold cross-validation[141] is used in the development of the BDT. Hence, no
events are used in both the training and the application of a BDT model. The model is also
applied to simulated samples and data with other data-taking conditions without observing a
performance loss. The 10-fold cross-validation is not exploited here, but an ensemble using
all 10 BDT models trained is used to predict the output score, and the mean is taken. This is
found to bring a small improvement in the discrimination power.

    Figure 8.9 (top left) shows the distribution of the kinematic BDT after the baseline se-
lection for the 𝑉𝐻 signal and the backgrounds. A good separation is observed. The BDT
discriminant is used to catgeorize into SR (BDT disc. > 0.55) and CR (BDT disc. < 0.55).
To show that the kinematic BDT is largely decorrelated with the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant and
the 𝐻cand mass, Fig. 8.9 also shows the BDT distribution in the three 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagging cate-
gories.This is further validated in Fig. 8.10, showing the BDT distributions separately for the
𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal and the main background, 𝑍+jets.

    After the selection based on the kinematic BDT, events are required to pass the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐
discriminant selections. The distribution of the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant is shown in Fig. 8.11 for
signal and background events in the high BDT region. It shows powerful separation power
between 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal and 𝑍+jets background. Events are categorized into three exclusive
𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 categories. Figure 8.12 displays the distribution of the mass of the 𝐻cand for events
                                               137
                                                                                        Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


with high kinematic BDT output scores and passing the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant selections for the
three categories. Since both the kinematic BDT and the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant are designed to
be independent of the mass of the large-𝑅 jet, the peak structure of the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal and
the nature of flat mass spectra of various backgrounds remain largely unchanged. This ensures
a signal extraction via a template fit in the 𝐻cand mass.

8.4.2 The 1-lepton channel

    The 1L channel targets 𝑊𝐻 events where the 𝑊 bosons decay leptonically. Events are
selected with exactly one electron or muon passing the loose lepton definition described in
Section 8.3.2. Furthermore, the electron and muon identification criteria are tightened to im-
prove the rejection of non-prompt leptons from the QCD multijet background. The 𝑝T of the
electron (muon) is required to be above 25 (30) GeV to match the trigger selection threshold.
The leptonically decaying 𝑊 boson is reconstructed as the vector sum of the momenta of the
charged lepton and the 𝑝miss
                        T . Besides, a selection on the azimuthal angle between the charged
lepton and the missing transverse momentum is required, i.e. Δ𝜙(𝑝⃗miss
                                                                  T , ℓ) < 1.5 according to
the 𝑊𝐻 event topology.
    For the boosted-jet analysis, the reconstructed 𝑊 boson is required to satisfy 𝑝T (𝑊 ) >
150 GeV. The leading large-𝑅 jet is selected as the 𝐻cand with the same 𝑝T > 200 GeV
requirement imposed. Small-𝑅 jets overlapping with 𝐻cand are also removed from the events,
and the remaining number of small-𝑅 jets is required to be less than 2 in the 1L channel to
suppress contribution from the 𝑡𝑡 process.
    Similar to the 2L channel, a kinematic BDT is also developed to reject the main back-
grounds, which are 𝑊 +jets and 𝑡𝑡, while maintaining the independence of the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 dis-
criminant and the 𝐻cand mass. The same training setup is used, but the variables are different
from the 2L case and are defined in Table 8.5.
    Figure 8.13 shows the distribution of the kinematic BDT after the baseline selection for
the 𝑉𝐻 signal and the backgrounds. For the 1L channel, three categories are defined based
on the BDT output. Two of them are considered as SRs: BDT disc. > 0.7 as the high BDT
region, and 0.55 < BDT disc. < 0.7 as the medium BDT region. The rest of the events enter
the low BDT CR. The thresholds are initially optimized to maximize the expected upper limits
on the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal. Again, validations show that the BDT discriminant in 1L is found in
good independence with the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant and the 𝐻cand mass.
    Events selected by the kinematic BDT are then required to pass the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant
selections. The distribution of the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagger discriminant is shown in Fig. 8.14 for signal
and background events in the high BDT region. The 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagging discriminant shows
a significant separation power between 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal and 𝑊 +jets background, which is
dominated by light flavour jets. The 𝑊 +jets background can be highly suppressed by the
𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant selection. Figure 8.15 shows the distribution of the 𝐻cand mass in the
high BDT region and passing three 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant selections. Again, a good mass spectra
                                              142
                                            Chapter 8    Analysis Method


separation is observed, which guarantees a sensitive extraction of the signal via the template
fit.

made, where the 𝑉𝐻 signal is normalized to the sum of all backgrounds[113] .



8.4.3 The 0-lepton channel
       The 0L channel targets mainly 𝑍𝐻 events with the 𝑍 boson decaying into a pair of neu-
trinos, giving rise to large 𝑝miss
                              T . Besides, 𝑊𝐻 events with 𝑊 boson decaying leptonically
can also enter this channel if the charged lepton misses identification. For the 0L channel,
events are selected with no electrons or muons passing the loose lepton definition described in
Section 8.3.2. The 𝑝⃗miss
                     T    corresponds to the transverse momentum of the vector boson in this
channel. The 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 candidate is reconstructed in the same way as in the 1L and 2L chan-
nels from the leading large-𝑅 jet with 𝑝T > 200 GeV. Small-𝑅 jets overlapping with 𝐻cand
are removed from the events and the number of remaining jets is required to be less than 2 to
suppress contribution from the 𝑡𝑡 process.
       As is done in the 1L and 2L channels, a kinematic BDT is developed to reject the main
backgrounds, which include 𝑍+jets, 𝑊 +jets and 𝑡𝑡 for 0L. The same design philosophy and
training setup are followed. For signal, both the 𝑍𝐻 and the 𝑊𝐻 samples are used, weighted
by their cross sections. The input variables used for training the BDT in 0L channel are defined
in Table 8.5.
       Figure 8.16 shows the distribution of the kinematic BDT after the baseline selection for

the 𝑉𝐻 signal and the backgrounds. The separation power is slightly weaker in 0L than in
the 1L and 2L channels, because only the kinematic information of the vector boson in the
transverse plane is reconstructed. Events with BDT disc. > 0.55 go to the SR and others go to
the CR. It has also been validated that the trained BDT discriminant is largely independent of
the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant and the 𝐻cand mass.
    Events are required to pass 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant selections on top of the BDT. The dis-
tribution of the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant is shown in Fig. 8.17 for signal and background events
in the high BDT region. For 0L, The 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant shows powerful separation power
between 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal and 𝑉 +jets background, significantly suppressing the 𝑉 +light flavour
backgrounds while leaving mostly 𝑉 +heavy flavour events and 𝑡𝑡 events. Figure 8.18 displays
the 𝐻cand mass distribution in the high BDT region and in different 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagging categories.
The spectrum of the signal and background events are in expected shapes.

Table 8.5 A list of variables used in the training of the kinematic BDT for the 0L, 1L and 2L
channels.

 Variable         Description                                                               0L 1L 2L
 𝑝T (𝑉 )          vector boson transverse momentum                                          ✓   ✓   ✓
 Δ𝑅(ℓ, ℓ)         angular separation between the two leptons                                        ✓
 𝑝T (𝐻cand )      𝐻cand transverse momentum                                                 ✓   ✓   ✓
 |𝜂(𝐻cand )|      absolute value of the 𝐻cand pseudorapidity                                ✓
 Δ𝜙(𝑉 , 𝐻cand )   azimuthal angle between vector boson and 𝐻cand                            ✓   ✓   ✓
 𝑝miss
  T               missing transverse momentum                                                   ✓
 Δ𝜂(𝐻cand , ℓ)    difference in pseudorapidity between 𝐻cand and the lepton                     ✓
 Δ𝜂(𝐻cand , 𝑉 )   difference in pseudorapidity between 𝐻cand and vector boson                       ✓
 Δ𝜂(𝐻cand , j)    min. difference in pseudorapidity between 𝐻cand and small-𝑅 jets          ✓   ✓   ✓

8.5 Signal extraction
    The 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal is extracted from a binned maximum likelihood fit in the 𝐻cand mass.
This is shown in the previous 𝐻cand mass distributions, namely Figs. 8.12, 8.15, and 8.18.
The signal characterizes a peak structure around the 𝑚𝐻 , while the background is relatively
fit within the mass window. This demonstrates how improvements in the ParticleNet 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐
tagging and mass regression performance enhance the search sensitivity: the former technique
enables a significant suppression in the background events, and the latter results in better res-
olution in signal and a further suppression of the background near the 𝑚𝐻 .
    It is crucial to calibrate the simulated event yield for all signals and backgrounds. The
calibration of the 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal and 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 background jets is introduced in Section 8.2.1.
For the rest of the background events, their normalization is constrained in the CR. Overall,
the normalization is acquired from a simultaneous fit for SRs and CRs to data, and the normal-
ization factor is shared across dedicated SRs and CRs, dependent on the background types and
the channel types. The normalization factor is not shared among the data-taking years. The
subtleties, hence, lie in the way of decomposing backgrounds and the determination of SR and
CR groups to share the normalization, as introduced below.


8.5.1 Control regions

    The CRs designed in the analysis include low-BDT regions and high-𝑁j regions. The
low-BDT regions are mainly composed of 𝑉 +jets backgrounds, and the high-𝑁j regions are
only defined in 0L and 1L channels to collect a high-purity 𝑡𝑡 sample. In both types of CRs, it
is important to note that events are required to satisfy the same 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant criteria
as in the SRs. As it is believed that the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagging algorithms may be a main source to
bring simulation–data discrepancy, it is crucial to keep SRs and CRs defined under the same
𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagger selections, and the splitting of CR and SR is uncorrelated with the tagger. This
guarantees that the normalization factor is transferable from corresponding CRs to SRs.


Background decomposition

    Within each channel and data-taking year, the 𝑉 +jets background is considered as a
whole. This is because, although a tight 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagger selection results in flavour content
change within each background (e.g. the 𝑉 +𝑐/𝑐𝑐 will be dominant within 𝑉 +jets process),
the flavour content should be similar across the SR and CRs, because they are split by kinematic
BDT or 𝑁j variables which are decorrelated with any flavour information. In this case, a single
                                               148
                                                        Chapter 8      Analysis Method




                             cc-tagging discriminant
                                                       low-BDT                         high-Nj
                                                                         SR
                                                          CR                            CR (*)




                                                             kinematic BDT
                                                        (*) Not   used in 2L channel   N(jets)
        Figure 8.19      Illustration of the setup of the signal region and control regions.


type of background with several flavour components can be safely considered as a whole, with
only one normalization factor acting on it and shared across SRs and CRs. Validations have
been showcased in Section 8.4, where the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant shape does not change in the
high- or low-BDT region.
    For the 𝑡𝑡 background, the estimation relies on a high-𝑁j control region. The change in the
small-𝑅 jet multiplicity can affect the composition of different 𝑡𝑡 components, particularly, the
relative fraction of the top-matched component (i.e. all three quarks from the top quark decay
are contained in a large-𝑅 jet) and the 𝑊 -matched component (i.e. only the two quarks from
the 𝑊 boson decay are contained in the large-𝑅 jet). Hence, one cannot treat 𝑡𝑡 backgrounds
as a whole. The 𝑡𝑡 process is split into three components:
    • 𝑡𝑡 (Top): all three quarks from the hadronic top quark decay are contained in the large-𝑅
       jet, i.e., satisfying Δ𝑅(𝑞, large-𝑅 jet) ≤ 1.5.
    • 𝑡𝑡 (𝑊 ): only the two quarks from the hadronic 𝑊 boson decay are contained in the
       large-𝑅 jet, i.e., satisfying Δ𝑅(𝑞, large-𝑅 jet) ≤ 1.5, while the 𝑏 quark from the top
       decay is outside the jet cone, i.e., Δ𝑅(𝑏, large-𝑅 jet) > 1.5.
    • 𝑡𝑡 (Other): if the large-𝑅 jet is neither top-matched nor 𝑊 -matched (e.g., the large-𝑅
       jet can contain the 𝑏 quark and only one of the two quarks from the 𝑊 boson decay in
       this category).

    Figure 8.20 shows the comparison between the low- and high-𝑁j regions for the three
𝑡𝑡 components in the 0L channel, respectively, as an example. The shapes of the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐
tagging discriminant and the mass of the 𝐻cand are very similar between the low- and high-𝑁j
regions for the top- and 𝑊 -matched components. Therefore, the normalization scale factors
derived from the high-𝑁j region can be directly applied to the low-𝑁j region. For the 𝑡𝑡 (Other)
component, a larger difference is observed; thus, the normalization scale factors will not be
                                                                        149
                                                                                                  Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


shared between the low- and high-𝑁j regions for this component.

Figure 8.20 Comparison of the 𝐻cand mass shapes between events with 𝑁j < 2 and 𝑁j ≥ 2 for
the top-matched (left), 𝑊 -matched (middle) and other (right) components of the 𝑡𝑡 process in
the 0L channel.

                                            However, to increase the statistical power, the same background components are speci-
fied to share the same normalization scale factors in different lepton channels after their similar
distributions are verified. Specifically, the 𝑍+jets normalization scale factors are shared be-
tween the 0L and 2L channels for each 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagging category and data-taking year, and the
𝑊 +jets normalization scale factors are shared between the 0L and 1L channel. To handle it
more properly, an additional 20% uncertainty is assigned to account for possible differences
in the normalizations between the channels due to small differences in the event kinematics.
For each component of 𝑡𝑡, the normalization scale factor is shared between 0L and 1L channels
and also with the single top (denoted by ST) process with the same decomposition, as they
are expected to have very similar characteristics. The normalization scale factor for 𝑡𝑡 (𝑊 )
component is also applied to the diboson process with a hadronic 𝑊 decay (𝑉𝑊 ). Except
for 𝑡𝑡 (Other), the same normalization scale factor is applied to the signal region and the low-
BDT and high-𝑁j control regions, while for 𝑡𝑡 (Other) a separate normalization scale factor is
applied to the high-𝑁j due to possible difference between the high- and low-𝑁j regions.
                                            As a summary, the decomposition list of all background processes with the specific merg-
ing of components among them is shown below.
                                            • 𝑍+jets 0L+2L (with additional 20% uncertainty for 0L/2L difference),
                                            • 𝑊 +jets 0L+1L (with additional 20% uncertainty for 0L/1L difference),
                                            • 𝑡𝑡 (Top) 0L+1L, with ST (Top) 0L+1L,
                                            • 𝑡𝑡 (𝑊 ) 0L+1L, with ST (𝑊 ) 0L+1L, and 𝑉𝑊 0L+1L+2L,
                                            • 𝑡𝑡 (Other) 0L+1L (for SR and low-BDT CR), with ST (Other) 0L+1L (for SR and low-
                                                  BDT CR)

    • 𝑡𝑡 (Other) 0L+1L (for high-𝑁j CR), with ST (Other) 0L+1L (for high-𝑁j CR)



8.6 Systematic uncertainties
    In this section, various categories of systematic uncertainties that affect the analysis are
discussed. They contribute to the nuisance parameters that participate in the fit by varying the
fitted templates or the overall yields. The contributions from the theoretical and experimental
uncertainties are listed separately as follows.


Theoretical uncertainties
    • Parton distribution function (PDF): effects of the uncertainty in the PDF is assessed
       by reweighting each event according to the 100 replicas of the NNPDF set, and taking
       the standard deviation of the 100 variations, following the prescription in[178] . Only
       effects on the shape are considered, i.e. the weights are rescaled to preserve the cross
       section of each process.
    • Renormalization and factorization scales: uncertainties due to the renormalization
       scale 𝜇𝑅 and the factorization scale 𝜇𝐹 are assessed by varying the scales independently
       by a factor of 0.5 or 2. The effects of such variations are implemented as weights in the
       event generators. Only effects on the shape are considered, while the inclusive cross
       section remains unchanged.
    • Cross section of the single top and diboson processes: the single top and diboson
       backgrounds are predicted directly from simulation. An uncertainty of 15% is assigned
       for the single top process based on the CMS cross section measurements[179, 180] . For
       diboson process, a 5% uncertainty is assigned based on the CMS cross section measure-
       ments[181-183] .
    • The 𝑝T spectra of diboson processes: the simulated diboson samples used in this
       analysis are generated at NLO QCD precision. Higher order NNLO QCD + NLO
       EWK corrections are used to reweight the simulated samples. Uncertainties due to
       this reweighting are taken into account as a shape uncertainty.
    • Cross section of 𝑉𝐻 processes: uncertainties on the 𝑉𝐻 production cross section due
       to uncertainty of PDF and the choice of the renormalization and factorization scales are
       taken into account in this analysis.
    • The 𝑝T spectra of 𝑉𝐻 processes: the simulated 𝑉𝐻 samples used in this analysis are
       generated at NLO QCD precision. Higher order NLO electroweak and NNLO QCD
                                                  151
                           Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


     corrections, parametrized in 𝑝T (𝑉 ), are used to reweight the simulated samples. Uncer-
     tainties due to this reweighting are taken into account as a shape uncertainty.
   • Treatment of the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 processes: contribution of the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 processes is
     fixed to the SM expectation in this analysis, and theoretical uncertainties on the 𝑉𝐻
     production cross sections and the 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 branching fractions are taken into account.



Exeprimental uncertainties

   • Jet energy scale and jet energy resolution: two uncertainties from the jet energy scale
     are assessed by varying up and down the energy scale and resolution scale factor of each
     jet within one standard deviation. The uncertainties are treated as correlated between
     the small-𝑅 jets and the large-𝑅 jets, by varying the energy scales of the small-𝑅 jets
     and the subjets of the large-𝑅 jets simultaneously. Effects of the variation are also
     propagated to 𝑝miss
                    T .
   • The 𝑝miss
          T    unclustered energy: the uncertainty in 𝑝miss
                                                       T    related to unclustered energy is
     assessed by varying each particle type by its own resolution.
   • L1 prefiring issue: the 2016 and 2017 data are affected by the Level 1 ECAL prefiring
     issue. Therefore, the simulation samples are reweighted according to the predefined
     recipe, and uncertainties on the reweighting are propagated.
   • HEM15/16 issue: the 2018 data are partially affected by the failure of HEM15/16. In
     addition to the mitigation measures, as an general recipe, an additional uncertainties on
     the jet energy scale is assigned for 2018 by scaling down the jet energy by 20% for jets
     with −1.57 < 𝜙 < −0.87 and −2.5 < 𝜂 < −1.3, and 35% for jets with −1.57 < 𝜙 <
     −0.87 and −3.0 < 𝜂 < −2.5, and also propagating it to the 𝑝miss
                                                                T .
   • Lepton efficiency: the efficiency of the muon and electron trigger, reconstruction,
     identification and isolation requirements are determined in data using the standard tag-
     and-probe technique with 𝑍 bosons. Simulation-to-data scale factors are applied to
     correct the efficiency of the simulation. Uncertainties on the measured scale factors are
     evaluated from the statistical uncertainties of the measurement and efficiency differ-
     ences using alternative samples and selections and are propagated to the analysis.
   • MET trigger efficiency: the efficiency of the MET triggers affects the expected yields
     of signals and of background processes that are predicted directly from simulated sam-
     ples in the 0L channel. An uncertainty of 2% is estimated as in the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 analysis.
   • Pileup reweighting: effects due to the uncertainty in the distribution of the number of
                                            152
                              Chapter 8   Analysis Method


  pileup interactions are evaluated by varying the cross section used to predict the number
  of pileup interactions in simulation by 4.6% from its nominal value.
• Pileup jet identification efficiency: the efficiencies and misidentification rates of the
  pileup jet identification algorithm applied to the small-𝑅 jets are corrected by the simulation-
  to-data scale factors. Uncertainties on scale factors are propagated in this analysis.
• Size of simulated samples: uncertainties due to the limited size of the simulated sam-
  ples are taken into account via the Barlow-Beeston method[184] .
• Statistical uncertainty of control regions in data: the limited size of the control re-
  gions causes uncertainty in the normalization of the 𝑉 +jets and 𝑡𝑡 process measured
  from the control regions.
• Integrated luminosity: the uncertainty in the integrated luminosity measured by CMS
  for the 2016, 2017 and 2018 data-taking period is taken into account.
• Efficiency of 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagging with ParticleNet: efficiency of tagging a pair of 𝑐 quarks
  with the ParticleNet algorithm is measured in data. Simulation-to-data correction fac-
  tors are applied to the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signals and the 𝑉𝑍 → 𝑐𝑐 process.

Chapter 9

9.1 𝑉𝑍 → 𝑐𝑐 validation results
    The full procedure of this analysis is validated by measuring the 𝑉𝑍 production with 𝑍 →
𝑐𝑐. The event selection, including the kinematic BDT, the selection on the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagging
discriminant and the signal extraction procedure, remains unchanged. The only difference is
that, instead of treating 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 as the signal process, we treat the 𝑉𝑍 → 𝑐𝑐 process as
the signal, while 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 is treated as a background and fixed to the expectation of the SM
prediction.
    Figures 9.1–9.2 show the comparison of the expected signal and background and the ob-
served data in the CRs after the simultaneous fit of all CRs and SR, taking the data collected
in 2018 and the 1L channel as an example. The corresponding plots for the SRs are shown in
Figs. 9.3–9.4. The best-fit 𝑉𝑍 → 𝑐𝑐 signal strength is 𝜇𝑉𝑍→𝑐𝑐 = 0.87+0.25
                                                                    −0.21 , consistent with SM
expectation.
    Table 9.1 shows the expected and observed significance on 𝑉𝑍 → 𝑐𝑐 as well as the best-fit
signal strength 𝜇𝑉𝑍→𝑐𝑐 .

                                                                                                                        158
                                                                                            Chapter 9                     Analysis Results


9.2 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 results
              The extraction of the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal is performed via a binned maximum likelihood
fit on the mass of 𝐻cand with the SRs and CRs. Figures 9.5–9.6 show the comparison of the
expected signal and background and the observed data in the CRs after a simultaneous fit of all
the CRs and SRs, taking the 2018 data and the 1L channel as an example. Data from all three
years are fit simultaneously. The corresponding plots for the high-BDT and medium-BDT SRs
are shown in Figs. 9.7–9.8.

                                                       Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


       The 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal yields are scaled by the best-fit signal strength, which is measured to
be 𝜇𝑉𝐻→𝑐𝑐 = 7.2+4.4
               −4.2 . The post-fit yields for background and signal processes and the observed
data in the signal regions are summarized in Tables 9.2–9.4.
       In addition, Fig. 9.9 shows the post-fit values of the nuisance parameters and their impacts
on the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal strength. Only the first 30 nuisance parameters with the largest impacts
on the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal strength are shown. No significant excess of events is observed beyond
the expected contribution from SM processes. The observed (expected) upper limit on the
𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal strength 𝜇𝑉𝐻→𝑐𝑐 is 15 (8.5) at 95% confidence level. The results in each
category and year are summarized in Table 9.5.
