                                   Chapter 9   Analysis Results


9.3 Combined 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 results with resolved-jet analysis
     To further improve the sensitivity of the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 search, the merged-jet analysis is
statistically combined with the resolved-jet approach. To achieve orthogonality between the
two analyses, events are categorized based on whether a large-𝑅 jet satisfying the requirement
described in Section 8.3.1 is present and passing an additional 𝑝T threshold. The 𝑝T threshold
of the large-𝑅 jet has been optimized to maximize the sensitivity in 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 and 𝑉𝑍 → 𝑐𝑐
while keeping a robust and stable fit, and a 𝑝T threshold of 300 GeV is found to give the best
results.
     The correlation scheme followed for the treatment of the systematic uncertainties between
the two analyses is summarized below:
     • The theoretical uncertainties are treated as fully correlated between the resolved-jet and
       merged-jet analyses.
     • The normalization SFs for 𝑡𝑡 and 𝑉 +jets backgrounds are treated as uncorrelated be-
       tween the two analyses. This is because the two analyses probe different phase spaces.
       In addition, in the merged-jet analysis, the tagger efficiencies for 𝑉 +jets and 𝑡𝑡 are
       estimated directly from the CRs and reflected in these SFs; therefore, they cannot be
       correlated with the SFs in the resolved-jet topology.
     • Uncertainties related to the charm–tagging SF are treated as uncorrelated. The pro-
       cedure followed for the calibration of the two algorithms (DeepJet and ParticleNet) is
       significantly different and also probes a very different phase space.
     • Experimental uncertainties related to JEC, JER, 𝑝miss
                                                        T , pileup and lepton efficiencies are
       treated as correlated between the two analyses.

     The full analysis procedure in the combination scheme is first validated by measuring
the 𝑉𝑍 production with 𝑍 → 𝑐𝑐. This best-fit signal strength for 𝑉𝑍 → 𝑐𝑐 is found to be
𝜇𝑉𝑍→𝑐𝑐 = 1.01+0.23
             −0.21 , with an observed (expected) significance of 5.7 (5.9) standard deviations.
     For the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 signal process, no significant excess of events is observed beyond the ex-
pected contribution from SM processes. The observed (expected) upper limit on the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐
signal strength 𝜇𝑉𝐻→𝑐𝑐 is 14 (7.6) at 95% confidence level. The best-fit signal strength is mea-
sured to be 𝜇𝑉𝐻→𝑐𝑐 = 7.7+3.8
                        −3.5
                             . The result is interpreted in the 𝜅-framework[185] , where 𝜇𝑉𝐻→𝑐𝑐
is reparameterized in terms of the Higgs-charm Yukawa coupling modifier 𝜅𝑐 , assuming that
only the Higgs boson decay widths are modified, according to the relation:
                                                        𝜅𝑐
                           𝜇𝑉𝐻→𝑐𝑐 =                                     .                   (9.1)
                                       1 + (𝜅𝑐2   − 1) ⋅ ℬSM (𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐)
                                                  165
                                    Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


The observed 95% CL interval for 𝜅𝑐 is 1.1 < |𝜅𝑐 | < 5.5, with the expected constraint given
by |𝜅𝑐 | < 3.4.



9.4 Discussion and outlook
      The 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 analysis provides the most direct constraint to 𝜇𝑉𝐻→𝑐𝑐 and 𝜅𝑐 to data
after a combination of the merged-jet and resolved jet topologies. The observed sensitivity is
driven by the resolved-jet result, nevertheless, the expected upper limit of 𝜇𝑉𝐻→𝑐𝑐 , extracted
from simulated events, are led by the advancement in the merged-jet analysis. The improved
sensitivity in the merged-jet topology reveals the benefits of exploiting the advanced deep-
learning technology to analyse complex data. The techniques mainly involve the advancement
in DNN architecture based on ParticleNet to analyze the information contained in the large-
𝑅 jet with the large radius 𝑅 = 1.5, and it is assisted with sufficient training samples and
appropriate training configurations to make the DNN expressive.
      In addition, the ParticleNet models are accommodated with the VHcc analysis workflow
to make it valid for data analysis. This includes the tagger’s reasonable decorrelation per-
formance with the jet mass to allow signal extraction from the 𝐻cand mass spectra, using a
sampling-based reweighting as an improved mass-decorrelation technique compared to the
predecessor, the DeepAK15-MD tagger 1 . Notably, the robust and reasonable calibration of
the ParticleNet-MD 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 discriminant on the signal and background jets is also vital in
the analysis. The signal calibration is done by a novel sfBDT method, and the background is
calibrated by dedicated CRs and the shared normalization factors.
      Based on the merged-jet analysis workflow, the sensitivity of the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 search is pro-
jected to HL-LHC corresponding to the 3000 fb−1 data. From the likelihood scan on 𝜇𝑉𝐻→𝑏𝑏
and 𝜇𝑉𝐻→𝑐𝑐 , shown in Fig. 9.10, the expected best fit of 𝜇𝑉𝐻→𝑐𝑐 is roughly 1.0 ± 0.8, yielding
an expected upper limit of 𝜇𝑉𝐻→𝑐𝑐 at 95% C.L. at around 1.6. The results can be improved
with further enhancement of the DNN technique, e.g. by exploiting a more performant network
architecture, as discussed in Chapter 4. As a concrete practice within the CMS experiment,
Chapter 11 will introduce the next-generation large-𝑅 jet tagger, which improves by a factor of
1.5–2 on the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagging identification against the major backgrounds. This provides one
direction to enhance sensitivity in the merged-jet analysis. For resolved-jet analysis, it can be
a viable solution to incorporate signature identification with advanced DNNs using the dijet

1   The mass decorrelation of the DeepAK15-MD tagger is achieved by including an adversarial training approach. The
    technique is discussed in Section 5.1, the introduction of DeepAK8-MD.

                                                       166
                                              Chapter 9    Analysis Results


or full event-level information with proper analysis workflow designs. Overall, for the future
of the 𝑉𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 analysis, there is more to expect regarding improvement in the analysis tech-
niques combined with the deep learning advances, and it is surely a more charming journey
ahead.

Application Paradigm for Pre-training and
              Fine-tuning




                   169
                          Chapter 10   Perspectives on Future Jet Models




Chapter 10
Perspectives on Future Jet Models
    The rapid development of deep learning is driving significant advances in AI, sparking
a revolution in all aspects of human activity, including scientific research. This progress is
expected to become a driving force for the next industrial revolution. Over the past five years,
the particle physics community has witnessed the large success of modern deep learning in
tasks such as large-𝑅 jet tagging. In the coming decades, the basic form of research into
fundamental particle physics will still likely depend on accelerators and particle collisions
without a major change. In this regard, given the incredible speed of the AI revolution, it is
both intriguing and important to explore the potential role of AI in enhancing the processing
of data at the LHC and converting this data into meaningful physics results.
    This part of the dissertation will extend beyond the specific application of ParticleNet
in the CMS experiment and will instead explore how more advanced AI and deep learning
techniques could transform future analyses of LHC physics. In particular, it will highlight
the potential of these methods in jet tagging and their broader application in physics analyses,
focusing on the benefits of pre-training large, comprehensive models and their subsequent fine-
tuning.
    It is important to note that, at this stage of development, state-of-the-art deep learning
tools for analysis still rely heavily on modularized event processing (e.g. models developed
to analyze reconstructed jets, leptons, etc.). This is also the basis of the work documented in
this chapter. However, in the future, when AI is more advanced, one may foresee more radical
paradigm shifts in processing data, potentially moving away from the traditional reliance on
jet-based models.
    This chapter will begin with a discussion of various perspectives on the future develop-
ment of jet models.



10.1 Jet tagging problem revisited
    As new jet models subsequently surpass their predecessors in tagging performance, a
fundamental inquiry arises in the community: where do the performance boundaries of a jet
                                               171
                             Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


tagging task lie, and how do we embed the problem into a theory framework to understand the
limit? It is worth noting that, the question can be generalized for all classification problems,
e.g. the problem to classify signal and background events, which lies at the heart of almost all
LHC analyses.

    The statistical theory is the answer to the question. For each collision event recorded by
the detector (or a jet composed of reconstructed particle candidates), based on the underly-
ing information used to represent it, it can be mapped to a single point on an abstract latent
space, although the dimension can be very high. One can imagine that this is achievable if
every calorimeter cell or tracker hit is considered one dimension in that space. Then, events
in each physics process (or jets from each class) are obtained by sampling points from a prior
probability density function (PDF) on that space. If this prior PDF is known, then the best
discriminant one can build to separate two classes is simply the ratio of their PDFs, according
to the Neyman–Pearson lemma[187] . This provides a theoretical bound for the classification
performance one can reach.

    Additionally, it implies that DNNs have provided the best practical solution so far. This is
because the aim of DNN training is to manipulate a tunable and complex enough function, our
neural network, to estimate the unknown and complex PDF ratio. For example, in the binary
classification task to separate signal and background, the most optimal network, with its train-
ing loss reaching the theoretical minimum, will output probabilities exactly corresponding to
the ratios of signal and background PDFs. This estimation lies within the maximum likeli-
hood estimation problem because the network only sees limited points sampled from the PDF.
When the number of data points is huge, and the capacity of the neural network is massive,
the optimized neural network will estimate a function closer to the prior PDF ratio, touching
the upper bound. This picture, brought from the statistical view, intuitively matches the recent
advances in jet tagging, where studies show that a larger dataset size and a more expressive
and bigger network are the keys to yield better classification performance.

    More importantly, it is not hard to see that this statistical view actually has deep relations
with particle physics itself—the aforementioned prior PDF for a given physics process corre-
sponds to the differential cross sections based on all constructed dimensions. For instance,
in the example of discriminating ggF 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 events and QCD multijet events with DNN,
the estimation target is the ratio of their PDFs, which also corresponds to the ratio of their
fully differential normalized cross sections in every event phase space. This gives an under-
lying physics meaning when one delivers the DNN classification tasks in the particle physics
                                              172
                           Chapter 10   Perspectives on Future Jet Models


scenario. In contrast, in other domains, e.g. the image classification task where one trains an
optimal vision network to separate images of cats and dogs, the interests lie merely on the
classification performance metric but not in the underlying statistcal interpretation. That is to
say, less interest is put on the PDF that lives in the dimensions of pixels and colour channels
of an image, formed by the entire image class of cats or dogs.
     The above discussion introduces a way to interpret the DNN classification task applied
to particle physics and reveals its physics meaning. As an inspiration for future analyses, it
is then imaginable that, with enhanced use of deep learning tools, one should be capable of
reaching near-optimal signal and background discrimination for every analysis. Note that the
improvement can be especially sizeable for hadronic channels, which have more complex de-
tector responses. Finding a suitable experiment technique to reach the goal is a pursuit for
future analyses delivered at the LHC, and it can imply a revolution in how physicists analyze
LHC data. The development may involve various topics, such as the design of the network,
the format of input it should proceed with, its training strategy, its integration into the LHC
analyses workflow, etc. This part of the dissertation will present a first yet promising explo-
ration of these directions using the fast simulation dataset and its implementation within the
CMS experiment.



10.2 Future models
     This section explains the design philosophy of several future models from the author’s
perspective. It focuses on three aspects of the model: its expressiveness, large capacity, and
comprehensiveness. Amidst the rapid development of deep learning, experiences can be drawn
from CV and NLP when designing the future jet model.

Expressiveness
     Progress has been made when it comes to using a DNN to deal with particle-level in-
puts. Early strategies include using an MLP or nested neural network structure like CNN to
deal with particle data[79-81, 188] ; then, the community recognized that the structure for a point
cloud or a graph is closer to the essence of particle-format data and improves the expressive-
ness of the neural network[87, 88, 90] . Advanced neural network architectures involve GNNs
or Transformers[65, 88-90, 93, 105] , and will continue to lead the future direction in this domain.
These architectures respect the inherent properties of particle-format data, including their in-
variance or near-invariance to a set of symmetries, including permutation of particles, Lorentz
                                                173
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


transformations or its subsets of the particles. (details can be found in Section 4.1.1). Specif-
ically, experiences in deep learning suggest that Transformers are very suitable for building
large models as they scale well with parameter and data sizes[56, 189] , and a similar conclusion
is drawn in jet physics domain[65] .
     As these advances are made in processing particle-format data, a special note is that the
foreseeable ultimate network that processes collider data will likely handle the raw detector
information directly. The community has also witnessed the development of such models,
such as CNNs to process energy deposition in calorimeters or GNNs to handle raw hits in
trackers (taking Refs.[190, 191] as examples). Presently, the work introduced in this dissertation
still focuses on the particle-level input instead of the raw input, for it delivers the jet tagging
task where jets are clustered from the reconstructed particle—an intermediate format processed
from the raw data.

Large capacity
     The large capacity of the network is the key to achieving a closer estimate towards the prior
PDF. Recent advances in CV and NLP have witnessed rapid development in large vision models
and language models. For example, Fig. 10.1 illustrates the evolution of parameter counts in
large models over time. It can be foreseeable that the high-energy physics community will
also encounter large collider event models or large jet models. The Transformer developed in
this work has reached 10 M parameters, which is smaller than those language or vision models
by a factor of thousands, but it has reached 20 times the parameters of ParticleNet developed
in CMS. Studies presented in this dissertation reveal that the size of the present Transformer
model is compatible with the current amount of data used for training.

Comprehensiveness
     The invention of the Transformer[56] has not only made it easier to train larger models but
has also invigorated another subdomain, the “pre-training and fine-tuning” training paradigm
(such as Refs.[50, 51] in the NLP domain). This is a two-step strategy: initially, a general model
is trained on a broad task, enabling it to learn a comprehensive latent space to embed inputs;
afterwards, this model can be fine-tuned for specific tasks. Given the abundant knowledge ac-
quired during the pre-training process, this knowledge can be transferred to downstream tasks,
achieving decent performance with a minimal sample size. The work presented in this disser-
tation illustrates that pre-training and fine-tuning have a lot of untapped potential in particle
physics. They will be elaborated in the following section (Section 10.3) from the fine-tuning
                                               174
                           Chapter 10   Perspectives on Future Jet Models




Figure 10.1    The evolution of the large language model size (by 2022). Source: Hugging face.


perspective, but a few remarks are presented first on how to make the pre-trained model com-
prehensive.
     By reviewing the historical developments of model pre-training in the CV and NLP do-
main, one implication is a paradigm shift from supervised to self-supervised training[50, 51, 192] .
This is due to the nature that most image and textual data used for training is unlabelled. At
the earlier stage, the model pre-training in a supervised approach relies on the inclusiveness of
the training set and a large-scale classification task. Because the jet training data largely relies
on simulation, the presence of truth labels is a key feature. As a first approach, this work relies
on a supervised training approach. Similar to ImageNet, which contains up to 1000 different
labelled images, extending the categories to the hundreds level would be a viable approach for
the jet tagging dataset.
     It is worth noting that there are two benefits to pre-training a large jet model via the large-
scale classification approach to reach comprehensiveness. First of all, as suggested earlier, the
classification problem lies at the centre of all physics analyses. An important fact to recognise is
that a multiclass classifier can be seen as stacking multiple binary classifiers; that is, the output
probabilities can be used to construct optimal discriminants to separate any two classes (or even
a mixture of classes) from the initial hundreds of training classes. A theoretical explanation
is given in Section 11.2.1 with a detailed validation, and proof can be found in Appendix B.
This allows one to extend the usage of one model to a large number of analyses, reaching an
optimal discrimination of different types of signals and backgrounds all at once. Secondly,
                                                175
                               Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


as model-agnostic searches are gaining momentum due to the lack of new observations of a
BSM particle, models resulting from large-scale classification can be an approach to detect
potentially unseen signatures. As the model has seen a variety of simulated jets and learned
their pattern, it will ensure an easily reached generalization ability.



10.3 Fine-tuning techniques
     This section aims to explain why “pre-training and fine-tuning” paradigm has a large po-
tential in LHC experiments. An intriguing fact is that, although there have not been many
discussions on this paradigm in the LHC analyses, it has subtly integrated into the design logic
of some analyses and is poised to emerge.
     As a thought experiment, we consider an analysis using a neural network-based AK4 jet 𝑏
and 𝑐 tagger. The traditional approach involves applying a specific working point of the tagger,
but when the number of required 𝑏 and 𝑐 jets is large, selecting the tagging discriminant might
drastically reduce signal efficiency. A common practice is to employ machine learning tools
(a BDT or a neural network) to deliver a multivariate analysis, taking the tagger score of each
jet as input. This is essentially an event classifier for signal versus background discrimination.
It is intriguing to see that the paradigm is much akin to performing transfer learning on the
output score of the jet model—jet models (the jet taggers) tend to be more complex due to
the need to handle low-level input features and require larger training datasets; in contrast, the
analysis-level neural network is typically an MLP containing fewer parameters. Hence, it can
be regarded as transferring the knowledge from the more complex model to the event level.
     To expand this application scenario to a real transfer learning scene, replacing the input
tagger scores with the hidden layer neuron values read from the pre-trained model is simply
possible. It means that one can discard the last 1–2 layers of the jet model and connect the
hidden neurons directly to the subsequent neural network. This is depicted in Fig. 10.2. In
typical CMS analyses, this approach suits just well: one treats the last hidden layer neurons as
new jet features, storing them to the “analysis object data” used for delivering analyses, then
uses them as inputs for the event-level neural network training.
     The hidden layer neurons should provide a comprehensive jet representation as long as
the jet model is pre-trained comprehensively on a large variety of data. Utilizing them as
input provides powerful ability in the classification task. This is because the pre-trained model
essentially finds a way to map the abstract jet phase space comprised of features of all particles
(with varying numbers) to a fixed-dimensional phase space. This space is sufficiently versatile
                                                176
                          Chapter 10   Perspectives on Future Jet Models




       the pre-trained
                                                                                       customized scores!
         Transformer
                                                                                     (optimized for analysis)
           network

           network

            use the
                                                   retrain the last layer
         hidden layer




Figure 10.2 Illustration on the connection from the conventional analysis-level neural net-
work training strategy to the concept of transfer learning.


and has strong discrimination power over essential jet attributes, as a few simple MLP layers in
the original model can transform this latent representation into optimal discriminants capable
of distinguishing any two kinds of signatures.

    The following chapters in this dissertation will showcase the new developments following
this spirit of design, inside and outside of the CMS experiment. In Chapter 11, a pre-trained
model is developed as a large-scale classifier over hundreds of jet classes in CMS. The model
is dubbed Global Particle Transformer (GloParT). Then, in Chapter 12, two fine-tuning ap-
plication schemes within the CMS experiment are studied on GloParT using the simulated
event to illustrate its superiority compared to existing approaches. The philosophy of GloParT
training is also studied using the fast-simulation dataset fast-simulated produced via DELPHES,
as detailed in Chapter 13. This chapter introduces the JETCLASS-II dataset, along with the
“Sophon model”, which is close to the philosophy of the GloParT training dataset and the
GloParT model. The development process will be detailed, demonstrating the vast potential of
the Sophon model to boost the resonance search programmes at the LHC, which inspires the
future meaning of the GloParT model in the CMS experiment.




                                                177
                                    Chapter 11     GloParT: Model Pre-training




Chapter 11

GloParT: Model Pre-training
      Pre-training a DNN jet model that is expressive, large, and comprehensive is the key
to enable a high performant fine-tuning applications to CMS analyses. Hence, this chapter
introduces detailed specifics about model pre-training, which is performed on a large-scale
classification and regression and exploits the ParT architecture as the backbone. Due to the
comprehensive large-𝑅 phase spaces the model sees and learns, it is named the Global ParT
(or GloParT). Its development can be regarded as an all-around extension of the ParticleNet
tagger and the regression model introduced in Section 8.2.1. This chapter will illustrate the
technology being used, but will also attempt to explain the principle of such techniques in the
                     1
statistical view         .
      The training samples and details on the categorization are first introduced in the following
text. The development details are shown, highlighting several control experiments on the new
features incorporated in model pre-training that have not been studied before. The performance
on a number of metrics is provided in the last section.



11.1 Generated samples and jet labels
      The training of GloParT is a simultaneous classification and regression task. The large-
scale classification is delivered by separating 314 different jet final-state topologies. Like the
other jet tagging model, it is designed to distinguish the jets initiated from the QCD multijet
process (QCD jets) and various types of jets initiated by a resonance, which usually constitute
the signal in dedicated analyses. In order to enlarge the classes of the resonance jets, they
are expanded in three dimensions: (1) the multiprongness of the jet that is highly related to
the resonance decay process, (2) the combinations of specific particle types and flavour that
initiate each prong, and (3) the kinematics of the secondary resonance if it is valid. This results

1   Author’s note (edited November, 2024): The model presented in this dissertation is referred to as the “GloParT stage-
    2” model, which represents the second iteration of the model developed within the CMS collaboration. This model has
    undergone further refinement, and the “GloParT stage-3” model was integrated into the CMS software in November 2024.
    This updated model is expected to enhance upcoming CMS analyses using full Run 3 data, as well as joint Run 2 and Run
    3 data, in the coming years.

                                                          179
                                            Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


in the full category list composed of up to 314 classes of jets, visualized in Fig. 11.1. These
categories involve:

Figure 11.1 The 314 jet categories for multiclass classification used in the GloParT model
development.


    1. A full suite of the two-prong resonance decay process, shown in the first circle. To
        initiate these signatures, the included processes include a spin-0 scalar 𝐻 decaying to
        𝑏𝑏, 𝑐𝑐, 𝑠𝑠, 𝑏𝑠, 𝑞𝑞, 𝑔𝑔, 𝑒+ 𝑒− , 𝜇+ 𝜇 − , 𝜏 + 𝜏 − , or a charged spin-0 scalar decaying to 𝑏𝑐 and
        𝑐𝑠.
    2. The three- or four-prong resonance initiated from a resonance 𝐻 decaying to a sec-
        ondary 𝑊 -like vector-boson resonance, subsequently decaying into leptons or quarks.
        The masses of 𝐻 and 𝑊 are configured as variables, but 𝑚𝑊 /𝑚𝐻 has been set differ-
        ently in three groups, targeting signatures including SM-like 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗ (𝑚𝑊 /𝑚𝐻 is
        SM-like), the 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 case, and the 𝐻 → 𝑊 ∗ 𝑊 ∗ or 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗ case with at least
        one off-shell secondary resonance. This creates three copies of 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗ categories,
        as shown in the second circle.
    3. The three- or four-prong resonance initiated from a resonance 𝐻 decaying to a sec-
        ondary 𝑍-like vector-boson resonance. The situation is similar with the 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗
        cases, as shown in the third circle.
    4. The two- or three-prong resonance with similar topology to 𝑡 → 𝑏𝑊 decay, where the
        masses of 𝑡 and 𝑊 are configured as variables. This is summarized in the fourth circle.
                                                                      180
                                 Chapter 11   GloParT: Model Pre-training


     5. The categories for QCD jets are split into five classes based on their flavour composition,
         shown in the fifth circle.

     The generated events contain the QCD multijet events and the customized BSM events
to acquire different types of resonant jets. The QCD jets are collected from the 𝑝T -binned
QCD multijet events generated by PYTHIA. The initial ME production is a 2 → 2 QCD process,
with the 𝑝T of one object configured within the range of (350, 470), (470, 600), (600, 800),
(800, 1000), (1000, 1400), (1400, 1800), (1800, 2400), (2400, 3200), and (3200, +∞) GeV.
     For the customized BSM events that produce resonance jets, one commonality for sam-
ples is their flat control of the jet 𝑝T and mass distribution. Like the previous jet tagger designs,
identification of a certain final-state signature is required to be decoupled with the jet mass and
𝑝T such that the analysis selection on the trained tagging discriminant will not sculpt the back-
ground distributions on these basic kinematics features. To achieve this, the original resonance
is guaranteed to span over a wide range of 𝑝T and mass and yield a flat distribution. This poses
a requirement for the event production, which is described as follows.


11.1.1 Generation of two-prong resonant jets

     Events that produce resonance jets are initiated by a di-𝐻 production process, which effec-
tively doubles the jet sample size. We utilize both resonance and the nonresonance production
of the di-𝐻 process.
      For the first case, the events used for producing the scalar 𝐻 decaying to two prongs are
initiated by a diresonance process, 𝐺  ̃ → 𝐻𝐻, generated by MG at LO, which is similar to the
ParticleNet-MD tagger development described in Section 8.2.1. To refine the kinematics of
                            ̃ to control the 𝐻 scalar 𝑝T (hence the jet 𝑝T ) and the 𝐻 mass to

      For the second case, the events are produced by a nonresonance di-𝐻 production, where

thus brings three additional categories. Among them, 𝐻 ± → 𝑐𝑠 is a most useful class because
the corresponding probability score can be used to identify 𝑊 → 𝑐𝑠 signatures. This is not
included in the ParticleNet-MD tagger. For handling the generated jet 𝑝T span, we use the

      The parton showering of the event is proceeded by PYTHIA using the v8.230[28] , using the
CP5 underlying event tune[137] with the NNPDF3.1 NNLO parton distribution function (PDF)
set[138] .


11.1.2 Generation of multipronged resonant jets

      The multipronged resonance jets are composed of three different topologies. As indicated
in Fig. 11.1, they are jets similar to the topology of a 𝐻 → 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 (∗) jet, 𝐻 → 𝑍 (∗) 𝑍 (∗) jet, and
𝑡 → 𝑏𝑊 jet. It is clear that they are not exactly like jets initiated from the SM process because
all the resonance masses are reconfigured to span over a wide range; however, the decay modes
of the resonances inherit the properties of SM 𝑊 and 𝑍 bosons.


The 𝐻 → 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 (∗) jets

                                            ̃ → 𝐻𝐻 process simulated with MG. The se-
      The events are produced with the same 𝐺

retain the spin correlation of two 𝑊 bosons. Specifically, to configure the fully hadronic or
semi-leptonic decay of the di-𝑊 , the (DecayMode1, DecayMode2) is set to (5, 5) and (10, 5),
respectively. The production rates of fully hadronic over semileptonic modes are manually
set as 1 ∶ 2 to emphasise a bit more on the semileptonic jets. Under each main class of fully
hadronic and semileptonic cases, the decay into specific quarks follows the branching ratio of

    Additionally, an important aspect is to configure the mass of the secondary resonance
mass, i.e. the 𝑊 boson mass, because different configurations in 𝑚𝑊 /𝑚𝐻 result in different jet
topologies. In application, one may hope that the tagging discriminant is optimal for tagging
SM 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗ jets, but it may also be used in the BSM searches to identify a heavy reso-
nance 𝑋 decaying to on-shell 𝑊 𝑊 signatures, or a lighter resonance decaying to 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 ∗
signatures but is different from 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗ . It is found in our study that mixing all these
phase spaces in one training category actually will result in less optimal performance for all
scenarios. Therefore, two different sets of events are simulated:
    1. the “fixed 𝑚𝑊 /𝑚𝐻 ” sample, where 𝑚𝑊 /𝑚𝐻 is always fixed in the SM configuration,

     Overall, the 𝐻 → 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 (∗) jets encompass three sets of categories, which differ by their
event topology. One set targets tagging SM-like 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗ jets, and the other two aim
to tag the signature of 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 or 𝐻 → 𝑊 ∗ 𝑊 (∗) . Each category has the same fine jet
categorization based on the 𝑊 boson decay mode and the particle contents within the jet cone.
They form the 𝐻 → 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 (∗) categories in Fig. 11.1.
                                                                                184
                            Chapter 11   GloParT: Model Pre-training


The 𝐻 → 𝑍 (∗) 𝑍 (∗) jets

    Another bunch of multipronged jet phase spaces is brought by the 𝐻 → 𝑍 (∗) 𝑍 (∗) topol-
ogy, which is similar to the 𝐻 → 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 (∗) case but is complemented with additional jet
content, including more quark flavour types (e.g. 𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏) and the ℓℓ𝑞𝑞 signature resulting from
the semileptonic channel. As the 𝑍 boson has richer decay modes than 𝑊 boson when they
are accurate to the quark flavours, these jets bring a larger number of categories, as shown in
Fig. 11.1.
   The generation of events that produces 𝐻 → 𝑍 (∗) 𝑍 (∗) jets is very similar to that produces
𝐻 → 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 (∗) except for the change of particle 𝑊 to 𝑍 in the ME simulation step. The 𝐺     ̃
and 𝐻 mass adopts the same configuration, and the 𝑍 boson mass is specified the same with
                                                                              80.399
𝑊 boson configuration: for the “fixed-𝑚𝑍 /𝑚𝐻 ” sample, it adopts 𝑚𝑍 =          125
                                                                                     𝑚𝐻 ;   for the
“spanned-𝑚𝑍 /𝑚𝐻 ” sample, it is sampled uniformly from the span (0.3, 1.5) times the central
value 𝑚𝑍 defined above. In JHUGEN, the fully hadronic or semileptonic decay of the di-𝑍
is specified by setting (DecayMode1, DecayMode2) as (1, 1) and (8, 1), respectively. The rate
over the two modes is also configured as 1 ∶ 2. Still, three sets of topologies result from the
sample production, which split the full categories threefold.


The 𝑡 → 𝑏𝑊 jets

    Resonant jets close to the topologies initiated by a top quark are generated. There are
practical considerations because one can use the corresponding probability scores to construct
a discriminant that aims to veto the top-initiated backgrounds (e.g. the 𝑡𝑡 or single top quark
processes) in many analyses. However, similar to the general design sprit, the top quark and
𝑊 boson masses are specified and are all configured as variables.
    The events that produce 𝑡 → 𝑏𝑊 jets is a spin-0 resonance 𝑍 ′ decaying to 𝑡𝑡, generated by
MG. In the same configuration of the di-resonance sample production as above, the resonance
mass varies between 60 and 6000 GeV; the mass of the top quark varies between 15 to 250 GeV
in steps of 10 GeV, with 15–30 GeV in steps of 5 GeV. Also, a crucial aspect is to configure 𝑚𝑊
according to 𝑚𝑡 . Considering that our motivation for including top-initiated jet topologies is
for the purpose of vetoing the real SM top jets, the 𝑚𝑊 /𝑚𝑡 is close to the SM configuration but
is allowed to be smeared around the centre by specifying the 𝑊 boson width equal to 𝑚𝑊 . The
hadronic or leptonic decay of the 𝑊 boson is specified in the ME level. The rate for producing
hadronic over leptonic 𝑡 → 𝑏𝑊 jet is also configured 1 ∶ 2, similar to the fully hadronic over
semileptonic rates when producing 𝐻 → 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 (∗) and 𝐻 → 𝑍 (∗) 𝑍 (∗) jets.
                                              185
                                    Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


11.1.3 Samples for test
    To test the performance of the model, dedicated samples are produced that produce jets
closer to the SM property.
     For 𝐺̃ → 𝐻𝐻 events that generate two-prong resonant jets, the 𝐺
                                                                   ̃ mass is fixed at 1000 GeV,
and the scalar 𝐻 mass is fixed at 125 GeV such that the resonant jet resembles an SM Higgs
                                                                            ̃ → 𝐻𝐻 events that
jet. Only the 𝑏𝑏, 𝑐𝑐, 𝑠𝑠, and 𝑞𝑞 (𝑞 = 𝑢, 𝑑) final states are generated. For 𝐺
generate 𝐻 → 𝑊 ∗ 𝑊 (∗) jets, two sets of samples are produced, where one specifying both 𝑚𝐻
and 𝑚𝑊 as the SM value, and then another varying 𝑚𝐻 within the range of (15, 250) GeV and
keeping 𝑚𝑊 as the SM value. These two schemes are illustrated in Fig. 11.2 in the red dot and
red line. Lastly, for the 𝑍 ′ → 𝑡𝑡 process that produces 𝑡 → 𝑏𝑊 jets, the initial resonance 𝑍 ′
has its mass sampled from 1200–4500 GeV while both 𝑚𝑡 and 𝑚𝑡 are fixed at the SM value.

11.1.4 Truth labels of jets
Category labels       Resonant jets initiated from the training samples are labelled to one of the
classes in Fig. 11.1 based on the matching criteria or discarded. The matching is based on the
Δ𝑅 distance between the GEN-level quarks or leptons to the jet axis. For large-𝑅 jets clustered
with the radius 𝑅 = 0.8, a matched GEN-level particle requires Δ𝑅 < 0.8. The quarks and
leptons are the direct particles produced by the resonance, and they are read from the PYTHIA
list. The situation is different from the tau leptons. If it decays hadronically, then one matches
the original tau leptons to the jet just like other particles; if it decays leptonically, then one
instead uses its decayed 𝑒 or 𝜇 daughter to deliver matching. The neutrinos are excluded from
the GEN-level particles because they are invisible to the detector. For a better illustration,
Fig. 11.4 shows four examples from the 𝐻 → 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 (∗) jets that are matched to one category.


    Jets labelled as 4q            Jets labelled as 3q          Jets labelled as ℓqq       Jets labelled as τℓqq
        (e.g. csqq)                     (e.g. csq)               (e.g. csev, csτhv)             (e.g. csτev)
                          q                                                      q                            q
   H                      q̄   H                         q̄ H                    q̄    H                      q̄
                          q                              q                       ℓ
                                                                                                  (τℓ ν)      ℓ
                          q̄                             q̄

Figure 11.4 A illustration of the matching criteria from the GEN-level quarks or leptons to
the jet axis to define the category. The subplots show four possible final states resulting from
𝐻 → 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 (∗) jets.


    For the QCD jets, it is exclusively assigned to the five categories by performing a ghost
                                                          186
                                 Chapter 11    GloParT: Model Pre-training


matching from the heavy-flavour 𝐵 or 𝐷 hadrons to the jet and counting the numbers of the
matched hadrons. This follows the same definition as in Table 6.6.



GEN-level masses         In addition to the category labels assigned to the jet, two GEN-level
masses of a jet are defined, used as the regression target. For resonant jets, these are the
“GEN-level resonance mass” and the “GEN-level visible-particle mass”.
    The first one is defined as the mass of the resonance (𝐻 or 𝑡) that initiates the jet; in
contrast, the second one is defined as the invariant mass of its composed GEN-level quarks or
leptons (excluding neutrinos) that is Δ𝑅-matched to the jet. Again, for the special tau lepton
case, we consider its subsequent visible decay products to exclude all neutrinos. Here are a
few examples to illustrate the rules.
    • for the 𝐻 → 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 (∗) → 𝑒𝜈𝑒 𝑐𝑠 class, the selected visible particles are: 𝑒, 𝑐, 𝑠;
    • for the 𝐻 → 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 (∗) → 𝜏𝑒 𝜈𝜏 𝑐𝑠 class, where 𝜏𝑒 decays to 𝑒 + 2𝜈, the selected visible
       particles are: 𝑒, 𝑐, 𝑠;
    • for the 𝐻 → 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 (∗) → 𝜏h 𝜈𝜏 𝑐𝑠 class, if 𝜏h decays to 𝜋 ± + 2𝜋 0 + 𝜈, then the selected
       visible particles are: 𝜋 ± , 𝜋 0 , 𝜋 0 , 𝑐, 𝑠.
    For QCD jets, the mass labels collected from the matched GEN-jet are assigned to ensure
they have roughly consistent regression target definitions. As a correspondence to GEN-level
resonance mass, the soft-drop mass of the matched GEN-jet with neutrinos included in the
GEN-jet clustering is adopted; as for GEN-level visible mass, we use the soft-drop mass of
the matched GEN-jet clustered without including the neutrinos. To sum up, the mass labels
assigned to jets are

                     ⎧
                     ⎪ GEN-level resonance mass                          for resonant jets,
     Mass label #1 = ⎨                                                                        (11.2)
                     ⎪
                     ⎩ GEN-jet (clustered including 𝜈) 𝑚SD               for QCD jets.

                     ⎧
                     ⎪ GEN-level visible particle mass                   for resonant jets,
     Mass label #2 = ⎨                                                                        (11.3)
                     ⎪
                     ⎩ GEN-jet (clustered excluding 𝜈) 𝑚SD               for QCD jets.



11.2 Development details
    This section introduces details of developing a pre-trained jet model, including the class
definition, training objective, input data format, input preprocessing, and training details.
                                                        187
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


11.2.1 Training objective

     The GloParT model outputs 314 classification nodes and 2 regression nodes. The train-
ing objective is to minimize a hybrid classification and regression loss function. We provide
the details of the objective constructions below, and, more importantly, we will highlight an
interpretation of the statistical meaning given the loss function definition.




Classification part

     For the classification part, the multiclass cross-entropy loss is used by convention. To be
specific, we consider the class 𝑐 ∈ {1, ⋯ , 𝐶} (𝐶 = 314) which has one-to-one correspondence
to a model’s output logit 𝑧𝑐 ∈ ℝ. For an input jet represented as 𝑥, the label 𝑦𝑐 ∈ {0, 1}
indicates whether the jet belongs to class 𝑐. The loss function is written as
                              ∑              exp(𝑧𝑐 )            ∑
                    𝐿cls = −      𝑦𝑐 log ∑                    =−   𝑦𝑐 log 𝑦𝑐̂ .            (11.4)
                                            𝑐 ′ exp(𝑧𝑐 ′ ,𝑖 )
                               𝑐                                 𝑐

Here,
                                         exp(𝑧𝑐 )
                               𝑦𝑐̂ = ∑                  = softmax(𝑧𝑐 ).                    (11.5)
                                         𝑐 ′ exp(𝑧𝑐 ′ )

The number is considered the probability score of each jet 𝑥 classified as the class 𝑐, and it
         ∑
satisfies 𝑖 𝑦𝑐̂ = 1.

     From the statistical point of view, jets from each class 𝑐 are sampled from a PDF denoted
by 𝜌𝑐 (𝑥). It can be proven that (see details in Appendix B), in the limit of infinite data point,
the solution that minimizes 𝐿cls yield
                                                    𝜌𝑐 (𝑥)
                                      𝑦𝑐̂ (𝑥) = ∑                .                         (11.6)
                                                    𝑐 ′ 𝜌𝑐 ′ (𝑥)

Therefore, the target of the multiclass neural network is to estimate the ratio of all PDFs. An
illustrative explanation is provided in Fig. 11.5, which takes a binary classifier as an example.
Note that although training multiclass classifiers has become conventional in particle experi-
ments, the statistical interpretation has often been overlooked by the community in recent years.
It is essential to recall the mathematical foundation here because it is the key to the physics
analyses in defining the optimal discriminant when the multiclass classifier is used. Specifi-
cally, this work introduces a large-scale classifier model and guides how to use the hundreds
of probability scores.

                                            Chapter 11          GloParT: Model Pre-training

Figure 11.5 Illustration on the fact that each class of jets corresponds to a PDF in an abstract
jet phase space Ω, and that a binary or multiclass classifier network trained with the cross-
entropy loss is learned to estimate the a normalized PDF ratio.




                                                                        189
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


Regression part

     Similar to the mass regression model introduced in Section 8.2.2, the regression still
adopts a log-cosh loss, which can be recognized as an L2 loss at the vicinity of the target
value and an L1 loss when at a distance. The regression process can also be integrated from a
statistical view which can be found in Appendix B.
     The final objective is to minimize the loss function 𝐿tot which can be written as

                                 𝐿tot = 𝐿cls + 𝜆(𝐿reg1 + 𝐿reg2 ).                           (11.7)

The hyperparameter 𝜆 is tuned and adjusted to 5. As there is no entanglement between the
terms, the optimal case when the hybrid loss reaches the minimum should result in both optimal
performances in classification and regression.


11.2.2 Model architecture

     The GloParT model adopts the ParT architecture, introduced in Section 4.2, as the back-
bone. ParT is a Transformer model that currently achieves state-of-the-art performance on the
100 M JETCLASS dataset. The network proceeds input particles as a permutational-invariant set
and updates its features by a stack of multihead self-attention blocks. The pairwise attention in
each block is calculated from the particle-level features and the pairwise features between the
particles. Two adjustments are made compared to the original architecture, including handling
multiple data groups and scaling the model size.
     First, the CMS GloParT training involves three types of data, the charged PF candidates,
the neutral PF candidates, and the SV. The split of PF candidates is due to the significant differ-
ence in their feature space, e.g. the neutral candidates lack features related to the track impact
parameters or the pixel hits. Therefore, separate embedding of their feature space is a viable
solution. The split has been adopted by several small-𝑅 and large-𝑅 jet tagging algorithms in
teh CMS experiments, such DeepJet[193] , DeepDoubleX[85, 86] , and Robust ParT AK4[194] .
     Second, three Transformer sizes are studied. We modified the primary decoder dimension
of the Transformer, setting it to either double or half, which roughly changes the number of
parameters in the model by a factor of 4 or 1/4, which we refer to as ParT-L (large) and ParT-
lite. The original size is referred to as ParT-B (base). The parameter settings for the three sizes
of ParT models are listed below. The ParT-L is the final choice for the GloParT model, and a
summary of its parameters is presented in Table 11.1. For a clear illustration, the ParT model
architecture in the ParT-L size annotated with its main parameters is depicted in Fig. 11.6.
                                               190
                                                                                                                Chapter 11                           GloParT: Model Pre-training

11.2.3 Input

     The input features of the GloParT are summarized in Table 11.2. The features include
those from the constituent PF candidates and the SVs that are Δ𝑅-matched with the jet, simi-
lar to the ParticleNet training in CMS. The difference is that PF candidates are split into charged
and neutral ones, and for charged candidates, additional features, including pixel hit patterns
in multiple CMS tracker layers, are added. Also, the lost tracks that do not pass the PF algo-
rithm are added back to the charged candidates. A boolean variable (isLostTrack) is used to
distinguish if the object is a lost track.
     To illustrate the complexity difference for PF candidates and SVs across the categories,
Fig. 11.7 shows the distributions of the length of charged, neutral PF candidates and SVs.
Typical jet categories are shown, including the merged QCD classes, the merged hadronic
𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗ classes, the 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 class, the merged hadronic 𝑡 → 𝑏𝑊 classes, and the merged
hadronic 𝐻 → 𝑍𝑍 ∗ classes, are shown. It can be concluded that multipronged jets generally
have larger PF candidates, and jets originating in 𝑏-enriched final states have larger SVs.
     To provide a full visualization of each input jet, Fig. 11.8 shows the point cloud visualiza-
tion that plots all constituent PF candidates and matched SVs on the 𝜂–𝜙 plane. In the plottings,
each PF candidate or SV is displayed as a marker with its area proportional to the energy. The
circles, triangles (upward- or downward-directed), and pentagons are in the green/blue colour,
representing the hadrons, leptons (electrons or muons), and photons in the merged list of PF
candidates and lost tracks. The solid (hollow) markers stand for electrically charged (neutral)
particles. A marker with a brown edge indicates it is a lost track. The orange/red squares corre-
spond to the SVs. The colour reflects the impact parameter, where a larger displacement with
the primary vertex results in more blue (for PF candidates) or more red (for SVs). The category
of the plotted jet, its 𝑝T , its GEN-level resonance and visible-particle mass for a resonant jet
are displayed in the figure. The visualization reveals the prongness, track displacements, and
appearance patterns of various types of PF candidates, and SVs.


11.2.4 Preprocessing

     Jets are required to pass preselections of 200 < 𝑝T < 2500 GeV, |𝜂| < 2.4, and 20 <
𝑚SD < 260 GeV. Similar to ParticleNet-MD development in CMS discussed in Section 8.2.1,
the mass decorrelation is achieved by sampling-based reweighting, where “reweighting” stands
for sampling jets with a predefined probability to form the training batch on the fly. The sam-
pling probability is binned on jet 𝑝T and 𝑚SD . The fine classes are merged to a level to form
                                                 192

11.2.5 Training setup
      The training is performed using the WEAVER framework, a research and development plat-
form tailored for high-energy physics applications, powered by the PYTORCH backend. A key
advantage is its ability to dynamically sample inputs according to predetermined probabilities
and its data loader’s capability to handle large-scale data in ROOT format.
      The training is performed on 3 Nvidia GTX 3090 graphics cards, using the data distributed
parallel (DDP) mechanism to distribute the workload across multiple GPUs. The training batch
size, LR and number of epochs are indicated in Table 11.1. Each epoch is defined by iterating
over a fixed number of 7.68 M jets for training, using the first 80% of the dataset, then iterating
over 0.512 M jets for validation, using the last 20% of the dataset. The iterator keeps looping
over the entire dataset used for training and validation. A snapshot of the model is saved at the
                                                                                       196
                              Chapter 11   GloParT: Model Pre-training


end of each epoch. The model snapshot showing the lowest loss on the validation dataset is
selected for the final evaluation. The training adopts the LOOKAHEAD optimizer[101] with 𝑘 = 6
and 𝛼 = 0.5 and the inner optimizer is RADAM[102] with 𝛽1 = 0.95, 𝛽2 = 0.999, and 𝜖 = 10−5 .
The LR, set according to Table 11.1, remains constant for the first 70% of the iterations, and
then decays exponentially, changing at the beginning of every following epoch, down to 1% of
the initial value at the end of the training.
     Figure 11.10 shows the training and validation metrics for the three sizes of the ParT
model. Performance leap from ParT-lite (orange) to ParT-B (blue), and then to ParT-L (red)
can be seen. By comparing training and validation curves, both ParT-lite and ParT-B training
maintain similar trends, but for ParT-L one can see that the performance on the validation
dataset noticeably lags behind at the later stages of training. This indicates a potential upper
limit of the model size that the current data size can accommodate.



11.3 Overall performance
     Although the physics goal for GloParT development is to pre-train the model on compre-
hensive data such that it learns a latent jet representation with excellent generalization ability,
The nature of the training task ensures it is suitable for direct classification and regression
purposes, simply using its output nodes.


11.3.1 Classification performance

     For classification, it has been shown in Eq. (11.6) that the optimal multiclass classifier
learns a probability score for each class directly corresponding to the PDF ratio. Thus, taking
the scores for class 𝐴 and 𝐵, namely 𝑝𝐴 and 𝑝𝐵 , and using the notion in Eq. (11.6) for PDFs,
one has
                                                𝑝𝐴 (𝑥)          𝜌𝐴 (𝑥)
                       𝐴 vs. 𝐵 disc. ∶=                   =                                 (11.8)
                                           𝑝𝐴 (𝑥) + 𝑝𝐵 (𝑥) 𝜌𝐴 (𝑥) + 𝜌𝐵 (𝑥)
for every input 𝑥, which is the optimal discriminant defined to distinguish class 𝐴 from class
𝐵. This implies that no matter how many classes one adds to train the classifier, the optimal
discriminant for class 𝐴 and 𝐵 can be constructed the same way, and it is irrelevant to all other
classes. This is a good property of a fully optimized neural network classifier trained with the
multiclass cross-entropy loss we can make use of. Figure 11.11 gives a visual illustration of this
aspect. Practically, this has been successfully validated in the jet tagging experiment—with
adequate input data size and large enough capacity of the model, such as the GloParT model,
                                                197
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation

(blue), and ParT-L (red) model, including the classification accuracy (top), the classification
loss (middle), and the regression loss (bottom), for each epoch of the training (left) and evalu-
ation (right) procedure.




                                               198
                                   Chapter 11     GloParT: Model Pre-training


the training can result in nearly identical separating power for given two classes regardless of
additional classes added in training.

model should represent an estimate of 𝜌𝐴 (𝑥)/(𝜌𝐴 (𝑥)+𝜌𝐵 (𝑥)) regardless of additional categories
included in the training.


      Therefore, despite that there are 314 classes in GloParT, one constructs 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 and 𝑋 →
𝑐𝑐 discriminants by

                                                                     𝑝(𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏)
                           GloParT bbvsQCD disc. =                    ,
                                                   𝑝(𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏) + 𝑝(QCD)                                         (11.9)
                                                       𝑝(𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐)
                           GloParT ccvsQCD disc. =                    ,
                                                   𝑝(𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐) + 𝑝(QCD)

which only exploits the output nodes corresponding to the 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏, 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐, and QCD
classes 1 .
      Figures 11.12–11.14 compare the performance of GloParT with ParticleNet-MD regard-
ing the discrimination ability among 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏, 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 and QCD jets, in terms of ROC curves.
The horizontal line indicates the signal efficiency 𝜖𝑆 when background efficiency 𝜖𝐵 = 0.01.
The 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 and 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 jets are originated from the SM Higgs boson and collected from the
test data. For both GloParT and ParticleNet-MD, the discriminant used to distinguish 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐
from 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 jet is defined as 𝑝(𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐)/(𝑝(𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐) + 𝑝(𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏)). It is clear that GloParT
reaches superior results, with QCD background rejection improved by a factor of approxi-
mately 1.5 in terms of 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 and 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagging, and around 2 times 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 background

1   Note that, compared with Eq. (5.2), the notion is changed from 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 (𝑐𝑐) to 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 (𝑐𝑐) but they are in the same
    meaning.

                                                         199
                                                                         Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


rejection against 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 jets. This demonstrates that expanding the training categories on
a large scale is viable. At the same time, the discrimination ability for certain classes is not
diminished, making it a very promising option for the future of the tagging model.

Figure 11.16 The mass decorrelation performance of the 𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐 tagging discriminant for the

yses in the boosted topology. For two-prong final states, it covers novel flavour contents such
as 𝑏𝑐, 𝑏𝑠. These signatures can be induced from a resonance predicted by many BSM models,
such as 2HDM. The inclusion of 𝑐𝑠 final state also provides a unique handle to tag SM 𝑊
jets in a mass-decorrelated manner, which is not covered in existing mass-decorrelated tag-
gers. For multipronged final states, a straightforward usage is to exploit scores corresponding
to 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗ → 4𝑞 /ℓ𝜈𝑞𝑞 final states that can be used to identify the boosted Higgs boson
in the 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗ decay mode. The score corresponding to the top-like categories can be
put to the denominator when constructing the discriminants, providing a solution to the veto
𝑡𝑡 background. Appropriate calibration of these discriminants will be an important and open
project for future study.


11.3.2 Regression performance

     The performance of large-𝑅 jet mass reconstruction can be studied utilizing the regression
node of GloParT. Figures 11.17–11.18 show the mass regression performance for reconstruct-
ing the Higgs boson mass in the SM 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏 and 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 jets. Both the regression nodes
with GEN-level resonance mass and visible-particle mass as targets are shown and compared.
Also compared is the ParticleNet regressed mass, a standard tool for CMS analyses, devel-
oped with the same strategy as explained in Section 8.2.2 but for AK8 jets. Results show that
GloParT regressed mass with GEN-level visible-particle mass target performs slightly better
                                                                                        202
                                           Chapter 11              GloParT: Model Pre-training


than the ParticleNet benchmark, while the other GloParT regressed mass reveals a displaced
peak structure with worsened resolution.

    To explain the performance, one can draw from statistical theory (see Appendix B) that the
regression performance cannot be maintained when additional classes of jets are introduced,
                                                                              203
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


because the theoretically predicted optimal regression value is dependent on all regression tar-
gets from all classes. This explains the rightward shift in the left plots of Figs. 11.17 and 11.18
because the additional multipronged jets, with their regression target relatively larger than the
real jet mass due to the missing neutrino effect, may affect the overall optimal regression re-
sult. The problem is alleviated when using the GEN-level visible-particle mass as a regression
target.




11.4 Ablation experiments

     There are several key features of the GloParT model that one would expect to see the exact
influence on the model performance when they are brought in. This section focuses on three
aspects: the impact on certain tagging performance when the number of classes increases, the
effect of introducing classing splitting within the 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗ major category according to the
mass configuration of 𝑚𝐻 and 𝑚𝑊 , and the improvement brought by enlarging the scale of the
Transformer.



11.4.1 Increasing catgetories

     The GloParT model covers major categories, including the 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗ categories, the
2-prong resonance categories, and the top-jet categories. Figure 11.19 shows the dedicated
tagging performances for distinguishing three 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗ classes against QCD jets compared
over three models, the one with only 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗ and QCD categories included, and the other
two sequentially adding 2-prong categories and top categories. The baseline model is based on
the ParticleNet architecture developed at an earlier stage and is denoted as “cat. study baseline”
in the figure, and the other two models are annotated as “+2-prong” and “+2-prong +top”. For
the tagging performance study for class signal 𝐴 versus background 𝐵, the tagging discrimi-
nant is defined according to Eq. (11.8) such that the scores on the numerator and denominator
depend on the 𝐴 and 𝐵 classes. Also, the signal and background jets used for making the ROC
curve correspond exactly to the 𝐴 and 𝐵 classes. It is evident that the inclusion of additional,
irrelevant classes has a minor impact on the tagging performance in our studied case. This
provides a major source of confidence for upgrading the model towards a large-scale classifier.

                                                                                                                            205
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


11.4.2 Class splitting via kinematics

    A major feature of GloParT is its splitting of 𝐻 → 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 (∗) categories into three sets with
a kinematics difference in the 𝑚𝐻 and 𝑚𝑊 configurations. In this design, the discriminants
to tag 𝐻 → 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 (∗) can adopt three definitions: one can choose the score corresponding
to the 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗ class, or two BSM classes 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 and 𝐻 → 𝑊 ∗ 𝑊 (∗) (according to
Eq. (11.1)). This results in three different discriminants for tagging 𝐻 → 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 (∗) jets, and
their performances may differ when tagging SM 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗ jets or BSM 𝐻 → 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 (∗)
signatures with different 𝑚𝐻 .
    To demonstrate that this design is effective, the Higgs boson mass is configured as 50, 100,
and 200 GeV such that the 𝐻 → 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 (∗) jets appear to be offshell–offshell, onshell–offshell,
and onshell–onshell. Figure 11.20 compares the performance of three discriminant definitions
in the three 𝑚𝐻 configurations. The model used to deliver this comparison is still based on
ParticleNet as of early development, denoted as “kin. study baseline” in the figure. It can be
concluded that the 𝐻 → 𝑊 ∗ 𝑊 (∗) discriminant performs better for 𝑚𝐻 = 50, 100 GeV, while
the 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗ discriminant performs better for 𝑚𝐻 = 200 GeV. The conclusion is that, while
it is confident that the SM 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗ discriminant is optimized only for SM 𝑚𝐻 and 𝑚𝑊
configurations and hence should perform the best for that case, it is no longer optimal for other
kinematics configurations, and these dedicated BSM classes bring additional improvements.


11.4.3 Transformer scales

    A comparison of the performance of models in three Transformer sizes, namely, ParT-
lite, ParT-B, and ParT-L, explained in Section 11.2.5, is shown in Fig. 11.21. The comparison
spans from different types of jet tagging to the mass regression performance.
    The jet tagging performance indicates that overall performance improvements can be
reached when the Transformer scales up. The improvement from ParT-B to ParT-L is less
substantial, which is potentially due to the limited data size when training ParT-L, as can be
seen from the loss curve for validation (see explanation in Section 11.2.5). Besides, by study-
ing various tagging tasks, it is found that the progress is more significant in specific tagging
modes, such as 𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 versus 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏, or 𝐻 → 𝑊 𝑊 ∗ versus 𝑡 → 𝑏𝑊 jet tasks (lower
plots in Fig. 11.21) compared with discriminating jets versus the QCD jets. The findings im-
ply that DNN’s capability to discriminate against QCD jets has become saturated. Tasks that
potentially rely more, for instance, on track displacement features, still have room for further
improvements.

                                      Chapter 12      GloParT: Model Fine-tuning




Chapter 12
GloParT: Model Fine-tuning
      This chapter will focus on the important implication of building a large, comprehensive
GloParT model: its subsequent fine-tuning to downstream tasks. The design of GloParT as
a “large model for large-scale classification” ensures it learns a profound jet representation
covering a wide range of phase space; thus, one would expect it to generalize to downstream
tasks, the ones that dedicated analysis may explore. It is worth noting that this study only
considers transfer learning, a specific type of fine-tuning that freezes all model layers before the
targeted latent space. This method is most suitable for LHC experiment applications because
the large model inference will be delivered in central computing, and the targeted latent space
features will be stored in the analysis object data, which is then passed on to downstream
analyses.
      As a proof-of-concept illustration of its fine-tuning applications, this chapter specifically
focuses on two schemes.
      In the first scenario, introduced in Section 12.1, the model is fine-tuned to tag a new cat-
egory of jet that is not included in the training sample. This is most likely met in analyses
because the signature of the signal may not necessarily be included in the model’s direct clas-
sification nodes. This is analogous to the deep learning vision task, where large vision models
pre-trained on ImageNet can be fine-tuned to other image domains. In the study, in order to
benchmark the fine-tuning ability, the tagging performance is compared with the maximum
ability in this task, given the model’s original capacity.
      In the second scenario, introduced in Section 12.2, the model is fine-tuned to derive
other jet models within the CMS experiment. A study is performed to fine-tune this mass-
decorrelated GloParT model to perform top jet tagging tasks without imposing requirements
to decorrelate with mass. During fine-tuning, the jet-level variables 𝑚SD and 𝑝T are passed to
                 1
the network          . The study will benchmark the performance with the ParticleNet top tagger as
an official tool in CMS, and perform a theoretical calculation on this problem to explain the
1   Author’s note (edited November, 2024): This model fine-tuning scenario has been integrated into the CMS software
    alongside the “GloParT stage-3” model to infer mass-correlated top tagging discriminant. The base GloParT stage-3
    model is first run to extract and store the hidden-layer neuron values. The 𝑚SD and 𝑝T variables are subsequently fed into
    the fine-tuned MLP layers, where they are processed with the stored neuron values to produce the final discriminant scores.

                                                             211
                               Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


fine-tuning network designs.


12.1 Application on novel tagging
12.1.1 New jet sample and labels
    The potential of tagging a leptoquark (LQ) with LQ → 𝑏𝜏 decay is studied, where three tau
leptons decay channels, 𝜏𝑒 , 𝜏𝜇 , and 𝜏h , are considered. The jet is produced in a di-leptoquark
event production process. The configuration of event 𝐻T and the leptoquark mass is the same as
nonresonance di-𝐻 production used to generate a part of the two-prong jet (see Section 11.1.1).
This provides a similar control on jet 𝑝T and 𝑚SD .
    To assign a truth label to the generated jet, the same matching criteria are adopted for the
𝑏 quark and tau lepton. This produces three new jet labels for three 𝜏 decay modes.

12.1.2 Fine-tuning details
    The third-to-last layer of the original GloParT model, with a dimension of 256, is used as
a latent space feature. The transfer learning of GloParT from these features is then equivalent
to treating them as 128 jet features and training a simple MLP. This is exactly the fine-tuning
picture to foresee for future analysis applications. The training of MLP is delivered in a mul-
ticlass classification task. Besides the three novel classes for LQ → 𝑏𝜏, the remaining ones are
13 two-prong categories, 17 𝑡 → 𝑏𝑊 classes, and the 5 QCD categories, resulting in 38 classes
in total. These categories are brought in such that discriminants can be defined to separate the
LQ signature against the two-prong classes (such as 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏), the 𝑡 → 𝑏𝑊 classes (such as
𝑡 → 𝑏𝜏𝜈), and QCD classes. The MLP comprises two layers, with dimensions of 1024 and 38.
Figure 12.1 gives an intuitive illustration of the network setup for this fine-tuning task.
    The training of the MLP adopts the same input preprocessing procedure as in the original
GloParT training. A sampling-based reweighting on the jet (𝑝T , 𝑚SD ) bins is performed on the
fly of training. The training procedure is very lightweight. Each epoch is defined by training
on 256 k jets, substantially smaller than 7.68 M jets used in the original training. The train-
ing process consists of 15 epochs. The 1CYCLE LR policy[195] for fine-tuning, where the LR
changes every batch instead of every epoch. It is raised from an initial LR to a maximum LR,
designed as 2 × 10−3 , then dropped to some minimum LR much lower than the initial LR. The
same LOOKAHEAD optimizer with the inner optimizer RADAM is adopted. Figure 12.2 shows
several metrics for training and validation, together with the LR. It is clear that the training
converges very quickly.

novel jet categories.


12.1.3 Fine-tuning peformance
      Figure 12.3 shows the performance for identifying the LQ → 𝑏𝜏h signal against the QCD,
𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏, 𝐻 → 𝜏h 𝜏h , or 𝑡 → 𝑏𝑊 → 𝜏h 𝜈 backgrounds, comparing with a training-from-scratch
GloParT model with the three classes included initially. The latter signifies the best perfor-
mance the present model’s capacity can afford.
      The results imply that the GloParT model illustrates good transfer learning ability, and it
has achieved superior performance to veto against QCD, 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏, 𝐻 → 𝜏h 𝜏h backgrounds. Sep-
arating the signal and 𝑡 → 𝑏𝜏h 𝜈 is the most challenging. The background rejection is around
∼ 2 less than the optimal tagging performance. Overall, the result is satisfying because the
fine-tuning step is very lightweight compared to training everything from scratch, and the per-
formance is competitive to a certain extent.



12.2 Application on deriving new models
      As a comprehensively trained model, it is straightforward to consider it as a base jet model
in data analysis such that other jet models can be fine-tuned on top of it. Therefore, the second
application targets a derivation of the new model from the pre-trained GloParT. The CMS
experiment has developed a ParticleNet model to identify large-𝑅 jet initiated from 𝐻, 𝑊 ,
𝑍, or top quark without decorrelating with the jet mass. This study aims to derive the same
model, specifically reproducing the separating power between top and QCD jets, using the
                                                    213
                             Peking University Doctoral Dissertation

evaluation (right) procedure.

Figure 12.3 The performance on tagging a new class of jet, LQ → 𝑏𝜏h , versus the QCD,
𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏, 𝐻 → 𝜏h 𝜏h , or 𝑡 → 𝑏𝑊 → 𝜏h 𝜈 jets as backgrounds. The performance is compared with a

                                                                                                   215
                               Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


original GloParT but incorporating auxiliary features jet 𝑚SD and 𝑝T .
    Essentially, this fine-tuning experiment attempts to recover the model’s ability to discrim-
inant top and QCD jets optimally in the dependence of 𝑚SD . It can be shown from the statistical
theory, however, that the optimal classifier in both the mass-decorrelated and mass-correlated
cases can be converted to each other by taking 𝑚SD as a dependent variable. In this sense, it
is reasonable to envision that by passing in the 𝑚SD variable and allowing it to entangle with
the existing GloParT jet representation within the fine-tuning network, the network will learn
such dependency of 𝑚SD and hence recover the ability to do optimal mass-correlated tagging.


12.2.1 Experiment setup

Jet classes, input and output

    The entire 𝑡 → 𝑏𝑊 and QCD categories of the GloParT model are used to derive the new
model. That means, for the last fully-connected layer in GloParT, only connections to these
corresponding output nodes are preserved while the others are removed. This results in 22
output nodes.
    To maintain consistency with the training of the original ParticleNet tagger in CMS, i.e. a
similar training dataset is adopted. They include top jets produced from 𝑍 ′ → 𝑡𝑡 process with
varying 𝑍 ′ mass but the top quark mass fixed to the SM value (the same with the test dataset,
in Section 11.1.3), and the original QCD sample for GloParT training. Jets are assigned top
and QCD labels using the same matching criteria as in the GloParT training.
    The fine-tuning input involves two parts: (1) the latent features with dimension 256 from
the original GloParT model, and (2) the jet 𝑚SD and 𝑝T in addition.


Network design

    The network design is illustrated in Fig. 12.4. The original two-layer MLP that converts
the latent features to the final QCD and 𝑡 → 𝑏𝑊 logits are preserved; in addition, an auxiliary
three-layer MLP network is introduced, which encodes the features of the latent features to-
gether with the jet 𝑝T and 𝑚SD variables. The layer dimensions of the auxiliary network are
(258, 256, 256, 22).
    The output nodes of the auxiliary path are added back to the original output logits, then
proceed with the softmax. The auxiliary network can be considered to provide “residues” to
correct the original logits. The design is reasonable from a theoretical standpoint, as will be
explained in Section 12.2.2.
