Figure 12.4 Illustration on the fine-tuning experiment of GloParT to derive the mass-
correlated top-jet tagging model.


Preprocessing and training The preprocessing of input data adopts the development of the
ParticleNet mass-correlated tagger in CMS. In this case, the sampling-based reweighting is
performed on the 1D jet 𝑝T template but not on the 2D (𝑝T , 𝑚SD ) template so that the mass
decorrelation requirement is discarded.
     The detailed training setup is similar to the first experiment. The training is performed on
15 epochs, each undergoing a sample size of 256 k. The LR is adjusted based on the 1CYCLE
policy. The LOOKAHEAD optimizer with the inner optimizer RADAM is employed.

12.2.2 A guide in theory
     It is worth noting that one can find a statistical foundation in this fine-tuning network
design. A theoretical relation between the optimal mass-correlated and the mass-decorrelated
classifier model can be found, and they differ only on a dependency of 𝑚SD .
     Denote an input jet as 𝑥 and a jet class with class index 𝑐 represented as a probability
density function 𝜌𝑐 (𝑥). Given that the mass-decorrelated training is achieved via sampling-
                                                            217
                                   Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


based reweighting on 𝑚SD , it changes the prior PDF to 𝜌′𝑐 (𝑥) which is dependent on these
variables,
                                   𝜌′𝑐 (𝑥) = 𝜌𝑐 (𝑥) ⋅ 𝜆𝑐 (𝑚SD (𝑥), 𝑝T (𝑥)).                       (12.1)

The 𝜆𝑐 (⋅) is thus the reweighting factor for the class 𝑐, which is a function of 𝑚SD .
     For a normal training without sampling-based reweighting on 𝑚SD , denote the output prob-
abilities of an optimal network as 𝑝𝑐 (𝑐 = 1, ⋯ , 𝐶), then for any class 𝐴 and 𝐵, one has
                                         𝑝𝐴 (𝑥)          𝜌𝐴 (𝑥)
                                                   =                .                             (12.2)
                                    𝑝𝐴 (𝑥) + 𝑝𝐵 (𝑥) 𝜌𝐴 (𝑥) + 𝜌𝐵 (𝑥)
As an analogue, in the mass-decorrelated network training, the output probabilities 𝑝′𝑐 should
satisfy
             𝑝′𝐴 (𝑥)                             𝜌𝐴 (𝑥) ⋅ 𝜆𝐴 (𝑚SD (𝑥), 𝑝T (𝑥))
                          =                                                                   .   (12.3)
      𝑝′𝐴 (𝑥) + 𝑝′𝐵 (𝑥)       𝜌𝐴 (𝑥) ⋅ 𝜆𝐴 (𝑚SD (𝑥), 𝑝T (𝑥)) + 𝜌𝐵 (𝑥) ⋅ 𝜆𝐵 (𝑚SD (𝑥), 𝑝T (𝑥))
     Looking back at the network design in Fig. 12.4, denoting the original network’s output
                                                                      ∑
logits (before softmax) as 𝑓𝑖 , the probabilities should be 𝑝𝑖 = 𝑒𝑓𝑖 / 𝑗 𝑒𝑓𝑗 . Hence, the binary
discriminant we defined on class 𝐴 vs 𝐵 should be
                                            𝑝𝐴        𝑒𝑓𝐴
                                                  = 𝑓        .                                    (12.4)
                                          𝑝𝐴 + 𝑝𝐵  𝑒 𝐴 + 𝑒𝑓𝐵
When a residual path is added, an additive bias 𝛼𝑖 is provided to each neuron, and the modified
probabilities are modified as
                                        𝑝′𝐴                  𝑒𝑓𝐴 ⋅ 𝑒𝛼𝐴
                                                 =                           .                    (12.5)
                                     𝑝′𝐴 + 𝑝′𝐵       𝑒𝑓𝐴   ⋅ 𝑒𝛼𝐴 + 𝑒𝑓𝐵 ⋅ 𝑒𝛼𝐵
This exactly corresponds to Eq. (12.3) as long as we allow the bias 𝛼𝑖 for each class 𝑖 to encode
the 𝑚SD variable and estimate the reweighting factor log (𝜆𝑖 (𝑚SD (𝑥), 𝑝T (𝑥))). It just implies
that the auxiliary network can be an estimate of log(𝜆𝑖 (⋅)).
     The derivation is about adding an auxiliary path to the normal mass-correlated network
to learn a reweighting function and make it mass-decorrelated. Our fine-tuning application
in this section is an inverse procedure; therefore, it learns a log(1/𝜆𝑖 (⋅)) function that converts
𝜌′𝑐 (𝑥) back to 𝜌𝑐 (𝑥).
     As an experimental validation of this interpretation, the contribution of the auxiliary path,
in terms of exp(𝛼𝑖 ), is visualized in Fig. 12.5. In this validation, a special auxiliary network is
employed that only takes the jet 𝑝T and 𝑚SD as input. After fine-tuning, the learned auxiliary
network is studied by feeding the (𝑝T , 𝑚SD ) pairs, with 𝑝T fixed at 500 GeV and 𝑚SD vary-
ing from 30–260 GeV. This allows obtaining the values of exp(𝛼𝑖 ) as a function of 𝑚SD . In
                                                       218
                             Chapter 12   GloParT: Model Fine-tuning


Fig. 12.5, we present a plot of exp(𝛼𝑖 + Δ), where we have shifted all 𝛼𝑖 values by a constant
Δ to ensure the minimum value is zero. The result aligns with the understanding of the role of
𝑚SD in assisting the mass-correlated tagging. For instance, for the class 𝑖 being the three-prong
bcq or bqq case, exp(𝛼𝑖 + Δ) has observed a large value around the top mass. An explanation
of the performance of exp(𝛼𝑖 ) for different top tagging classes is annotated in the figure.

                                                      Interpret what the auxiliary network learns
                                                                       for jets with bqq (3-prong) structure,
                                                                       mSD in [160, 180] are assigned more
                                                                       probability to be identified as top
                                                                       for jets with 2-prong structure, mSD in
                                                                       the wide range of [60, 150] are
                                                                       assigned more probability to be a top
                                                                       jet
                                                                       mass doesn’t help to identify leptonic

                                                                       come from QCD




Figure 12.5 A visualization of exp(𝛼𝑖 +Δ), where 𝛼𝑖 is the output value of the auxiliary network
for class 𝑖, and it is shifted by a constant Δ to ensure the minimum value is zero. An annotation
about how to explain the performance of different top tagging classes is shown in the plot.

     As a final remark, although it is adequate to only encode 𝑚SD in the auxiliary network,
in reality, better performance is achieved if the entire latent features are all encoded into the
auxiliary network, and this eventually leads to a network design in Fig. 12.4.

12.2.3 Fine-tuning performance
     Figure 12.6 shows the performance of tagging top jets while vetoing against QCD jets in
ROC curves. The fine-tuned GloParT model and the original GloParT model are both shown
in the plot, illustrating a big leap in performance when the additional mass information is used
to distinguish top and QCD jets. Also, their performance is compared with the ParticleNet top
tagger developed in CMS to benchmark the performance achieved so far. It is promising to
see that the fine-tuned GloParT outperforms the ParticleNet by a factor of ∼ 2 in background
rejection.
     This marks a major success in this fine-tuning application, as one may expect to infer a
large jet model and then use it to derive other necessary jet models within an experimental

                                                                                                          220
                                 Chapter 13   The Sophon model




Chapter 13

The Sophon model

    The GloParT model has demonstrated its efficacy as a universal jet model within the CMS
experiment. It surpasses previous taggers in existing tagging tasks and also serves as a robust
base model for fine-tuning across various downstream analysis scenarios, delivering superior
performance.
    To extend the design concept of GloParT beyond the CMS experiment, we replicate its
capabilities in experimental scenarios external to CMS. This chapter presents the reproduction
results of the GloParT model, trained on a DELPHES-based dataset while adhering closely to its
original development framework. This effort has led to the creation of the novel JETCLASS-II
dataset, characterized by jet kinematics spanning a wide range of 𝑝T and mass values, including
diverse jet phase space configurations. Additionally, we introduce the Sophon model, pre-
trained as a multiclass classifier on the JETCLASS-II dataset.
    The pre-training methodology employed is referred to as signature-oriented, where the
“signature” corresponds to generator-level jet properties that characterize the unique origin
information. This approach proposes a training scheme that enables the model to associate
jet input data with their generator-level properties. The simplest form of such a signature is
the truth label of a jet under this definition. Consequently, training a multiclass classifier to
identify various jet labels becomes the foundational approach for comprehensive pre-training.
    Notably, the signature-oriented pre-training paradigm was developed under the concept of
Signature-Oriented Pre-training for Heavy-resonance Observation (Sophon)[196] , which was
primarily aimed at accelerating resonance search programmes at the LHC. The pre-trained
model is referred to as the Sophon model within this context.
    The remainder of this chapter is organized as follows. Section 13.1 provides a detailed
overview of the JETCLASS-II dataset. The model pre-training methodology is described in
Section 13.2, and its performance benchmarks are presented in Section 13.3. Section 13.4 dis-
cusses the implications of the Sophon model for LHC resonance searches. Finally, a discussion
and outlook are provided in Section 13.5.
                                              221
                                    Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


13.1 The JetClass-II dataset
     The JETCLASS-II dataset comprises 188 jet classes. Compared to JETCLASS (described in
Section 2.3), it features resonant jets with a much more extensive set of final states and broader
𝑝T and mass ranges. In simple terms, a resonant jet may contain two, three, or four prongs, with
each prong originating from a quark, gluon, or lepton. These jets are finely categorized based
on particle types, quark or lepton flavours, their combinations, as well as the decay modes of
tau leptons.
     The generator-level simulation of these processes is described in detail below.

13.1.1 Simulation details
     The JETCLASS-II dataset includes both resonant jets and QCD jets. Its categorization
strategy is generally similar to that of the GloParT training set (illustrated in Fig. 11.1) but
incorporates simplifications in the generation of three- and four-prong final states. Specifi-
cally, multiple 𝐻 → 𝑊 (∗) 𝑊 (∗) and 𝐻 → 𝑍 (∗) 𝑍 (∗) scenarios are merged, focusing solely on
𝑋 → 𝑌 𝑌 → 4-prong final states, where both 𝑋 and 𝑌 are spin-0 resonances with variable
masses.
     In summary, the resonant jets originate from the following processes:
   (1) 𝑋 →2 prong signatures with neutral resonance 𝑋,
   (2) 𝑋 →2 prong signatures with charged resonance 𝑋,
   (3) 𝑋 → 𝑌 (∗) 𝑌 (∗) →4 prong signatures.

     The case (1) is generated using the 𝑝𝑝 → 𝐻𝐻 process, simulated with MG 2.9.18[25]
generator at LO accuracy, employing the HEFT model. A total of 16 M events are produced.
To control the resonant jet’s 𝑝T and mass, the minimum 𝑝T of 𝐻 at the hard-scattering level is
sampled at 50 logarithmically spaced points within the range (100 , 2500) GeV. Additionally,
the 𝐻 mass is uniformly sampled from (15, 500) GeV in steps of 5 GeV. The decay of the
𝐻 resonance and subsequent parton showering are simulated using PYTHIA 8.3[28] . The decay
modes and corresponding branching ratios are as follows: 𝑏𝑏 ( 18 ), 𝑐𝑐 ( 18 ), 𝑠𝑠 ( 18 ), 𝑑𝑑 ( 16
                                                                                                1
                                                                                                  ),
     1
𝑢𝑢 ( 16 ), 𝑔𝑔 ( 18 ), 𝑒𝑒 ( 16
                           1           1
                              ), 𝜇𝜇 ( 16 ), and 𝜏𝜏 ( 14 ). Subsequent 𝜏 decay follows the SM configuration.
     The case (2) is generated using the 𝑝𝑝 → 𝐻 +𝐻 − process, simulated with MG at LO accu-
racy with the 2HDM model. A total of 12 M events are produced. The configuration for the
minimum 𝑝T of 𝐻 ± and its mass is identical to that used in the case (1). The decay of the 𝐻 ±
resonance and subsequent parton showering are handled by PYTHIA 8.3. The decay modes and
corresponding branching ratios are as follows: 𝑑𝑢 ( 16 ), 𝑠𝑢 ( 61 ), 𝑏𝑢 ( 16 ), 𝑐𝑑 ( 61 ), 𝑐𝑠 ( 16 ), and 𝑏𝑐 ( 61 ).
                                                       222
                                     Chapter 13   The Sophon model


        The case (3) is generated using the 𝑝𝑝 → ℎℎ process, simulated with MG at LO accuracy
with the 2HDM model. A total of 120 M events are produced. The configuration for the
minimum 𝑝T of ℎ and its mass is identical to that used in previous cases. The decay processes
are simulated using PYTHIA 8.3. In this case, the resonance ℎ decays into 𝐻𝐻 and 𝐻 +𝐻 − , each
with a branching ratio of 12 . Here, ℎ, 𝐻, and 𝐻 ± represent the two 𝐶𝑃 -even Higgs bosons and
the charged Higgs boson, respectively, as defined in the 2HDM model. The masses of 𝐻 and
𝐻 ± are parameterized as 𝜆𝑚ℎ , where 𝜆 is sampled uniformly within the range (0.2, 0.8). The
decay of 𝐻 follows the same configuration as in the case (1), while the decay of 𝐻 ± is similar
to that in the case (2), with the addition of decay channels involving neutrino final states. The
                                                           5          5         5           5         5
branching ratios for 𝐻 ± decay modes are as follows: 𝑑𝑢 ( 48 ), 𝑠𝑢 ( 48 ), 𝑏𝑢 ( 48 ), 𝑐𝑑 ( 48 ), 𝑐𝑠 ( 48 ),
     5           1         1
𝑏𝑐 ( 48 ), 𝑒𝜈 ( 16 ), 𝜇𝜈 ( 16 ), and 𝜏𝜈 ( 14 ). Subsequent 𝜏 decays follow the SM configuration.

        QCD jets are initiated by simulating a 2 → 2 QCD multijet process using the PYTHIA 8.3
generator, producing a total of 20 M events. The minimum 𝑝T of the hard-scattering process
is sampled logarithmically within the range (100, 5000) GeV to ensure extensive coverage of
jet 𝑝T and mass.

        The simulated events from all the aforementioned processes are proceeded via DELPHES
 [63]
3       for fast simulation of the detector response and object reconstruction. The DELPHES sim-
ulation card is adapted from the CMS detector configuration, with modifications to the impact
parameter resolution of charged particles to align with the CMS tracker resolution, similar to
the JETCLASS simulation[65] . In addition, pileup effects with an average of 50 pileup interac-
tions per event are included, adapted from the CMS detector configuration with pileup[63] . The
PUPPI algorithm[36] is applied for pileup removal, adapted from the CMS detector configura-
tion in Phase II[63] with additional parameter modifications based on the Phase-I CMS detector
condition and taking the CMS experimental configuration as a reference[35] . The PUPPI algo-
rithm assigns a value between 0 and 1 to each E-flow object, representing the probability that
the object originates from the primary interaction vertex. The four-momentum of each object
is scaled accordingly. The processed E-flow objects are used to cluster large-𝑅 jets using the
anti-𝑘T algorithm[33] with 𝑅0 = 0.8. The modified DELPHES card is a major highlight of the
JETCLASS-II dataset, as it creates detector conditions and a particle reconstruction workflow
closely resembling those of the CMS experiment during the Run 2 data-taking period.

        After DELPHES processing, jets are required to satisfy 200 < 𝑝T < 2500 GeV, 20 < 𝑚SD <
500 GeV, and |𝜂| < 2.5 to be included in the JETCLASS-II dataset.

                               𝑠𝑠𝑞, 𝑠𝑠𝑔, 𝑠𝑠𝑒, 𝑠𝑠𝜇, 𝑞𝑞𝑞𝑞, 𝑞𝑞𝑔𝑔, 𝑞𝑞𝑒𝑒, 𝑞𝑞𝜇𝜇, 𝑞𝑞𝜏h 𝜏𝑒 , 𝑞𝑞𝜏h 𝜏𝜇 , 𝑞𝑞𝜏h 𝜏h ,

                               𝑏𝑐𝑐, 𝑏𝑐𝑠𝑠, 𝑏𝑐𝑠, 𝑏𝑐, 𝑏𝑠𝑠, 𝑏𝑠, 𝑏, 𝑐𝑐𝑠𝑠, 𝑐𝑐𝑠, 𝑐𝑐, 𝑐𝑠𝑠, 𝑐𝑠, 𝑐, 𝑠𝑠, 𝑠, others


13.1.2 Jet labelling

     This subsection introduces the assignment of 188 jet labels to simulated jets from the var-
ious processes. The labels, summarized in Table 13.1, fall into three major categories. Reso-
nance jets produced in the aforementioned cases (1) and (2) are assigned a label corresponding
to the 𝑋 →2-prong scenario or discarded if unmatched. Jets from the case (3) are categorized
under 𝑋 →3-prong or 4-prong labels, while QCD jets are assigned QCD-specific labels.
     The label assignment follows a detailed matching criterion, described below.
     For a resonant jet, the first-generation decay products of the resonance 𝑋 or 𝑌 (∗) 𝑌 (∗) are
counted. These include two particles in cases (1) and (2), and four particles in case (3). The de-
cay products are categorized as follows: 𝑢 and 𝑑 quarks are grouped and represented as 𝑞, while
the tau lepton is further classified into three exclusive subclasses: 𝜏𝑒 , 𝜏𝜇 , and 𝜏h , corresponding
to sequential decays into an electron, muon, or hadrons, respectively. This classification results
in the following truth particle types that may appear in the final state: 𝑏/𝑐/𝑠/𝑞/𝑔/𝑒/𝜇/𝜏𝑒 /𝜏𝜇 /𝜏h ,
excluding neutrinos. To assign a truth label to a jet, the matching rule requires all truth particles
specified in the label to satisfy the following conditions.
    1. For all particle types except 𝜏𝑒 and 𝜏𝜇 , the angular distance between the particle and the
                                                      224
                                  Chapter 13   The Sophon model


       jet must satisfy Δ𝑅(particle, jet) < 𝑅0 .
    2. For 𝜏𝑒 and 𝜏𝜇 , the matching condition applies to their decay daughters (𝑒 or 𝜇), requiring
       Δ𝑅(daughter, jet) < 𝑅0 .

     For a QCD jet, a label is exclusively assigned based on the number of 𝑏, 𝑐, and 𝑠 quarks
identified from the PYTHIA parton list prior to hadronization. These quarks must satisfy 𝑝𝑇 >
10 GeV and be matched to the jet axis within Δ𝑅 < 𝑅0 . For each quark flavour, the count
is categorized into three cases: 0, 1, or ≥ 2. This classification results in a total of 27 QCD
classes.
     The JETCLASS-II dataset contains approximately 139 M labelled jets, in its training and
validation set. This includes 22 M resonant jets with 2 prongs, 99 M resonant jets with 3 or 4
prongs, and 18 M QCD jets.


13.1.3 Variables in JetClass-II

     The jet constituent features serve as input for neural network training. These features are
derived from E-flow objects and include kinematic properties, particle identification variables,
and impact parameter features, closely following the setup of the JETCLASS dataset. The dataset
also provides jet-level kinematic variables, which are used to construct the neural network
inputs. Additional variables include jet 𝑁-subjettiness observables[69] up to 𝑁 = 4, which
represent high-level jet characteristics.
     Moreover, several generator-level variables are provided to capture jet signatures. These
include the jet label, determined by counting the matched truth particles as described earlier,
and a detailed list of matched particles, specifying their types and kinematic properties.



13.2 Pre-training of the Sophon model
     The Sophon model is pre-trained as a multiclass classifier to distinguish 188 jet classes.
Since its development largely follows the training procedure of GloParT, a brief overview is
provided below.
     The Sophon model utilizes the Particle Transformer architecture, as described in Ref.[65] ,
with modifications to the fully-connected MLP, which is extended to two layers with dimen-
sions of (512, 188). The core of the Sophon model comprises 6 particle attention blocks and 2
class attention blocks, featuring an embedding dimension of 128 and 8 attention heads.
     The initial particle features are embedded using a 3-layer MLP with dimensions (128,
                                               225
                               Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


512, 128), while pairwise particle features are embedded with a 4-layer elementwise MLP with
dimensions (64, 64, 64, 8). The GELU activation function is used throughout the architecture.
The Sophon model contains a total of 2.3 M parameters, comparable in size to the original
Particle Transformer (ParT) introduced in Ref.[65] .
     The model processes input derived from all jet constituents, including kinematic features,
particle identification variables, and impact parameter information. The input structure is simi-
lar to the ParT training on the JETCLASS dataset (see Section 4.2). However, the Sophon model
introduces scaled kinematic inputs, where features related to constituent or jet four-momentum
are scaled by a coefficient such that the jet 𝑝T is normalized to 500 GeV. This scaling enhances
the scale-invariance of the model, improving its tagging performance across a wide range of
jet masses.
     The training of the Sophon model incorporates a sampling-based reweighting technique
as a critical component, following the same methodology employed in the GloParT training
process described in Section 11.2.4. The predefined sampling weights are derived from a two-
dimensional histogram spanned by (𝑝T , 𝑚SD ), within the range 200 < 𝑝T < 2500 GeV and
20 < 𝑚SD < 500 GeV. To define the reweighting classes, the original 188 finely classified
categories are partially merged: classes differing only by quark flavour have been combined,
and all 27 QCD jet classes are grouped into a single class. This approach aligns with the
strategy employed in GloParT training, resulting in a total of 30 reweighting classes.
     The model is trained with a batch size of 512 and an initial LR of 5 × 10−4 . The full
JETCLASS-II dataset is partitioned into 80/20% for training/validation. Training is conducted
over 80 epochs, with each epoch processing 10 M samples. The optimizer and LR sched-
uler configurations remain consistent with those used for GloParT training, as detailed in Sec-
tion 11.2.5.



13.3 Performance benchmark
     Similar to GloParT, we demonstrate the capabilities of the Sophon model in two tasks.
The first is a task based on the presence of final states, where we select the 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 tagging
task to evaluate its direct tagging ability. The second task tests its transfer learning capability.
Specifically, we leverage its hidden layers to adapt the model to a new task that was not in-
cluded during pre-training: 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑠 tagging, where 𝑏𝑠 corresponds to either 𝑏𝑠 or 𝑏𝑠. The two
application scenarios of the Sophon model are illustrated in Fig. 13.1.
     Our test dataset consists of dedicated signal and background samples, which will be de-

scribed in Section 13.3.1. The performance results can be found in Sections 13.3.2 and 13.3.3.


13.3.1 Production of the SM and signal processes

    We simulated the complete set of SM processes to emulate actual experimental scenarios,
requiring events to pass a boosted-jet-based trigger. This dataset corresponds to 100 fb−1
of data collected in LHC 𝑝𝑝 collisions at √𝑠 = 13 TeV, replicating the Run-2 data-taking
conditions. Specifically, the trigger requires the scalar 𝑝T sum of all 𝑅 = 0.8 jets to exceed
800 GeV, with at least one jet having a trimmed mass[68] greater than 50 GeV.
    Events passing the trigger include approximately 5 × 107 events from QCD multijet pro-
cesses, 9 × 105 𝑉 (= 𝑊 /𝑍)+jets events, 3 × 105 events from 𝑡𝑡 and single top quark processes,
and additional events from processes such as diboson (𝑉 𝑉 ) and Higgs boson production. The
signal process originates from a hypothetical heavy spin-0 (Higgs-boson-like) resonance 𝑋0
with a mass of 200 GeV, decaying to 𝑏𝑏 or 𝑏𝑠 (namely, 𝑏𝑠 or 𝑏𝑠).
    All events, except for those from QCD processes, are simulated using MG and PYTHIA for
the ME calculation at LO accuracy and hadronization. The QCD process is simulated entirely
with PYTHIA. To meet the high-𝑝T jet requirements, each MG process applies a predefined
(highest feasible) selection on the scalar 𝑝T sum of final-state partons to enhance computational
                                                                                                                 227
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


efficiency.
     For both signal and SM processes, the leading 𝑅 = 0.8 jet satisfying the trigger criteria
is used to evaluate various algorithms. The QCD event yield is significantly higher than that
of other SM processes; therefore, the tagger developed for identifying 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 or 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑠 jets
focuses on effectively rejecting QCD jets while maximizing the purity of signal jets.


13.3.2 Directly tagging capability

     According to the methodology outlined in Section 11.2, the optimal discriminant for 𝑋 →
𝑏𝑏 tagging is designed to reject QCD jets. Specifically, the discriminant is defined as

                                                             𝑝(𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏)
                Sophon 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 vs. QCD disc. =                   ∑               ,          (13.1)
                                                     𝑝(𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏) + 27
                                                                  𝑙=1 𝑝(QCD 𝑙 )

where 𝑝(𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏) represents the 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 output score, and 𝑝(QCD𝑙 ) corresponds to the scores
of the 27 QCD classes. Ideally, this approach is equivalent to training a binary classifier DNN
to distinguish 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 jets from undivided QCD jets and then using the 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 score as the
discriminant.
     The top panel of Fig. 13.2 illustrates the tagging performance in terms of the discovery
significance, 𝑍 [197] , for 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 jets with 𝑚𝑋 = 200 GeV. This performance is shown as a
function of the SM background selection efficiency, based on 40 fb−1 of data and considering
events within the mass window 150 < 𝑚SD < 230 GeV. The search significance is calculated
by
                               𝑍 = √2((𝑠 + 𝑏) log(1 + 𝑠/𝑏) − 𝑠),                           (13.2)

where 𝑠 and 𝑏 represent the post-selection yields of signal and background events, respectively,
within the defined mass window. The curve has an equivalent interpretation to standard ROC
curves, as employed in the GloParT studies, to evaluate the performance of the tagger. The
bottom panel shows the stacked distribution of all SM processes at a background selection
efficiency of 𝜖𝐵 = 10−4 . The distribution exhibits a smoothly falling background, with the
signal appearing as a bump-like structure near the resonance mass value.
     Several tagging models are compared in Fig. 13.2. To illustrate the current tagging perfor-
mance achievable at LHC experiments, we train two dedicated tagging models for both tasks:
one using the state-of-the-art ParT[65] architecture and another using the ParticleNet model[90] .
These architectures are representative of the current tagging capabilities within the CMS ex-
periment[109, 113, 115] . Both models are trained as binary classifiers to distinguish 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 jets

from QCD jets, employing similar training settings. To address the limited number of signal
𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 jets in the JETCLASS-II dataset, which might constrain the models’ performance, we
generate an extended set of 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 jets with the same configuration. This enlargement creates
a dedicated category containing 16 M jets, reaching a comparable size to the QCD jet category.
The results indicate that the Sophon model’s performance in 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 tagging surpasses that of
the dedicated ParT and ParticleNet trainings, demonstrating its superior tagging capabilities.

     In addition, to investigate whether the Sophon model’s superior 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 vs. QCD jet
tagging performance stems from the large-scale classification task, we perform an ablation
study by training a variant of the model on only 42 classes (denoted as Sophon*), including all
two-prong resonant-jet classes and the QCD classes. Consequently, the training dataset size is
reduced to 48 M jets, compared to the 168 M jets in the full JETCLASS-II dataset. The results
indicate that the Sophon model trained on 188 classes with the comprehensive dataset achieves
significantly better tagging performance. This demonstrates the importance of pre-training on
a large and diverse dataset, even when the additional data samples are not directly relevant to
the specific task.
                                                                         229
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


13.3.3 Generalization capability
     The generalization capability of the Sophon model is evaluated on the novel 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑠 tag-
ging task. For 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑠 tagging, transfer learning is applied to the latent features of the Sophon
model, with a latent feature dimension of 𝑁latent = 128. A two-layer MLP with (512, 2) nodes
is used, where the parameters of the first linear layer are initialized from the corresponding
layer in the Sophon model to facilitate learning. The MLP has two output nodes, designed to
classify 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑠 jets and QCD jets.
     The training sample is specifically generated using a similar mass-decorrelation approach
as in the JETCLASS-II dataset. 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑠 jets are produced with variable 𝑚𝑋 while maintaining the
same kinematics as jets in JETCLASS-II, and the same mass-decorrelation technique is applied
during training. Notably, the transfer learning process is significantly simpler and faster than
the original Sophon model training. It requires only a small fraction (1/320) of the total Sophon
training dataset, with a computational cost (in terms of floating-point operations per second)
of just 1/1 000 000 of the original training.
     The top panel of Fig. 13.2 presents the discovery significance of the 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑠 signal jets
with 𝑚𝑋 = 200 GeV as a function of the background efficiency. The performance is compared
across various algorithms. Similar to the 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏 tagging study, the ParticleNet and ParT
classifiers are trained on a dedicated 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑠 vs. QCD tagging task using an expanded 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑠
jet dataset (16 M jets) to represent the best achievable capabilities with current technology at
the LHC. The bottom panel again displays the stacked distribution of all SM processes and the
signal process, following a selection on the 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑠 tagging discriminant at 𝜖𝐵 = 10−4 .
     The results demonstrate that the Sophon model, after transfer learning, surpasses the per-
formance of the dedicated ParticleNet and ParT models. As a validation check, we examine
whether the high performance in 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑠 vs. QCD jet tagging is due to the transfer of knowl-
edge from the latent space rather than leveraging the tagging ability of existing classification
nodes. To this end, we identify the output node for 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑞 jets that most closely resembles
𝑋 → 𝑏𝑠 jets and evaluate its performance using Sophon’s 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑞 vs. QCD jet tagging discrim-
inant. The latter performs significantly worse, confirming that the transfer learning process is
essential for achieving high performance.


13.4 Implications to LHC resonance search
     The outstanding tagging and generalization capabilities enable Sophon to accelerate the
resonance search programmes at the LHC. To illustrate this point, this section discusses three

application scenarios. The first two (discussed in Sections 13.4.1 and 13.4.2) focus on global
resonance searches in single-jet and dijet final states, while the third (Section 13.4.3) ex-
plores anomaly detection through the use of weakly-supervised classifiers to broadly search
for anomalous signals exhibiting a dijet resonance structure.

13.4.1 Generic single-jet resonance search
     The all-inclusive classification nodes of the Sophon model introduce a novel paradigm of
physics search: by constructing custom discriminants from the 188 output scores in specific
combinations, where the numerator and denominator are defined as shown in Fig. 13.1 (b),
the model can be tailored to focus on specific signal channels. This approach enhances the
sensitivity to signals in the targeted channel, pushing the detection capabilities to the forefront.
     For illustration, we examine the case where the final-state particles from the resonance
decay can be clustered into a single large jet. The following discriminant is proposed to reject
QCD jets while enhancing the purity of specific signal processes,
                                                                                     𝑝(𝐴)
                             Sophon 𝐴 vs. QCD disc. =                               ∑27           .                          (13.3)

where the 𝑝(𝐴3 ) term in the discriminant is designed to capture all existing three-prong sig-

    At the tightest working point, corresponding to a selection efficiency of 𝜖 = 10−4 , the

                                                                  232
                                   Chapter 13   The Sophon model


decays, 𝑍 → 𝑏𝑏̄ decays, and 𝑡 → 𝑏𝑊 → 𝑏𝑞𝑞 ′ decays.
     This example demonstrates a universal approach for using the Sophon model to establish
a resonance search programme. Even when the target resonance properties are unknown, it is
feasible to conduct a comprehensive study with minimal effort, exploring almost all final-state
signatures to identify potential peak structures.



13.4.2 Generic dijet resonance search

     When the resonance mass extends to hundreds of GeV or even TeV scales, its final-state
particles are unlikely to be Lorentz-boosted into a single jet. Instead, it becomes feasible to ana-
lyze multiple objects, including combinations of large-𝑅 jets, small-𝑅 jets, leptons, or photons
produced in the event.
     In this example, we consider a BSM process with a triboson final state, initiated by 𝑊 ′ →
𝑊 𝜙 → 𝑊 𝑊 𝑊 , where 𝑚𝑊 ′ = 3 TeV, 𝑚𝜙 = 400 GeV, and all 𝑊 bosons decaying hadron-
ically. The physics model is described in Refs.[198, 199] and was explored in an early model-
agnostic study[200] . The simulation of this process follows the same workflow as the earlier
examples.
     Events are required to pass the same large-𝑅 jet-based trigger and contain at least two
𝑅 = 0.8 jets with 𝑝T > 250 GeV and |𝜂| < 2.5. The 𝑊 ′ resonance is reconstructed from the
two large-𝑅 jets with the highest 𝑝T . The dijet invariant mass 𝑚𝑗𝑗 is used to reconstruct the
𝑊 ′ mass. The SR is defined as 𝑚𝑗𝑗 ∈ (2500, 3100) GeV.
     A discriminant is constructed using the Sophon model’s output scores to select the ex-
pected signatures for both jets. Ideally, one jet is expected to exhibit a two-prong structure,
while the other jet should exhibit a four-prong structure. However, given the large 𝑚𝜙 , it is
possible for the jet to only capture three quarks within its cone. Therefore, the discriminant
selects jets with signatures corresponding to two prongs (initiated by a 𝑊 boson) or three and
four prongs (initiated by 𝑊 𝑊 ). The event-selection discriminant is then optimized as the sum
of the tagging discriminants for the two jets, expressed as
                                      ∑                 𝑝(𝐴, jet)
                      event disc. =                     ∑27                   ,              (13.5)
                                    jet=1,2 𝑝(𝐴, jet) +    𝑙=1 𝑝(QCD 𝑙 , jet)

where 𝑝(𝐴) is defined as

                    𝑝(𝐴) ∶= 0.3 𝑝(𝑊 (2) ) + 0.1 𝑝(𝑊 𝑊 (4) ) + 0.6 𝑝(𝑊 𝑊 (3) ),               (13.6)
                                                 233
                               Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


and

                     𝑝(𝑊 (2) ) ∶= 𝑝(𝑋 → 𝑐𝑠) + 𝑝(𝑋 → 𝑞𝑞),
                  𝑝(𝑊 𝑊 (4) ) ∶= 𝑝(𝑋 → 𝑐𝑐𝑠𝑠) + 𝑝(𝑋 → 𝑞𝑞𝑐𝑠) + 𝑝(𝑋 → 𝑞𝑞𝑞𝑞),
                                   ∑                                                       (13.7)
                  𝑝(𝑊 𝑊 (3) ) ∶=       𝑝(𝑋 → sig).
                                sig∈{𝑐𝑐𝑠,𝑐𝑐𝑞,𝑠𝑠𝑐,
                                𝑠𝑠𝑞,𝑞𝑞𝑐,𝑞𝑞𝑠,𝑞𝑞𝑞}


As observed, the nodes corresponding to three-prong signatures are assigned a larger weight
during optimization. This is partly because three-prong signatures are more likely to appear
than the two- or four-prong cases in the actual signal events.

      To demonstrate the discovery potential, the significance 𝑍, defined in Eq. (13.2), is eval-
uated using events in the SR and presented as a function of the SM background efficiency.
Compared to the single-jet scenarios shown in Figs. 13.2 and 13.3, the significance contin-
ues to increase as the selection becomes tighter (i.e. as the SM background efficiency de-
creases), illustrating even stronger discrimination power at the most extreme selection working
points. This improvement is partly due to applying selection criteria on both large-𝑅 jets using
the Sophon model. At selection points with SM background efficiencies of 𝜖𝐵 = 10−4 and
𝜖𝐵 = 10−5 , the distributions of the signal and SM processes are shown in Fig. 13.5. Notably,
all SM processes are simulated to match the expected event counts for 40 fb−1 of data. At
𝜖𝐵 = 10−5 , it is evident that hardly any background events survive, while the signal events
remain prominent. This leads to a high discovery sensitivity.

      It is intriguing to note that the trend observed in Fig. 13.5 suggests a promising scenario.
Specifically, in LHC searches, where hadronic backgrounds are typically overwhelming, the
power of the Sophon model can be leveraged to suppress QCD backgrounds to nearly zero
while achieving optimal sensitivity. This represents a novel scheme that has been largely un-
seen in previous LHC analyses. Interestingly, such a phenomenon was more common in earlier
collider experiments. For example, in the 1970s-80s, the discovery of the gluon at the PETRA
collider was based on a single signal event, where the statistical principle relied on the ex-
tremely low likelihood of the background producing a “three-jet process”. Therefore, a key
insight from this phenomenological study of the Sophon model at the LHC is that, even data
collected at the LHC have immense complexity and a huge volume, particularly in the region
of the most common fully-hadronic final states, it remains possible to recreate the aforemen-
tioned experimental discovery scenarios, when we exploit advanced background-suppressing
AI tools, such of the Sophon model, or the GloParT developed within the CMS experiment.
                                                    234
                               Chapter 13   The Sophon model




                                                               ϵB = 10−4




                                                               ϵB = 10−5




Figure 13.5 Top: Discovery significance for the 𝑊 ′ → 𝑊 𝜙 → 𝑊 𝑊 𝑊 signal as a function
of the SM background efficiency, utilizing an optimized Sophon tagging discriminant. Middle
and bottom: Stacked distributions of various SM background processes and the signal process,
selected using the discriminant at 𝜖𝐵 = 10−4 and 10−5 , respectively.




13.4.3 Dijet anomaly detection



    As a final experiment, we investigate the potential of employing the Sophon model’s fine-
tuning for anomaly detection applications. The discussion begins with an introduction to the
concept of anomaly detection using weak supervision, followed by an explanation of why com-
bining Sophon’s fine-tuning method with this approach can substantially enhance sensitivity.
An experiment is then followed to support our statement.
                                            235
                                                  Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


Introduction to anomaly detection via weakly supervised approaches

                  Anomaly detection using weak supervision originated from the Classification Without
Labels (CWoLa) method[200-202] . This method assumes the search for resonance with unknown
properties but relies on knowledge of its mass, which can be reconstructed in a dijet final state
within a specified mass window (which is considered the SR). A weak classifier is then trained
to distinguish events within the mass window from those in the sideband (SB) region. This
enables the differentiation of the signal process from the background. Figure 13.6 visualizes
this approach. After applying a tight selection, the presence of new physics can be revealed by
identifying a bump structure over a smoothly falling background, as shown in Fig. 13.7.




Figure 13.6 Illustration of the anomaly detection method employed in the weakly supervised
approach[201] .

                                                                                           No signal               With signal
                       106                                                    100
                                         Signal
                       105               region                             10−2    3σ                                       10%
                       104
   Events / 100 GeV




                                                                            10−4    4σ
                                                                                                                             1%
                       103
                                                                            10−6    5σ
                                                                       p0




                       102
                                                                            10−8
                                                                                    6σ
                       101
                                                                            10−10
                       100
                                                                                    7σ
                                                                            10−12
                      10−1 Sideband                                                                                          0.2%

                        2000      2500    3000     3500    4000                     2500     3000      3500 2500      3000          3500
                                                                                                       mJJ / GeV
                                          mJJ / GeV


Figure 13.7 Results from Ref.[200] showcasing the capability to search for 𝑊 ′ → 𝑊 𝜙 →
𝑊 𝑊 𝑊 within the 𝑚𝑗𝑗 spectrum using the weakly supervised anomaly detection technique.

                  This early approach requires the classifier to be uncorrelated with mass information, rely-
ing solely on features such as jet substructure to identify the signal. Otherwise, it risks distort-
                                                                   236
                                   Chapter 13     The Sophon model


ing the background’s mass distribution. Subsequent methods have introduced various improve-
ments (see Refs.[203-207] for a non-exhaustive list), primarily focusing on better defining the
background sample for weak classification (e.g. the “mixed sample 2” illustrated in Fig. 13.6).
The most idealized approximation assumes that the background sample can be directly taken
from the SR.
     As a result, Ref.[205] proposed the idealized case, where the classifier is trained to dis-
tinguish data in the SR from the SR background. This scenario is referred to as the idealized
anomaly detection (IAD) limit. The IAD limit establishes a baseline for the maximum achiev-
able search sensitivity in the data, independent of the specific anomaly detection method em-
ployed.
     The background of the anomaly detection described above implies that when the signal
injection is minimal, this method becomes insensitive. In such cases, the two classes in the IAD
approach become nearly indistinguishable, resulting in a classifier that consistently outputs 50–
50% probability split. To improve sensitivity, additional prior knowledge is required to better
differentiate between signal and background, so that even a small injection of signal events can
be effectively identified by the model.
     In this sense, the Sophon model is particularly well-suited for this purpose due to its ability
to recognize hundreds of jet topologies with high sensitivity. This capability stems from its pre-
training as a classifier, specifically optimized to distinguish any two types of jets. Conceptually,
the knowledge embedded in the Sophon model can guide the training of weakly-supervised
classifiers. From a technical aspect, this can be achieved through transfer learning—a simpler
type in the fine-tuning paradigm—where the 128 neuron values from Sophon’s hidden layers
are used as inputs for training the classifier.


Experiments

     The experiment is conducted as follows. We adopt the same signal and dijet reconstruction
strategy described in Section 13.4.2. Notably, the signal process and resonance mass values
are identical to those used in the early CWoLa experiment[200] . To perform the IAD classifier
training, the SR is defined as 𝑚𝑗𝑗 ∈ (2500, 3100) GeV, the same as in Section 13.4.2, and an
additional SB region is defined as 𝑚𝑗𝑗 ∈ (2200, 2500) ∪ (3100, 3400) GeV. Two IAD models
are compared: one leveraging Sophon’s transfer learning approach and the other utilizing high-
level jet inputs designed to replicate the results in Ref.[200] .

     • To train the IAD classifier using Sophon’s transfer learning approach, we employ a 3-
                                                  237
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


       layer MLP with (512, 64, 2) nodes and RELU activation functions to construct the clas-
       sifier. The input comprises two latent feature vectors extracted from the Sophon model,
       each with a dimensionality of 128, for the two jets. The processing begins with the two
       input vectors being passed through the first and second layers of the MLP, respectively.
       The resulting outputs are subsequently summed and forwarded through the third layer.
       An ensemble of 100 networks is trained, and the average score corresponding to the
       data node is used as the discriminant.
    • To train the IAD classifier using high-level jet observables as input, we consider, for
       each jet, the jet 𝑚SD , the number of constituents, the 𝑁-subjettiness variables[69] 𝜏1 ,
       𝜏2 , 𝜏3 , and 𝜏4 . Each variable is logarithmized and standardized to fall within the range
       (−2, 2). The input comprises two six-dimensional vectors, one for each jet. A 4-layer
       MLP with (512, 128, 128, 2) nodes and RELU activation functions is utilized. Similar
       to the previous case, the input vectors are independently passed through the first and
       second layers of the MLP. The outputs are then summed and processed through the third
       and fourth layers.

     In both cases, an ensemble of 100 networks is trained, and their averaged score corre-
sponding to the data node is used as the discriminant. This is a common approach to address
the instability in the performance of the weakly-supervised classifier.
     With the trained IAD classifier discriminant, we evaluate the maximum significance im-
provement, defined as max{𝑍|𝜖𝐵 >10−4 }/𝑍|𝜖𝐵 =1 , under a selection imposed on the discrimi-
nant, as a function of the injected signal yield, illustrated in Fig. 13.8. Our results demonstrate
that Sophon’s transfer learning approach, combined with the IAD method, not only enhances
the maximum significance improvement but also increases sensitivity to the signal at low in-
jection levels. Specifically, the initial significance at which the method becomes sensitive to
the presence of a signal is approximately 0.6–1.0 𝜎.
     This result indicates that leveraging the “knowledge” from a pre-trained Sophon model
effectively resolves the challenge of achieving higher distinguishing capability from lower-level
input features without compromising sensitivity to low signal injections[208] . Furthermore, the
curve corresponding to conventional high-level inputs shows that the sensitivity improvement
in our results is approximately consistent with that reported in the original work[200] , where an
initial sensitivity of 2 𝜎 was shown to improve to roughly 7 𝜎.
     Before concluding this section, it is worth including a comparison involving a signal-
specific search method by incorporating an optimized Sophon discriminant dedicated to this
                                                238
                                                           Chapter 13   The Sophon model


signal, which has been investigated in Section 13.4.2. As shown in Fig. 13.8, Sophon’s signal-
targeted discriminant significantly accelerates the achievement of a 5 𝜎 discovery, requiring
approximately 3.5 times fewer signal events than the IAD method based on Sophon’s transfer
learning.

                                                                                     Initial significance Z | B = 1
                                                   0.2         0.5        1.0        2.0              5.0
                                            35
            max{Z | B > 10 4 }/ Z | B = 1




                                                                                Sophon discr.
                                                 Z=

                                                 5

                                            25                                  5 limit

                                                 100     200            500      1000         2000            5000
                                                                                              Signal events (NS )

Figure 13.8 Comparison of the Sophon model’s effectiveness in model-specific searches and
its model-agnostic capabilities, alongside the conventional method, for probing 𝑊 ′ → 𝑊 𝜙 →
𝑊 𝑊 𝑊 within the 𝑚𝑗𝑗 spectrum.


    The advancement shown in this result can be attributed to the inherent characteristics
of signal-specific searches, where the discriminant is explicitly optimized for a certain signal
hypothesis. Thus, its performance no longer depends on the number of signal events presented
in the data. This tailored approach highlights the advantage of designing certain discriminants
(can be hundreds of them when leveraging the 188 outputs of the Sophon model) in rapidly
reaching discovery thresholds under a wide range of well-defined signal conditions.



13.5 Discussion and outlook
    In this section, we discuss the insights gained from the experiments with the Sophon model
and JETCLASS-II, as well as the potential implications of GloParT for future CMS analyses.
    In the Sophon model experiment, we primarily demonstrated the utility of transfer learn-
ing. While its performance may not reach a full-model fine-tuning, its advantage lies in the
                                                                         239
                             Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


opportunity to store hidden-layer neuron representations in the dataset centrally produced in
the experimental data workflow, allowing hundreds of downstream analyses directly using
them as input to train analysis-level DNNs. This process is essentially similar to traditional
DNN training but substitutes the conventional jet input with these hidden-layer “black-box”
variables. Yet, this simple change enables rapid and convenient use of the “knowledge” of the
pre-trained model to improve the performance in downstream tasks.
    In our example, the philosophy of “signature-oriented pre-training” is illustrated with the
most straightforward application, where pre-training is performed in a classifier framework uti-
lizing only jet labels as the signatures. As discussed, future research can extend this approach
to incorporate a broader range of signatures, including the abundant kinematic properties and
PID information of generator-level particles, which can be either the initial resonance or sta-
ble particles that initiate the jets. Such studies, combined with novel training techniques, are
expected to further enhance the expressiveness and generalization capabilities of the method.
It is important to note that these generator-level features have been stored in our JETCLASS-II
dataset. This makes it a valuable resource for testing and improving future training strategies.
    We would also like to emphasize the significance of JETCLASS-II for subsequent training
of foundation models for the broader high-energy physics community. Advancements in large
language models in recent years have undoubtedly propelled various fields to leverage their
abundant datasets to develop foundation models capable of addressing diverse tasks. However,
compared to other fields, where datasets typically cover the scope of all its domain-specific
tasks, the unique nature of high-energy physics lies in its goal of potential discoveries of un-
known particles. Consequently, the relevant phase space might not be adequately highlighted
in the real data collected at the experiment. In contrast to the data or simulated events for SM
processes, a simulated dataset encompassing as much of the BSM phase space as possible will
likely be of greater help for developing multi-purpose base models. This makes JETCLASS-II
particularly significant when compared to its predecessor JETCLASS and other datasets. We
believe that JETCLASS-II will make substantial contributions to advancing high-energy physics
models in the future.
    Finally, the success of the Sophon model implies various promising yet unexplored op-
portunities for employing the GloParT model in the CMS experiment. This impact can be
understood in three key aspects.

    1. For the planned analyses to be delivered in Run 3, greater sensitivity improvements are
       anticipated based on the tagging discriminant of GloParT. Notable examples include
                                              240
                             Chapter 13   The Sophon model


   Higgs boson property measurements in several channels, such as 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏, 𝑐𝑐, 𝑊𝑊 ∗ ,
   and 𝜏𝜏, as well as studies of Higgs boson self-coupling through di-Higgs searches.
   Additionally, the model is expected to enhance precision studies in SM physics that
   require advanced tagging of boosted 𝑊 , 𝑍, and top jets, among others.
2. The direct tagging capabilities of the GloParT model can be extended to a broader range
   of final states, thereby expanding the scope of boosted-topology searches. Fine-tuning
   the model on customized phase space enables generalization to even broader tagging
   tasks. However, a significant challenge lies in calibrating the dedicated signal processes
   using the corresponding discriminants, primarily due to difficulties in finding appropri-
   ate proxy jets in data. Nevertheless, it is certain that innovative calibration approaches
   will play a vital role in advancing GloParT applications in previously unexplored sig-
   natures.
3. GloParT establishes new paradigms in utilizing boosted jets as a tool to discover new
   physics. Firstly, the rich final states provided by GloParT offer a straightforward and
   highly effective approach to searching for new physics. This involves constructing hun-
   dreds of discriminants and applying stringent selection criteria to search for potential
   bumps in the mass spectrum. Invariant masses can be defined from arbitrary combi-
   nations of objects, including large-𝑅 jets, small-𝑅 jets, reconstructed leptons, photons,
   and 𝑝miss
        T . This method has the capability to push the sensitivity of final-state-oriented
   searches to their limits while circumventing the need for signal calibration. Secondly,
   based on the Sophon anomaly detection experiment, the extensive “knowledge” en-
   coded during the pre-training of the model is anticipated to universally enhance the
   sensitivity of anomaly detection. That is to say, beyond the weakly supervised ap-
   proach discussed in this dissertation, this method also provides a new vision for the
   autoencoder-based approach (e.g. as described in Ref.[209] , with a very recent imple-
   mentation by the ATLAS experiment reported in Ref.[210] ). Conducting these searches
   at the LHC under the base model fine-tuning scheme is expected to push various un-
   derexplored new physics scenarios to unprecedented levels of sensitivity.




                                          241
                                         Chapter 14    Outlook




Chapter 14

Outlook

     Collider physics plays a crucial role in humanity’s efforts to explore the smallest building
blocks of nature and unravel the mysteries of the universe. It is reasonable to believe that
even a century from now, particle colliders will continue to be essential tools for investigating
fundamental particles and their interactions. These machines allow us to create and study
extremely high-energy conditions that are otherwise unattainable. However, the technologies
that support collider experiments have evolved significantly over time. Compared to the earliest
collider experiments more than 50 years ago, today’s LHC can quickly convert detector signals
into digital data and transmit them to sites distributed across the globe for permanent storage.
This remarkable capability might have been beyond the imagination of physicists half a century
ago, and it is a direct outcome of the technological progress brought about by the third industrial
revolution—marked by digitalization and information technology.
     We are now in the middle of what many consider the next industrial revolution, driven
by deep learning and AI breakthroughs, fuelled by large datasets, powerful models, and sub-
stantial computing capabilities. The future shape of collider experiments, empowered by this
technological revolution, may be unimaginable to us now, but the present researchers are deter-
mining its trajectory. It is a unique opportunity for the author to work at the frontier of collider
physics and deep learning, collaborating with scientists to apply state-of-the-art deep learning
methods to our experiments. This approach enables physics discoveries that were previously
considered out of reach and opens new directions for searches at the LHC.
     This dissertation focuses on the study of large-𝑅 jets. Within the modular analysis frame-
works used by the current ATLAS and CMS experiments, large-𝑅 jets are among the most com-
plex objects to analyze. Their complexity makes them a powerful test case for advanced deep
learning techniques. Theoretically, large-𝑅 jets are valuable tools for studying highly Lorentz-
boosted event topologies and high-energy particle resonances. In many analyses, identifying
events with signal-like large-𝑅 jets is a critical first step, corresponding to the task of large-𝑅 jet
tagging. This dissertation aims to cover the latest advances in modern deep-learning methods
applied to large-𝑅 jet tagging.
                                                 243
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


    The dissertation discusses four aspects centred around this technology: the latest develop-
ments on DNN algorithms, the new calibration technique critical for reliable physics analyses,
a direct application in measuring the Higgs boson properties related to the charm sector, and
prospects on how jet tagging may revolutionize its form in the near future, particularly with
the “pre-training and fine-tuning” paradigm. All these aspects showcase significant progress
in their respective domains. Given the rapid development of deep learning, there are surely
new possibilities ahead. While each part of the dissertation includes an outlook for each field,
a few selected aspects are highlighted before concluding the dissertation.

    On the algorithm side, although Transformer models have been widely adopted across
various fields, they have a known deficiency with quadratic computing complexity. As future
models handle more complex collider data, the number of tokens will increase, which calls for
the development of models with improved computing efficiency. Additionally, each task has
its own inductive bias, and this paper focuses on the impact of Lorentz symmetry preservation,
an inductive bias that has previously been less systematically considered. Just as permutational
symmetry preservation is receiving increasing attention, Lorentz symmetry preservation may
also become a standard feature in future network designs. Given the high complexity of current
pairwise feature-based methods, finding better model designs that incorporate this symmetry
is a viable research direction. Finally, as network design inputs become increasingly low-level,
e.g. reaching the level of energy deposits or track hits, it is worth considering how to embed
Lorentz symmetry preservation in the lowest-level inputs.

    For calibration, we present a new approach that pushes the proxy-based method forward,
specifically using a BDT approach to select proxies. However, it is worth considering whether
more efficient calibration methods exist. For example, recent methods propose using DNNs
to construct full phase space reweighting or morphing, allowing neural networks to learn the
corresponding scale factors autonomously. In the future, as jet taggers are applied in more
scenarios and proxies become more challenging to identify from data, achieving full phase
space calibration will be a promising direction.

    For physics applications, this dissertation demonstrates that the integration of our global
tagging techniques with the LHC physics programme allows for the exploration of a much
broader boosted phase space. This enables searches for new physics through bump hunting
without requiring calibration, allowing rapid progress in tightening exclusion limits for various
theoretical models. When combined with advanced calibration methods, these techniques can
achieve more precise measurements and impose stronger constraints on boosted final states, as
                                               244
                                     Chapter 14    Outlook


illustrated by the progress in the VHcc search presented in this work.
    While there is often an unspoken sentiment among analysts that physics research at the
LHC has become less engaging, as a similar experiment is often repeated yearly, yielding
improvements under a predictable range, deep learning technologies will bring about one rev-
olution after another, allowing for a large gain previously unimaginable and bringing new
searching capabilities at the LHC. Just as the current scale of the LHC was unimaginable by
scientists 50 years ago, we are expected to witness and shape the changes in data processing
and analysis paradigms. These advancements are likely to redefine what is possible in particle
physics at the LHC.

                                               249
                 Appendix B    Statistical Framework of Classification and Regression




Appendix B

Statistical Framework of Classification and
Regression
     In this appendix, we derive the functions that minimize the multiclass cross-entropy loss
and the regression loss trained with a log-cosh function. The theory provides a statistical
meaning to the pre-trained model output scores when fully optimized.



B.1 Multiclass classification
     Multiclass classifier neural networks trained with cross-entropy loss learn the ratio of prior
PDFs of input classes in the limit of infinite data, and proof is shown here.
     Suppose we find a space Ω to represent input data, and each input class owns a PDF de-
noted by 𝜌𝑐 (𝑥). Note that the representation is abstract because the input features have variable
lengths. We require
                                       ∑
                                                 𝜌𝑐 (𝑥) d𝑥 = 1,                               (B.1)
                                            ∫Ω
                                        𝑐

such that if the entire data has 𝑁 entries (𝑁 is sufficiently large), the number of jets we have
for class 𝑐, mapping to the point 𝑥0 and its vicinity, will be (𝑁 ⋅ 𝜌𝑐 (𝑥0 ) d𝑥). We adopt here an
unconventional approach that the 𝜌𝑐 (𝑥) for each class 𝑐 is not normalized, but the integral for
∫Ω 𝜌𝑐 (𝑥) d𝑥 for class 𝑐 should be proportional to the number of jets in that class.
     From the statistical view, suppose the network outputs the probability scores 𝑦𝑐̂ (𝑥), which
are the softmax of logits. The sum of cross-entropy loss on all data is rewritten as
                                       ∑
                            𝐿cls = 𝑁 ⋅        −𝜌𝑐 (𝑥) log 𝑦𝑐̂ (𝑥) d𝑥;                         (B.2)
                                          ∫ Ω
                                            𝑐

or conventionally, it can be written as 𝐿cls = 𝑁 ⋅ 𝔼𝑥∼𝜌𝑐 (𝑥) [− log 𝑦𝑐̂ (𝑥)]. Notice that 𝑦𝑐̂ (𝑥) is
             ∑
constraint by 𝑐 𝑦𝑐̂ (𝑥) = 1.
     By minimizing 𝐿cls , we minimize
                                                ∑
                                 𝑙cls (𝑥) = −         𝜌𝑐 (𝑥) log 𝑦𝑐̂ (𝑥)                      (B.3)
                                                 𝑐

                                                     251
                                 Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


for every 𝑥, so we omit the label 𝑥. With the method of Lagrange multipliers, we construct
                                         ∑                 ∑
                         ℒ (𝑦𝑐̂ , 𝜆) = −   𝜌𝑐 log 𝑦𝑐̂ − 𝜆(   𝑦𝑐̂ − 1).                 (B.4)
                                                𝑐                     𝑐

Taking the derivative with respect to 𝑦𝑐̂ and 𝜆,
                                  𝜕ℒ        𝜌
                                ⎧       = − 𝑐 − 𝜆 = 0, for every 𝑐,
                                ⎪ 𝜕 𝑦𝑐̂     𝑦𝑐̂
                                ⎨ 𝜕ℒ        ∑                                                    (B.5)
                                ⎪ 𝜕𝜆    = −      𝑦𝑐̂ + 1 = 0.
                                ⎩              𝑐

We obtain
                                                         𝜌𝑐
                                               𝑦𝑐̂ = ∑            .                              (B.6)
                                                         𝑐 ′ 𝜌𝑐 ′

     To see that the extreme point is a minimum, consider a fluctuation on 𝑦𝑐̂ → 𝑦𝑐̂ + 𝛿 𝑦𝑐̂ with
the constraint 𝛿 𝑦𝑐̂ = 0, then calculate the fluctuation of 𝑙cls ,
                                               ∑   𝛿 𝑦𝑐̂    𝛿 𝑦2𝑐̂
                                𝛿𝑙cls   =−    𝜌𝑐 (         − 2 + 𝑜(𝛿 𝑦2̂ ))
                                            𝑐
                                                    𝑦𝑐̂     2𝑦𝑐̂
                                           ∑      2 ∑ 𝛿 𝑦𝑐̂
                                                               2
                                                                                                 (B.7)
                                        =(    𝜌𝑐 ) ⋅               + 𝑜(𝛿 𝑦2̂ )
                                           𝑐             𝑐
                                                            2𝜌𝑐

                                        > 0.

     As a side note, the derivation is more intuitive for binary classification cases (𝑐 = 1, 2).
In this case, to reach the minimum of

                   𝐿2-class
                    cls
                            =        − (𝜌1 (𝑥) log(𝑝(𝑥)) + 𝜌2 (𝑥) log(1 − 𝑝(𝑥))) d𝑥,             (B.8)
                                ∫Ω
by taking its derivative, one gets 𝑝(𝑥) = 𝜌1 (𝑥)/(𝜌1 (𝑥) + 𝜌2 (𝑥)).
     In conclusion, the proof shows that the function that minimizes cross-entropy loss is
learned to estimate the normalized PDF in the latent space. In our words, given a specific
jet, 𝑥0 , the learned score 𝑦𝑐̂ (𝑥0 ) represents the probability that the jet belongs to class 𝑐, which
explains why it is named the probability score.



B.2 Regression
     We then derive the function that minimizes the regression loss function in Eq. (8.3). In
the probability language, a regression target is a mapping from the jet 𝑥 ∈ Ω to 𝜃 ∈ ℝ. Since
the regression target is a GEN-level objective, the same jet 𝑥 can map to different 𝜃 since
the generator-to-reconstruction process is a smearing process. Hence, the regressed target is
                                                      252
                 Appendix B         Statistical Framework of Classification and Regression


sampled from a PDF on 𝑥 and class 𝑐:

                                               𝜃(𝑥0 ) ∼ 𝜇𝑐 (𝑥0 , 𝜃).                          (B.9)

                                                       ̂
     We denote the learned output regression value as 𝜃(𝑥). Therefore, with a sufficiently large
𝑁, the sum of regression loss across all samples is written as
                             ∑               +∞
               𝐿reg = 𝑁 ⋅                                                    ̂ − 𝜃 ) d𝑥d𝜃,
                                                  𝜌𝑐 (𝑥)𝜇𝑐 (𝑥, 𝜃) log cosh (𝜃(𝑥)             (B.10)
                                    ∫Ω∫−∞
                                𝑐
or
                                     ∑
                    𝐿reg = 𝑁 ⋅                                                 ̂ − 𝜃 )].
                                            𝔼𝑥∼𝜌𝑐 (𝑥) 𝔼𝜃∼𝜇𝑐 (𝑥,𝜃) [ log cosh (𝜃(𝑥)           (B.11)
                                        𝑐
                         ∑
By defining 𝑃 (𝑥, 𝜃) ≡       𝑐 𝜌𝑐 (𝑥)𝜇𝑐 (𝑥,      𝜃), we see
                                              +∞
                      𝑙reg (𝑥) = 𝑁 ⋅                                   ̂ − 𝜃 ) d𝜃,
                                                   𝑃 (𝑥, 𝜃) log cosh (𝜃(𝑥)                   (B.12)
                                            ∫−∞

and 𝐿reg = ∫Ω 𝑙reg (𝑥) d𝑥.
     Therefore, the optimization process is to reach the minimum regression loss for every jet
𝑥. This can also be intuitively reached by thoughts: the regression is independent of class 𝑐, so
one can just mix all classes together. By a given jet 𝑥0 , the GEN-level regression target should
be a distribution, and this is 𝑃 (𝑥0 , 𝜃). We are therefore minimizing 𝑙reg (𝑥), which is to find a
place, 𝜃,̂ in the middle of the distribution 𝑃 (𝑥0 , 𝜃) according to the regression loss form. For
the log-cosh loss, the optimized 𝜃 ̂ satisfies
                      d𝑙reg (𝑥)             +∞
                                    =                              ̂ 0 ) − 𝜃 ) d𝜃 = 0.
                                                 𝑃 (𝑥0 , 𝜃) tanh (𝜃(𝑥                        (B.13)
                         d𝜃 ̂           ∫−∞
When training with the regression objective with the log-cosh loss, we are reaching a function
that finds solutions of the above equation for every 𝑥0 .

1   An English edition of the author contributions follows below.

                                                            269
                              Peking University Doctoral Dissertation


    The author’s detailed contributions in Parts II–V of this dissertation are outlined as fol-
lows. Relevant references correspond to the indices provided in the subsequent section, Re-
search Work.

    In Part II, which focuses on advanced deep neural network algorithms, the author initially
participated in the development of the LorentzNet [6] and ParT [5] algorithms through collabo-
rations with Microsoft Research Asia and the CERN research team. These algorithms marked
significant advancements in the field. Building on this work, the author conducted an in-depth
analysis of the exceptional performance exhibited by LorentzNet and ParT, revealing that the
improvements observed in ParT brought by the pairwise features resulted from the enforcement
of Lorentz-symmetry preservation. Motivated by this insight, the author proposed a systematic
approach to examine the impact of Lorentz symmetry on network performance. Experimental
results demonstrated that respecting Lorentz symmetry serves as a critical inductive bias for
jet tagging network [4]. This finding provides a unified framework to explain several recent
results concerning symmetry networks. The discussion of these state-of-the-art algorithms is
structured according to the author’s logical framework, integrating prior works to which the
author played a leading role and contributed.

    In Part III, which focuses on the calibration methods, the author pioneered the applica-
tion of a boosted decision tree to select phase-space regions closely resembling 𝐻 → 𝑏𝑏/𝑐𝑐
jets using 𝑔 → 𝑏𝑏/𝑐𝑐 proxy jets. This approach, named the sfBDT method, overcame previous
limitations that have impeded the calibration of the then state-of-the-art ParticleNet jet tagger
in CMS analyses. Building upon this, the author developed a generalized framework capable
of calibrating all 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏/𝑐𝑐 taggers within the CMS experiment, covering arbitrary working
points and achieving better accuracy compared to existing methods. This technique has since
been adopted widely and implemented in nearly a dozen CMS analyses, including [1]. Dur-
ing the author’s service as the Level-3 convenor of the CMS BTV (𝑏-tagging and vertexing)
Performance group, the author leads the effort to present a comprehensive review of three cal-
ibration approaches for 𝑋 → 𝑏𝑏/𝑐𝑐 tagging algorithms within CMS [2], which included this
novel method.

    In Part IV, which involves using the then state-of-the-art ParticleNet jet tagger for mea-
surements of Higgs-charm Yukawa coupling [1], the author contributed to the CERN-led anal-
ysis. Specifically, the author played a pivotal role in the calibration of the novel ParticleNet
𝐻 → 𝑐𝑐 tagger and made substantial contributions to the optimization of the cutflow and fit
procedures. The author delivered the pre-approval presentation on behalf of the research team
                                                270
                                           Author Contributions


within the collaboration.
    In Part V, which discusses the research on future jet tagger developments, the author pi-
oneered the “large model for large-scale classification” method for jet tagging. Leading the
team, the author developed the first Transformer-based, pre-trained, and globally applicable
jet tagging model for the LHC experiments, GloParT. Following two iterative improvements
within the team, the latest version of this model (the GloParT stage-2 model) is presented in
this dissertation. Due to its general discrimination capability, optimal performance, and novel
opportunities enabled through fine-tuning, GloParT is poised to serve as the new baseline for
jet tagging during the Run-3 era, with significant potential to enhance the sensitivity of future
physics analyses. In addition, to promote open access to GloParT’s developing techniques, the
author led efforts to reproduce this method using DELPHES samples [3], resulting in the Sophon
model, and released the JetClass-II dataset. This work demonstrates the model’s potential to
accelerate searches for new physics resonances at the LHC.
